# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define a MEMS micro sensor. List **any four** different physical stimuli that can be detected by MEMS sensors, and state the corresponding transduction principle for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution: 1 + 1 + 1):**

A MEMS Micro Sensor is a microfabricated device that detects a physical, chemical, or biological stimulus and converts it into a measurable electrical signal.

| **Stimulus** | **Transduction Principle** |
|---|---|
| Pressure | Piezoresistive change of resistance in silicon diaphragm |
| Acceleration | Capacitance change due to proof-mass displacement |
| Temperature | Resistance change (RTD) or Seebeck voltage (thermopile) |
| Gas concentration | Resistance change of metal-oxide layer (e.g., SnO₂) |
| Magnetic field | Hall voltage or magnetoresistance (AMR/GMR) |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Differentiate between **electrostatic** and **piezoelectric** micro actuators in terms of (i) operating principle, (ii) typical force output, and (iii) one disadvantage each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution: 1 + 1 + 1):**

| **Parameter** | **Electrostatic Actuator** | **Piezoelectric Actuator** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Principle | Coulombic attraction between charged plates/fingers | Converse piezoelectric effect — applied $E$-field strains the crystal |
| (ii) Force output | Low to medium (µN to mN) | Medium to high (mN) |
| (iii) Disadvantage | Requires high voltage (50–200 V); pull-in instability | Hysteresis; requires AC drive for large stroke |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Sensors Focus*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a **piezoresistive pressure sensor** with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for the **gauge factor** of a piezoresistor and discuss why silicon exhibits a much higher gauge factor than metals.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction and working of a **capacitive accelerometer**. Derive the sensitivity expression for a parallel-plate capacitive sensor and explain how it depends on diaphragm geometry.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**
A piezoresistive pressure sensor consists of a thin silicon diaphragm (thickness 10–50 µm) formed by anisotropic etching. Four piezoresistors are diffused/ion-implanted on the diaphragm edges and connected in a Wheatstone bridge configuration. When external pressure deflects the diaphragm, two resistors experience tensile strain and two compressive strain, producing a differential output voltage proportional to the applied pressure.

**Derivation of Gauge Factor (3 Marks):**
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$$
$$G_F = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

For metals, $\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \approx 0$, so $G_F \approx 1.5–2.0$.
For silicon, the piezoresistive coefficient $\pi_{44}$ is very large → $G_F \approx 120–200$.

**Comparison Table (1 Mark):**
| Property | Metal (Constantan) | Silicon (p-type) |
|---|---|---|
| $G_F$ | 1.8 | 120–200 |
| $\Delta \rho / \rho$ term | Negligible | Dominant |
| TCR | Low | Higher (must compensate) |

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Construction (2 Marks):**
- Proof mass suspended by four flexure beams anchored to the substrate.
- Comb-finger electrodes form differential capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$.
- Under acceleration $a$, the proof mass displaces → $\Delta C = C_2 - C_1$ proportional to $a$.

**Working (2 Marks):**
- Differential capacitive output is fed to a charge amplifier.
- Closed-loop operation reduces nonlinearity.

**Sensitivity Derivation (2 Marks):**
$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}, \quad \frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = -\frac{\Delta d}{d_0}$$
For a diaphragm of radius $a$, thickness $t$:
$$S = \frac{\Delta C / C_0}{a} = \frac{a^4}{96 D d_0}, \quad D = \frac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

**Geometry Dependence (1 Mark):**
Sensitivity scales as $a^4$ (radius) and $1/t^3$ (thickness). Larger radius or thinner diaphragm → higher sensitivity but lower bandwidth.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Actuator Focus*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **electrostatic comb-drive actuator**. Derive the expression for the electrostatic force produced by an $N$-finger comb drive.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle and working of a **thermal bimorph actuator**. Derive the tip displacement expression for a two-layer cantilever and list two advantages and two disadvantages.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**Construction (2 Marks):**
- Two inter-digitated sets of comb fingers; one set anchored (stator), the other on a movable shuttle (rotor) suspended by folded-beam springs.
- Voltage applied between stator and rotor → lateral electrostatic attraction moves the rotor.

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
- Each finger pair forms a parallel-plate capacitor; lateral overlap area changes as fingers move.
- Force is constant over the travel range → no pull-in instability (unlike gap-closing plates).

**Derivation (3 Marks):**
$$C = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}, \quad F = -\frac{1}{2}V^2 \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$
$$\boxed{F = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}}$$
where $N$ = number of finger pairs, $A$ = overlap area, $d$ = finger gap.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Principle (2 Marks):**
A bimorph is a cantilever composed of two layers with different coefficients of thermal expansion ($\alpha_1 \neq \alpha_2$). Heating causes differential expansion → bending.

**Working (2 Marks):**
- Resistive heating element embedded in the bimorph passes current → Joule heating → $\Delta T$ rise → bending.
- Used in thermal microvalves, optical switches.

**Derivation (2 Marks):**
$$\delta = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{2 t \left(1 + \frac{E_1 t_1}{E_2 t_2}\right)}$$

**Pros and Cons (1 Mark):**
| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Large displacement (10–100 µm) | High power (mW–W) |
| Simple fabrication | Slow response (10 ms–s) |
| High force | Sensitive to ambient temperature |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Sensor vs Actuator:** Students often list the *same device* as both — strictly, a sensor detects and a transducer converts; an actuator converts into motion.
> 2. **Mixing Gauge Factor:** Remember $G_F = 120$ for silicon, not 1.6 — board examiners deduct 1 mark if you confuse metals and semiconductors.
> 3. **Piezoelectric Direction Confusion:** Direct effect = sensor; Converse effect = actuator. Always write this clearly.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention units** in derivations loses 0.5 marks per question.
> 5. **Bimorph displacement formula** must have the $\left(1 + E_1 t_1 / E_2 t_2\right)$ denominator — partial marks lost if you write it without the stiffness correction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **MEMS Sensor** detects a stimulus and converts it into an electrical signal; a **MEMS Actuator** converts electrical input into mechanical motion.
- The three primary sensor classes are **Piezoresistive**, **Capacitive**, and **Piezoelectric**.
- **Piezoresistive sensors** have the highest Gauge Factor in silicon (≈ 120) due to the $\Delta \rho/\rho$ term.
- **Capacitive sensors** dominate low-power applications (MEMS microphones, ADXL accelerometers).
- **Electrostatic actuators** offer fast, CMOS-compatible motion but limited force.
- **Thermal bimorphs** give large displacement but are slow and power-hungry.
- **Shape Memory Alloys** offer 8% strain recovery — ideal for biomedical microgrippers.
- The energy conversion arrow for **Sensors** is *Physical → Electrical*; for **Actuators** it is *Electrical → Mechanical*.
- Key formulas to memorize:
  - $G_F = (1 + 2\nu) + (\Delta \rho / \rho) / \varepsilon$
  - $F_{elec} = \varepsilon_0 A V^2 / (2 d^2)$
  - $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$
  - $\delta_{bimorph} = 3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T / [2 t (1 + E_1 t_1 / E_2 t_2)]$
  - $F_{Lorentz} = B I L$
- Sensitivity of capacitive diaphragm scales as $a^4$ — geometry dominates performance.
- For KTU viva: be ready to explain why MEMS devices are preferred over conventional sensors — answer: **small size, low power, batch fabrication, integration with CMOS, low cost**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Accelerometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT742 - INTRODUCTION TO MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Micro Accelerometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

A **Micro Accelerometer** is a Micro-Electro-Mechanical System (MEMS) sensor that measures the external acceleration acting upon a proof mass suspended by compliant mechanical springs relative to a fixed reference frame, and converts this mechanical displacement into a proportional, readable electrical signal (capacitive, piezoresistive, piezoelectric, or tunneling current).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Hook:** A MEMS accelerometer is fundamentally a **second-order mass-spring-damper (SDOF) system** where the seismic proof mass serves as the inertial element, and the transduction mechanism is integrated monolithically using silicon micromachining.

**Key Engineering Parameters (must memorize):**
- **Sensitivity:** Typically **1–1000 mV/g** (where **g = 9.81 m/s²**)
- **Bandwidth:** **DC to 1 kHz** (typical commercial range)
- **Dynamic Range:** **±1 g to ±100 g**
- **Noise Floor:** **µg/√Hz** level (high-end devices)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bowling Ball on a Trampoline"

Imagine a heavy bowling ball resting on a stretched trampoline suspended inside a sealed box. When you push the box sideways (acceleration), the trampoline and ball, due to **inertia**, appear to "lag behind" and deform relative to the box walls. By measuring *how much* the trampoline stretches (mechanical displacement) using sensors embedded in the walls, you can determine *how hard* you pushed (acceleration).

- The **bowling ball** = the silicon **proof mass**
- The **trampoline** = the **suspension springs** (flexures)
- The **wall sensors** = the **transduction elements** (capacitive fingers, piezoresistors)
- The **push** = the **external acceleration** to be measured

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Insight:** A MEMS accelerometer does NOT measure velocity or position directly — it exploits **Newton's Second Law (F = ma)** to convert inertial force into measurable displacement. The displacement is then transduced into an electrical signal.

### 1.3 Classification by Transduction Mechanism

| Transduction Type | Physical Principle | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacitive** | Change in capacitance between moving fingers | Automotive airbag, consumer electronics (smartphones) |
| **Piezoresistive** | Stress-induced resistance change in doped silicon | High-g shock sensing, industrial |
| **Piezoelectric** | Stress-induced charge generation (ZnO, PZT) | Vibration monitoring, high-frequency |
| **Tunneling** | Quantum tunneling current vs. gap distance | Ultra-sensitive, research-grade |
| **Resonant** | Frequency shift of a vibrating beam | Precision inertial navigation |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Seismic Mass Model

Every MEMS accelerometer is fundamentally a **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF)** second-order system. The governing equation of motion for the proof mass *m*, suspended by spring *k* with damper *b*, under external acceleration *a(t)* applied to the frame is:

**For a frame undergoing acceleration a(t):**

The inertial (pseudo) force acting on the mass relative to the frame is **−m·a(t)**. Combined with spring restoring force *−kx* and damping force *−bẋ*, Newton's Second Law on the relative displacement *x(t)* yields the classic SDOF equation:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + b\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = -m\,a(t)$$

This is the **foundation equation** for every KTU derivation on accelerometers.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Frame acceleration a(t)** is applied to the sensor housing.
2. **Inertial force −m·a(t)** acts on the suspended proof mass.
3. Mass deflects by **x(t)** relative to frame; spring force **−k·x** opposes.
4. Damping force **−b·ẋ** dissipates energy (squeeze-film damping usually).
5. Mass reaches equilibrium where inertial, spring, and damping forces balance.
6. Displacement **x(t)** is sensed by transduction mechanism → electrical output.
7. Electrical signal is **proportional to input acceleration**.

### 2.3 Frequency Response & DC Sensitivity

Taking the **Laplace Transform** of the governing equation (zero initial conditions), with *X(s)* and *A(s)* as the displacement and acceleration in s-domain:

$$ms^{2}X(s) + bsX(s) + kX(s) = -mA(s)$$

Solving for the transfer function **H(s) = X(s)/A(s)**:

$$H(s) = \frac{-m}{ms^{2} + bs + k} = \frac{-1}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$$

where the **natural (resonant) frequency** and **damping ratio** are:

$$\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{b}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{b}{2m\omega_{n}} \quad \text{[dimensionless]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD RESULT — DC Sensitivity:** As frequency *ω → 0* (DC or quasi-static acceleration), substituting *s = jω* and *ω = 0* gives the static displacement per unit acceleration:
>
> $$x_{DC} = \frac{m}{k} \cdot a = \frac{a}{\omega_{n}^{2}}$$
>
> This is why accelerometer designers **minimize k** (soft springs) and **maximize m** (heavy proof mass) to boost sensitivity — but this trades off bandwidth, since ω_n = √(k/m).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Natural Frequency | $\omega_n$ | $\sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | Resonant peak location |
| Damping Ratio | $\zeta$ | $b/(2\sqrt{km})$ | dimensionless | Degree of oscillation decay |
| DC Sensitivity | $S_{DC}$ | $m/k$ | m·s²/m | Static displacement per g |
| Quality Factor | $Q$ | $1/(2\zeta)$ | dimensionless | Sharpness of resonance |
| Bandwidth | $f_{BW}$ | $\approx f_n \cdot \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ | Hz | Useful measurement range |
| Brownian Noise Floor | $a_{n,rms}$ | $\sqrt{4k_B T b / m^{2}}$ | m/s²/√Hz | Minimum detectable a |
| Differential Capacitance | $\Delta C$ | $2C_0 (x/g_0)$ | Farads | Finger displacement signal |
| Piezoresistive Output | $\Delta R/R$ | $\pi_L \sigma$ | dimensionless | Gauge factor effect |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX NOTE:** In the above formulas, **never** write `|x|` using pipes inside a markdown table — it breaks the table. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead.

### 2.5 Capacitive Transduction — The Industry Standard

For a **differential capacitive accelerometer** with proof mass area *A*, nominal gap *g₀*, and permittivity *ε*:

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon A}{g_0 + x}, \quad C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon A}{g_0 - x}$$

The **differential output** (which cancels common-mode noise):

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \frac{2\varepsilon A x}{g_0^{2} - x^{2}}$$

For small displacements (**x ≪ g₀**), linearized approximation:

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2\varepsilon A}{g_0^{2}} \cdot x = 2C_0 \cdot \frac{x}{g_0}$$

This shows that capacitive sensitivity scales as **1/g₀²** — halving the gap *quadruples* the sensitivity.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive airbag systems** (Analog Devices ADXL series, ±35 g to ±250 g)
- **Smartphone screen rotation & step counting** (low-g, ±2 g)
- **Inertial Navigation Systems (INS)** in aircraft and missiles
- **Seismic monitoring** for earthquake detection
- **Wearable health devices** for activity recognition
- **Hard-disk drive protection** (free-fall detection)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Transfer Function Magnitude & Phase Response

**Starting Equation:** From Section 2.3, with *s = jω*:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{-1}{(j\omega)^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}(j\omega) + \omega_{n}^{2}}$$

**Step 1:** Expand the denominator using *j² = −1*:

$$(j\omega)^{2} = -\omega^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{-1}{\omega_{n}^{2} - \omega^{2} + j\,2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega}$$

**Step 3:** Rationalize by multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{-1(\omega_{n}^{2} - \omega^{2} - j\,2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega)}{(\omega_{n}^{2} - \omega^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega)^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Separate real and imaginary parts:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{-(\omega_{n}^{2} - \omega^{2})}{(\omega_{n}^{2} - \omega^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega)^{2}} + j\frac{2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega}{(\omega_{n}^{2} - \omega^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega)^{2}}$$

**Step 5:** Magnitude of transfer function:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(\omega_{n}^{2} - \omega^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega)^{2}}}$$

**Step 6:** Express in normalized form using frequency ratio *r = ω/ω_n*:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\omega_{n}^{2}\sqrt{(1 - r^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta r)^{2}}}$$

**Step 7:** Phase angle (degrees):

$$\phi(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^{2}}\right) - 180^{\circ}$$

**Step 8:** Identify the three operating regions:

- **r ≪ 1** (well below resonance): $\vert H \vert \approx 1/\omega_n^2$ — **flat, ideal accelerometer region**.
- **r ≈ 1** (at resonance): $\vert H \vert = 1/(2\zeta\omega_n^2)$ — **resonance peak (avoid for measurement)**.
- **r ≫ 1** (above resonance): $\vert H \vert \approx 1/\omega^2$ — rolls off at −40 dB/decade.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A MEMS accelerometer has a proof mass *m = 5 × 10⁻⁷ kg*, spring constant *k = 0.5 N/m*, and damping coefficient *b = 2.5 × 10⁻⁶ N·s/m*. The device is subjected to a constant acceleration *a = 5g*. Calculate:
(a) Natural frequency *f_n*
(b) Damping ratio *ζ*
(c) Quality factor *Q*
(d) DC displacement *x_DC*

**Solution (a) — Natural Frequency:**

Step 1: Apply the formula:

$$\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.5}{5 \times 10^{-7}}}$$

Step 2: Compute numerator/denominator ratio:

$$\frac{k}{m} = \frac{0.5}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 1 \times 10^{6} \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

Step 3: Take square root:

$$\omega_{n} = 1000 \text{ rad/s}$$

Step 4: Convert to Hz:

$$f_{n} = \frac{\omega_{n}}{2\pi} = \frac{1000}{2\pi} = 159.15 \text{ Hz}$$

**Solution (b) — Damping Ratio:**

Step 1: Apply the formula:

$$\zeta = \frac{b}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

Step 2: Compute √(km):

$$\sqrt{km} = \sqrt{(0.5)(5 \times 10^{-7})} = \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

Step 3: Substitute:

$$\zeta = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-3}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Result: ζ = 0.0025** (heavily underdamped — common in MEMS vacuum operation).

**Solution (c) — Quality Factor:**

Step 1: Apply the formula:

$$Q = \frac{1}{2\zeta} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.0025} = \frac{1}{0.005} = 200$$

**Result: Q = 200** (sharp resonance — need active damping circuit).

**Solution (d) — DC Displacement:**

Step 1: Apply the formula:

$$x_{DC} = \frac{m \cdot a}{k}$$

Step 2: Compute input acceleration *a* in m/s²:

$$a = 5g = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Step 3: Substitute:

$$x_{DC} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7})(49.05)}{0.5}$$

Step 4: Numerator:

$$5 \times 10^{-7} \times 49.05 = 2.4525 \times 10^{-5}$$

Step 5: Final:

$$x_{DC} = \frac{2.4525 \times 10^{-5}}{0.5} = 4.905 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 49.05 \text{ μm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Validation Check:** Since *x_DC = 49 µm* is typically less than the gap *g₀ ≈ 1–2 µm* in capacitive designs, this device would likely cause **pull-in**. In practice, *k* must be increased (stiffer springs) or *m* decreased. This illustrates the **design trade-off** examiners love to test.

---

### 3.3 Sensitivity Derivation from First Principles (Capacitive)

Starting from the differential capacitance (Section 2.5):

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2\varepsilon A}{g_0^{2}} \cdot x$$

Substituting *x = (m/k) · a*:

$$\Delta C = \frac{2\varepsilon A m}{g_0^{2} k} \cdot a$$

Defining the **capacitive sensitivity** *S_C*:

$$S_{C} = \frac{\Delta C}{a} = \frac{2\varepsilon A m}{g_0^{2} k} \quad \text{[F per m/s}^2\text{]}$$

Converting to F/g:

$$S_{C,g} = \frac{2\varepsilon A m \cdot g}{g_0^{2} k} \quad \text{[F/g]}$$

For *A = 1 mm² = 10⁻⁶ m²*, *g₀ = 2 µm = 2×10⁻⁶ m*, *m = 5×10⁻⁷ kg*, *k = 0.5 N/m*, *ε = 8.854×10⁻¹² F/m*:

$$S_{C,g} = \frac{2 \times (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-7})(9.81)}{(2 \times 10^{-6})^{2}(0.5)}$$

Numerator: *2 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹² × 10⁻⁶ × 5 × 10⁻⁷ × 9.81 = 8.685 × 10⁻²³*

Denominator: *4 × 10⁻¹² × 0.5 = 2 × 10⁻¹²*

$$S_{C,g} = \frac{8.685 \times 10^{-23}}{2 \times 10^{-12}} = 4.34 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/g} = 43.4 \text{ fF/g}$$

This is the **order-of-magnitude** sensitivity of a typical commercial MEMS capacitive accelerometer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — MEMS Accelerometer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Acceleration a t] --> B[Sensor Frame / Anchor]
    B --> C[Inertial Force F = m a]
    C --> D[Proof Mass Movement x t]
    D --> E1[Suspension Spring k]
    D --> E2[Squeeze Film Damper b]
    D --> F[Transduction Stage]
    F --> G1[Capacitive Fingers C1 C2]
    F --> G2[Piezoresistor Bridge]
    F --> G3[Piezoelectric Layer]
    G1 --> H[Signal Conditioning ASIC]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Low Noise Amplifier]
    I --> J[Anti Alias Filter]
    J --> K[ADC Converter]
    K --> L[Digital Output Vout or SPI]
    L --> M[Motion Processor / Host MCU]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Differential Capacitive Sensing Fingers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Moving_Frame
        M1[Moving Finger M1]
        M2[Moving Finger M2]
    end
    subgraph Fixed_Frame
        F1[Fixed Finger F1 above M1]
        F2[Fixed Finger F2 below M2]
    end
    M1 --- F1
    M2 --- F2
    F1 -->|C1| C[(Differential Capacitance)]
    F2 -->|C2| C
    C --> Out[Delta C Output]
```

### 4.3 System-Level Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Force-Balance Accelerometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OpenLoop_Path
        A1[Input Acceleration] --> A2[Proof Mass + Spring]
        A2 --> A3[Capacitive Displacement Sensor]
    end
    A3 --> A4[Charge Amplifier]
    A4 --> A5[PID Controller]
    A5 --> A6[Electrostatic Force Feedback Actuator]
    A6 -->|Restoring Force| A2
    A5 --> A7[Output Voltage Buffer]
    A7 --> A8[Analog or Digital Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Note:** In **closed-loop (force-balance)** designs, the proof mass is held near *x ≈ 0* by an electrostatic feedback force. This dramatically improves **linearity**, **bandwidth**, and **dynamic range** — the principle used in high-grade navigation accelerometers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define a MEMS accelerometer. Mention any two transduction mechanisms used.**

**Model Answer:**
A MEMS accelerometer is a micro-scale inertial sensor that measures acceleration using a suspended proof mass whose displacement relative to a fixed frame is transduced into an electrical signal.

Two transduction mechanisms:
1. **Capacitive** — change in capacitance between interdigitated fingers due to mass displacement.
2. **Piezoresistive** — change in resistance of doped silicon due to stress induced by the proof mass.

*(Any two valid mechanisms with correct description: 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is the significance of the damping ratio in a MEMS accelerometer? State the typical value range.**

**Model Answer:**
The damping ratio *ζ* determines how quickly oscillations decay and the sharpness of the resonance peak. It affects the usable bandwidth and stability of the sensor response.

**Typical range:** *ζ = 0.001 to 0.7* depending on operating pressure (vacuum-packaged devices are heavily underdamped; air-damped devices are near critical *ζ ≈ 1*).

A damping ratio too low (< 0.1) causes ringing near resonance; too high (> 1) limits bandwidth and reduces sensitivity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the governing equation of motion for a MEMS accelerometer treated as a SDOF mass-spring-damper system. Define natural frequency and damping ratio. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Consider a proof mass *m* suspended inside a sensor frame by a spring of stiffness *k*, with a damper of coefficient *b*. When the frame undergoes acceleration *a(t)*, the mass experiences an inertial (pseudo) force **F_inertial = −m·a(t)** relative to the frame.

Applying Newton's Second Law to the relative displacement *x(t)*:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = -m\,a(t)$$

This is the governing SDOF equation.

Taking Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):

$$X(s)[ms^{2} + bs + k] = -m\,A(s)$$

$$H(s) = \frac{X(s)}{A(s)} = \frac{-1}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$$

**Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{b}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{b}{2m\omega_{n}}$$

DC limit (*s = 0*): $x_{DC} = -m a / k$, confirming displacement is proportional to acceleration.

---

**(b)** A capacitive MEMS accelerometer has *m = 1 × 10⁻⁶ kg*, *k = 1 N/m*, *g₀ = 2 µm*, and sensing electrode area *A = 1 mm²*. Compute (i) natural frequency, (ii) DC sensitivity in m/g, and (iii) differential capacitance change for *a = 1 g*. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{10^{-6}}} = 1000 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{1000}{2\pi} \approx 159.15 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) DC Sensitivity:**

$$S = \frac{m}{k} = \frac{10^{-6}}{1} = 10^{-6} \text{ m·s}^{2}/\text{m} = 10^{-6} \text{ m per (m/s}^{2}\text{)}$$

Converting to m/g:

$$S_g = \frac{m}{k} \cdot g = 10^{-6} \times 9.81 = 9.81 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/g} = 9.81 \text{ μm/g}$$

**(iii) Differential Capacitance:**

First, displacement at *a = 1 g*:

$$x = S_g = 9.81 \text{ μm}$$

Note: This *exceeds* g₀ = 2 µm, indicating **pull-in** — design is unrealistic.

Theoretically (ignoring pull-in), the differential capacitance change:

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2\varepsilon A x}{g_0^{2}} = \frac{2 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times 10^{-6}}{(2 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}$$

Numerator: *2 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹² × 10⁻⁶ × 9.81 × 10⁻⁶ = 1.737 × 10⁻²²*

Denominator: *4 × 10⁻¹²*

$$\Delta C = \frac{1.737 \times 10^{-22}}{4 \times 10^{-12}} = 4.34 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F} = 43.4 \text{ fF}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a closed-loop (force-balance) MEMS accelerometer. Compare it with the open-loop configuration. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Open-Loop Accelerometer:**
Input acceleration displaces the proof mass; the displacement is sensed (capacitively or piezoresistively) and amplified directly. The output voltage is proportional to displacement, which is proportional to acceleration. Simple but suffers from nonlinearity, limited bandwidth, and cross-axis sensitivity.

**Closed-Loop (Force-Balance) Accelerometer:**
The displacement is detected and fed back via a controller to apply an **electrostatic restoring force** that keeps the proof mass near its null position (*x ≈ 0*). The feedback voltage required to null the displacement becomes the output.

**Block diagram:**

```
Input a(t) → Proof Mass + Spring → Displacement Sensor (ΔC)
       ↑                                      ↓
       |                              Charge Amplifier
       |                                      ↓
       |                                PID Controller
       |                                      ↓
       ←←←← Electrostatic Actuator ←←←←  High-Voltage Driver
                                              ↓
                                         Output V_out
```

**Key Advantages of Closed-Loop:**
- Excellent linearity (mass barely moves)
- Wider bandwidth (limited by electronics, not resonance)
- Higher dynamic range
- Better shock survival

**Disadvantages:**
- Complex ASIC required
- Higher power consumption
- Stability issues if loop gain not properly designed

---

**(b)** The transfer function of a MEMS accelerometer is given by:

$$H(s) = \frac{1}{s^{2} + 100s + 10^{6}}$$

Determine: (i) the natural frequency, (ii) the damping ratio, (iii) the quality factor, (iv) the DC sensitivity, and (v) classify whether it is overdamped, underdamped, or critically damped. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Comparing with standard form:

$$H(s) = \frac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$$

Wait — the given form has numerator 1, not ω_n². The standard accelerometer transfer function form is:

$$H(s) = \frac{-1}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$$

So matching coefficients:

**(i) Natural Frequency:**

$$\omega_{n}^{2} = 10^{6} \implies \omega_{n} = 1000 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{1000}{2\pi} = 159.15 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Damping Ratio:**

$$2\zeta\omega_{n} = 100$$

$$\zeta = \frac{100}{2 \times 1000} = \frac{100}{2000} = 0.05$$

**(iii) Quality Factor:**

$$Q = \frac{1}{2\zeta} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10$$

**(iv) DC Sensitivity:**

$$S_{DC} = \frac{1}{\omega_{n}^{2}} = \frac{1}{10^{6}} = 10^{-6} \text{ m·s}^{2}/\text{m} = 1 \text{ μm per (m/s}^{2}\text{)}$$

Converting to m/g:

$$S_{DC,g} = \frac{9.81}{10^{6}} = 9.81 \text{ nm/g}$$

**(v) Classification:**

Since **ζ = 0.05 < 1**, the system is **underdamped**. This means there will be some ringing near resonance, but flat response well below *f_n* — acceptable for most accelerometer applications.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert *g* to *m/s²* (multiply by 9.81) or *rad/s* to *Hz* (divide by 2π). Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for unit correctness.
>
> 2. **Sign Confusion in Governing Equation:** Some write *+ma(t)* on the RHS instead of *−ma(t)*. The sign indicates direction of mass motion *relative to the accelerating frame*.
>
> 3. **Confusing Resonant Frequency with Bandwidth:** Bandwidth ≈ *f_n* only for lightly damped systems. For *ζ > 0.7*, the −3 dB bandwidth is significantly *less* than *f_n*.
>
> 4. **Linearization Validity:** Students blindly use $\Delta C \approx 2C_0(x/g_0)$ even when *x* approaches *g₀*. Always state the **small-signal assumption** *x ≪ g₀* explicitly to earn full marks.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Pull-In:** If calculated displacement *x_DC* exceeds 1/3 of *g₀*, electrostatic pull-in occurs and the linear model breaks down. Examiners appreciate this critical observation.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to Draw the Free Body Diagram:** In 7-mark derivation questions, a clearly labeled FBD is worth **1 mark** itself. Never skip it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Governing Equation:** *mẍ + bẋ + kx = −m·a(t)* — memorize this verbatim.
- **Natural Frequency:** *ω_n = √(k/m)* — soft springs & heavy mass lower it.
- **Damping Ratio:** *ζ = b/(2√(km))* — controls resonance peak and step-response ringing.
- **DC Sensitivity:** *S = m/k = 1/ω_n²* — large mass & soft springs increase sensitivity but reduce bandwidth.
- **Capacitive Sensitivity:** Scales as **1/g₀²** — halving the gap quadruples the signal.
- **Open vs Closed Loop:** Open-loop is simple but nonlinear; closed-loop is linear but complex.
- **Trade-off Triangle:** Sensitivity ↔ Bandwidth ↔ Dynamic Range — improving one degrades the others.
- **Common Transduction:** Capacitive (consumer), Piezoresistive (high-g), Piezoelectric (vibration), Tunneling (ultra-sensitive), Resonant (precision).
- **Noise Floor:** Brownian noise *a_n ∝ √(b/m²)* — reduce damping (vacuum) and increase mass.
- **Fabrication:** Surface micromachining (Poly-Si on SiO₂ sacrificial layer) and bulk micromachining (deep reactive ion etching — DRIE) are the two primary manufacturing routes.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** Always **state assumptions** (small displacement, linear damping, SDOF, massless springs). Examiners give **bonus partial credit** for clearly written assumptions even if the final numerical answer is wrong. Draw the FBD first, write the governing equation, then derive — in that exact order.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Grippers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT742 - INTRODUCTION TO MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Micro Grippers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Micro Gripper** is a micro-electro-mechanical system (MEMS) device, typically fabricated through microfabrication techniques such as surface micromachining, bulk micromachining, or polymer molding (e.g., SU-8, PDMS), designed to physically grasp, hold, translate, and release micro-scale objects with characteristic dimensions ranging from **1 µm to 1 mm**. The device converts a non-mechanical input stimulus (electrical voltage, thermal gradient, magnetic field, or pneumatic pressure) into a precise mechanical displacement of its jaw/finger structures, thereby generating a controllable gripping force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** Micro grippers belong to the family of *Micro Actuators with Mechanical Output* and are evaluated under Course Outcome CO2: *"Apply microsystem design principles to select and analyze micro actuators and sensors."* (KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus, PEEVT742)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you are in a laboratory trying to pick up a single grain of sand or a single biological cell using your fingers. Your fingers are far too big and clumsy. A **micro gripper** is essentially a pair of robotic "tweezers" shrunk down to the micro scale.

- Your hand applies muscle force → The micro gripper applies electrostatic, piezoelectric, or thermal force.
- Your fingers rotate at the knuckle → The micro gripper jaws rotate at a compliant **flexure hinge**.
- Your brain decides grip strength → An applied voltage signal decides the actuation force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a micro gripper as the **microsurgical tweezer used by a surgeon operating under a microscope**, but automated, electrically controlled, and small enough to manipulate *living red blood cells* (diameter ≈ 7.5 µm) or *single microchips*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following fundamental constants and metrics govern micro gripper design and must be memorized:

- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$
- **Relative permittivity of air:** $\varepsilon_r \approx 1.0006$
- **Typical operating voltage range:** **5 V to 200 V**
- **Typical gripping force range:** **10 µN to 100 mN**
- **Typical jaw displacement range:** **1 µm to 500 µm**
- **Standard metric for scalability at micro scale:** Surface-to-volume ratio increases as $\propto 1/L$, making surface forces (electrostatic, surface tension) dominate over volume forces (inertia, gravity).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A micro gripper operates through a closed-loop functional chain:

1. **Input Stimulus Reception:** A control signal (voltage, current, heat, pressure, or magnetic field) is delivered to the actuator element.
2. **Energy Conversion:** The actuator transduces the input energy into mechanical strain or displacement (e.g., piezoelectric crystals deform under an electric field).
3. **Mechanical Amplification & Transmission:** The small actuator displacement is amplified through a **lever mechanism**, **compliant flexure**, or **amplifier hinge** and routed to the gripping jaws.
4. **Jaw Actuation:** The two opposing jaws close or open by rotating about their anchor points, generating gripping displacement $\Delta x$.
5. **Force Generation on Micro-Object:** Contact between the jaw tips and the target object generates a normal gripping force $F_g$, constrained by friction ($\mu$) to prevent slippage.

### 2.2 Classification of Micro Grippers by Actuation Principle

| S.No. | Actuation Type | Working Mechanism | Typical Force | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Electrostatic** | Coulombic attraction between charged combs/plates | 10 µN – 1 mN | Fast response, low power, CMOS compatible | High voltage, small displacement |
| 2 | **Piezoelectric** | Inverse piezoelectric effect in PZT, PZN-PT, AlN | 1 mN – 100 mN | High force, high precision | Needs high V, hysteresis |
| 3 | **Thermal / Bimorph** | Differential thermal expansion of two bonded layers | 5 mN – 500 mN | Large displacement, low voltage | Slow response, high power |
| 4 | **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** | Austenite-to-Martensite phase change | 50 mN – 5 N | Very large force, large stroke | Heating required, slow |
| 5 | **Magnetic / Electromagnetic** | Lorentz force on current-carrying conductor | 1 mN – 50 mN | Wireless actuation possible | Bulky external coils |
| 6 | **Pneumatic / Hydraulic** | Pressurized fluid expands elastomeric chamber | 1 mN – 1 N | Simple, biocompatible | Needs external pump |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas constitute the **high-yield mathematical backbone** required for solving any KTU numerical or analytical question on micro grippers.

| S.No. | Parameter | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Electrostatic Force (Parallel Plate)** | $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}$ | $A$: overlap area (m²), $V$: voltage (V), $d$: gap (m) | Capacitive actuators |
| 2 | **Electrostatic Force (Comb Drive)** | $F_e = \dfrac{n \, \varepsilon_0 \, t \, V^2}{g}$ | $n$: finger pairs, $t$: thickness (m), $g$: gap (m) | Comb-drive jaws |
| 3 | **Piezoelectric Free Strain** | $S = d_{33} \cdot E = d_{33} \cdot \dfrac{V}{t_p}$ | $d_{33}$: charge coeff. (C/N), $t_p$: piezo thickness (m) | PZT bimorph |
| 4 | **Bimorph Tip Deflection** | $\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{3}{2} L^2 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot \dfrac{1}{t}$ | $L$: beam length, $\alpha$: thermal exp. coeff., $\Delta T$: temp. rise | Thermal grippers |
| 5 | **SMA Recovery Stress** | $\sigma_r = E_A (\varepsilon_{total} - \varepsilon_T)$ | $E_A$: Austenite modulus, $\varepsilon_T$: transformation strain | Nitinol actuators |
| 6 | **Gripping Force (Friction Limit)** | $F_g \leq \mu \cdot F_{normal}$ | $\mu$: friction coeff. between jaw and object | Object slip criterion |
| 7 | **Displacement Amplification (Lever)** | $\dfrac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} = \dfrac{L_{out}}{L_{in}}$ | Ratio of lever arm lengths | Lever amplifier design |
| 8 | **Compliant Hinge Rotation** | $\theta = \dfrac{M \cdot L}{E \cdot I}$ | $M$: moment, $I$: area moment of inertia | Flexure analysis |
| 9 | **Stiffness of Cantilever Jaw** | $k = \dfrac{E \cdot w \cdot t^3}{4 L^3}$ | $w$: width, $t$: thickness, $E$: Young's modulus | Jaw compliance |
| 10 | **Capillary (Adhesion) Force at Microscale** | $F_c = 4 \pi \gamma R \cos\theta$ | $\gamma$: surface tension, $R$: radius of micro-object | Release force check |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

- **Biomedical Micro-manipulation:** Picking up individual cells (e.g., oocytes for IVF), microparticles, and micro-surgical tools.
- **Micro-assembly of MEMS:** Placing micron-scale gears, mirrors, and cantilever beams onto a substrate to build hybrid microsystems.
- **Microrobotics:** End-effector of swimming/ crawling microrobots used in pipe inspection and minimally invasive surgery.
- **Microfluidic Cell Sorting:** Integrated grippers within lab-on-chip platforms for sorting and characterization.
- **Optical Fiber Alignment:** Precise micron-level positioning of fiber ends inside photonic packages.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Electrostatic Gripping Force for a Parallel-Plate Actuator Jaw

We derive the force generated when a voltage $V$ is applied across a parallel-plate capacitor whose one plate is the moving jaw and the other is a fixed anchor.

**Step 1 — Energy Stored in a Parallel-Plate Capacitor**

The energy stored in the electric field between two parallel plates of area $A$, separation $d$, with applied voltage $V$ is:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2
$$

where the capacitance is $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute the Capacitance Expression**

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} \cdot V^2
$$

**Step 3 — Force is the Negative Gradient of Energy with Respect to Displacement**

The mechanical force tending to *close* the gap (attractive) is:

$$
F = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial d} = -\frac{d}{dd}\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the Differentiation**

Treating $A$, $\varepsilon_0$, $\varepsilon_r$, and $V$ as constants with respect to $d$:

$$
F = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2} \cdot \frac{d}{dd}\left(d^{-1}\right) = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2} \cdot (-d^{-2})
$$

**Step 5 — Final Force Expression (Attractive)**

$$
F_e = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}
$$

**Interpretation:** The force scales with the **square of voltage** — meaning even a small voltage doubling quadruples the gripping force. This quadratic relationship is the *why* behind the steep force response of electrostatic grippers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Displacement Amplification via a Lever Mechanism

Many piezoelectric and thermal actuators produce only 1–5 µm of stroke, which is insufficient to open a gripper jaw by 50 µm. A lever amplifies the stroke.

**Step 1 — Define the Geometry**

A rigid lever pivots at point O. The actuator input displacement $\delta_{in}$ is applied at distance $L_{in}$ from the pivot. The output displacement $\delta_{out}$ is measured at distance $L_{out}$ on the opposite arm.

**Step 2 — Apply Small-Angle Rotation Approximation**

For small angles $\theta$, the displacements along the lever are linear:

$$
\delta_{in} = L_{in} \cdot \theta
$$
$$
\delta_{out} = L_{out} \cdot \theta
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Two Equations for the Ratio**

Dividing the second equation by the first:

$$
\frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} = \frac{L_{out}}{L_{in}}
$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical Advantage Trade-off**

By conservation of energy (ideal rigid lever, no friction):

$$
F_{in} \cdot \delta_{in} = F_{out} \cdot \delta_{out}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = \frac{L_{in}}{L_{out}}
$$

**Key Insight:** A lever with $L_{out} \gg L_{in}$ amplifies **displacement** but reduces **force** by the same factor. KTU examiners frequently test this inverse trade-off.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Bimorph Jaw Deflection

A thermal micro gripper uses two bonded layers with different thermal expansion coefficients $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$. On heating by $\Delta T$, the layer with higher $\alpha$ wants to expand more, forcing the beam to bend.

**Step 1 — Curvature Equation from Timoshenko Bimorph Theory**

$$
\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{6 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T (1+m)^2}{h \left[3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)\left(m^2 + \dfrac{1}{mn}\right)\right]}
$$

where $m = t_1/t_2$ is the thickness ratio, $n = E_1/E_2$ is the modulus ratio, and $h = t_1 + t_2$.

**Step 2 — Tip Deflection for a Cantilever of Length L**

For small deflections, the tip deflection is:

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{L^2}{2 \rho}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\rho$**

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{3 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T \cdot L^2 (1+m)^2}{h \left[3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)\left(m^2 + \dfrac{1}{mn}\right)\right]}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical Limiting Case (Equal Thickness, Equal Modulus: $m=1, n=1$)**

The expression simplifies to:

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{L^2}{h} \cdot (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Final Simplified Bimorph Deflection Formula:**

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{2 (t_1 + t_2)}
$$

This is the form most commonly tested in KTU derivations.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-off Comparison

| Design Parameter | Electrostatic | Piezoelectric | Thermal / Bimorph | SMA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stroke** | Low (1–10 µm) | Medium (10–50 µm) | High (50–500 µm) | Very High (mm range) |
| **Force** | Low (µN range) | Very High (N range) | Medium (mN range) | High (N range) |
| **Response Time** | < 1 ms | < 1 ms | 10 ms – 1 s | 0.1 – 5 s |
| **Drive Voltage** | 30 – 200 V | 50 – 200 V | 2 – 12 V | 1 – 5 V (with heating) |
| **Power Consumption** | Very Low | Low | High | High |
| **CMOS Compatible?** | **Yes** | Partial | No | No |
| **Biocompatibility** | Good (with coating) | Good | Good | Excellent (Nitinol) |
| **Hysteresis** | None | 10–20 % | Low | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram maps the functional flow of a generic micro gripper system, showing the interaction between the input stimulus, actuator mechanism, mechanical amplification stage, and the final gripping action.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CONTROL INPUT SIGNAL] --> B[POWER CONDITIONING UNIT]
    B --> C[ACTUATOR CORE ELEMENT]
    C --> D[MECHANICAL AMPLIFIER LEVER OR FLEXURE]
    D --> E[COMPLIANT HINGE PIVOT]
    E --> F[GRIPPER JAW ASSEMBLY]
    F --> G[TARGET MICRO OBJECT]
    G --> H[FORCE SENSOR FEEDBACK]
    H --> A

    subgraph ACTUATION MODULE
        C
    end
    subgraph TRANSMISSION MODULE
        D
        E
    end
    subgraph END EFFECTOR MODULE
        F
        G
    end
    subgraph CLOSED LOOP FEEDBACK
        H
        A
    end
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Comparison of Actuation Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ELECTROSTATIC PATH
        E1[HIGH VOLTAGE SUPPLY] --> E2[CHARGED COMB FINGERS]
        E2 --> E3[CANTILEVER JAW]
    end
    subgraph PIEZOELECTRIC PATH
        P1[DRIVE VOLTAGE] --> P2[PZT OR ALN STACK]
        P2 --> P3[LEVER AMPLIFIER]
        P3 --> P4[GRIPPER FINGER]
    end
    subgraph THERMAL PATH
        T1[CURRENT PULSE] --> T2[RESISTIVE HEATER]
        T2 --> T3[BIMORPH BEAM]
        T3 --> P4
    end
    subgraph SMA PATH
        S1[JOULE HEATING] --> S2[NITINOL WIRE]
        S2 --> S3[PHASE TRANSFORMATION]
        S3 --> P4
    end
```

### 4.3 State-Transition Diagram of Gripper Operation Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OPEN_STATE
    OPEN_STATE --> APPROACH_OBJECT : Move gripper toward target using micropositioner
    APPROACH_OBJECT --> CLOSING_JAWS : Apply actuation stimulus
    CLOSING_JAWS --> HOLDING : Reach desired displacement or force setpoint
    HOLDING --> OBJECT_HELD : Maintain holding force using closed loop control
    OBJECT_HELD --> RELEASING : Remove or reverse actuation stimulus
    RELEASING --> OPEN_STATE : Jaws return to rest position by spring restoring force
    OPEN_STATE --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Piezoelectric Micro Gripper Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Function Generator] --> A2[High Voltage Amplifier]
    A2 --> A3[PZT Piezo Stack Actuator]
    A3 --> A4[Displacement Amplifier Lever]
    A4 --> A5[Compliant Flexure Hinge]
    A5 --> A6[Movable Gripper Jaw]
    A6 --> A7[Fixed Gripper Jaw]
    A7 --> A8[Micro Object held between jaws]
    A8 --> A9[Strain Gauge Force Sensor on jaw]
    A9 --> A10[PID Controller feedback loop]
    A10 --> A2
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

*Define a MEMS micro gripper. List any four actuation principles used for micro grippers.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A MEMS micro gripper is a microscale mechanical end-effector that uses microfabricated compliant structures to grasp, hold, and release objects with dimensions in the range of **1 µm to 1 mm**, by converting an input stimulus (electrical, thermal, magnetic, or pneumatic) into mechanical jaw displacement.

Four actuation principles are:
1. Electrostatic actuation (comb drive / parallel plate)
2. Piezoelectric actuation (PZT, AlN)
3. Thermal actuation (bimorph, shape memory alloy)
4. Electromagnetic actuation (Lorentz force)

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing four with one-line descriptions.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

*State the principle of operation of a piezoelectric micro gripper. Mention two advantages.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A piezoelectric micro gripper operates on the **inverse piezoelectric effect**, in which certain crystalline materials (e.g., Lead Zirconate Titanate) generate a mechanical strain when subjected to an external electric field. This strain is transmitted through a displacement amplifier to actuate the gripping jaws.

Two advantages:
1. High force output (millinewton to newton range) suitable for handling rigid micro-objects.
2. Fast dynamic response (sub-millisecond) and precise position control.

*(1 mark for principle, 1 mark per advantage.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

## ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 1 Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working of an **electrostatic micro gripper**. Derive the expression for the electrostatic gripping force. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare electrostatic and piezoelectric micro grippers on the basis of **gripping force, displacement, response time, drive voltage, and power consumption**. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Working and Derivation

A schematic of the electrostatic micro gripper is shown below. Two sets of interdigitated comb fingers form the actuator; one set is fixed to the substrate, the other is connected to a movable cantilever beam ending in the gripper jaw.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Fixed Comb Finger Set] --- A2[Movable Comb Finger Set]
    A2 --- A3[Compliant Suspension Beam]
    A3 --- A4[Movable Gripper Jaw]
    A4 --- A5[Micro Object]
    A5 --- A6[Fixed Gripper Jaw]
```

**Working Principle:**

When a voltage $V$ is applied between the fixed and movable comb finger sets, opposite charges accumulate on the facing surfaces, producing a Coulombic attractive force. This force pulls the movable fingers laterally, displacing the cantilever beam and rotating the movable jaw toward the fixed jaw, thereby gripping the object.

**Derivation:**

The capacitance of an interdigitated comb drive with $n$ finger pairs, finger thickness $t$, and gap $g$ is:

$$
C = n \cdot \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 t L}{g}
$$

where $L$ is the overlap length of the fingers.

The stored energy is $U = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$.

The lateral electrostatic force is obtained by differentiating energy with respect to lateral displacement $x$:

$$
F_e = \frac{\partial U}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}
$$

For comb drives, $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \dfrac{2 n \varepsilon_0 t}{g}$.

Therefore:

$$
F_e = \frac{n \, \varepsilon_0 \, t \, V^2}{g}
$$

**Significance:** The force is independent of lateral displacement $x$, which is why comb drives provide *constant-force* actuation — ideal for grippers where consistent gripping force is required across varying jaw openings.

*(3 marks working, 4 marks derivation — full marks if units and assumptions are stated.)*

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Electrostatic Gripper | Piezoelectric Gripper |
|---|---|---|
| **Gripping Force** | Low (10 µN – 1 mN) | High (1 mN – 100 mN) |
| **Displacement** | Very small (1 – 10 µm) | Moderate (10 – 50 µm) |
| **Response Time** | < 1 ms (very fast) | < 1 ms (very fast) |
| **Drive Voltage** | High (30 – 200 V) | High (50 – 200 V) |
| **Power Consumption** | Very low | Low |
| **Hysteresis** | Negligible | 10 – 20 % |
| **CMOS Compatibility** | Excellent | Poor |

**Conclusion:** Electrostatic grippers are preferred for **low-force, high-precision CMOS-integrated applications**, while piezoelectric grippers dominate **high-force biomedical and micro-assembly tasks** where larger gripping force is essential.

*(1 mark per parameter × 6 parameters, 1 mark for the conclusion = 7 marks.)*

---

## ❓ Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 1 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the **bimorph thermal micro gripper**. Derive the expression for tip deflection of a bimorph cantilever. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A thermal micro gripper uses two layers of aluminum ($\alpha_1 = 23 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{K}$) and silicon ($\alpha_2 = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{K}$), each of thickness $t_1 = t_2 = 10\ \mu\text{m}$, length $L = 5\ \text{mm}$. If the heater raises the temperature by $\Delta T = 100\ \text{K}$, calculate the **tip deflection**. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Bimorph Thermal Micro Gripper

A bimorph thermal micro gripper consists of two layers of materials with **different coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE)** that are bonded together. One layer is typically a passive structural material (silicon) and the other is an active heater layer (metal or polysilicon with embedded resistive heater).

**Working:** When current is passed through the resistive heater, the temperature rises. The layer with higher CTE expands more than the other, causing the beam to bend. This bending is transmitted to the gripper jaws, causing them to close.

**Derivation:**

Consider two layers (1 and 2) with thicknesses $t_1, t_2$, Young's moduli $E_1, E_2$, and thermal expansion coefficients $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$. The beam is heated uniformly by $\Delta T$.

The Timoshenko bimorph curvature formula gives:

$$
\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{6 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T (1+m)^2}{h \left[3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)\left(m^2 + \dfrac{1}{mn}\right)\right]}
$$

where $m = t_1/t_2$, $n = E_1/E_2$, and $h = t_1 + t_2$.

The tip deflection of a cantilever of length $L$ is:

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{L^2}{2 \rho}
$$

For the practical case where $m = 1$ and $n = 1$:

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{2 (t_1 + t_2)}
$$

*(3 marks working, 4 marks derivation.)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation

**Given Data:**
- $\alpha_1 = 23 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{K}$ (Aluminum)
- $\alpha_2 = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{K}$ (Silicon)
- $t_1 = t_2 = 10\ \mu\text{m} = 10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$
- $L = 5\ \text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$
- $\Delta T = 100\ \text{K}$

**Step 1 — Verify the simplified formula conditions**

Since $t_1 = t_2$, the ratio $m = 1$. Assuming equal Young's moduli ($n = 1$), we apply the simplified formula.

**Step 2 — Calculate $\alpha_1 - \alpha_2$**

$$
\alpha_1 - \alpha_2 = (23 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{K} = 20.4 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{K}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the tip deflection equation**

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{3 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot (20.4 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 100}{2 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-6} + 10 \times 10^{-6})}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator Evaluation**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 3 \cdot (25 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (20.4 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 100
$$

$$
= 3 \cdot 25 \cdot 20.4 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12}
$$

$$
= 153000 \times 10^{-12} = 1.53 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 5 — Denominator Evaluation**

$$
\text{Denominator} = 2 \cdot (20 \times 10^{-6}) = 40 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Division**

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{1.53 \times 10^{-7}}{40 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.53 \times 10^{-7}}{4.0 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\delta_{tip} = 3.825 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_{tip} \approx 3.83\ \text{mm}}
$$

This large deflection explains why thermal bimorph grippers are favored for applications requiring substantial jaw opening.

*(1 mark data identification, 2 marks formula substitution, 2 marks step-wise computation, 1 mark final answer with correct unit, 1 mark interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Marks Loss Points**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students often write $L$ in mm without converting to meters before substituting into formulas derived in SI units. **Always convert to SI (metres) before calculation.**
> 2. **Forgetting $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$:** The bimorph formula requires the **higher CTE value first**. Reversing the order introduces a sign error.
> 3. **Ignoring the 1/2 factor:** Several students omit the factor of $\dfrac{1}{2}$ in the energy-to-force conversion $F = -\partial U/\partial x$.
> 4. **Confusing Comb-Drive vs Parallel-Plate Force:** Comb drives give constant force independent of displacement $x$; parallel-plate force depends on $1/d^2$. KTU expects the correct formula for each geometry.
> 5. **No mention of hysteresis and creep** in piezoelectric gripper answers — a guaranteed 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **micro gripper** is a microscale end-effector for grasping objects in the 1 µm – 1 mm size range.
- [ ] Four primary actuation principles: **Electrostatic, Piezoelectric, Thermal (Bimorph), and SMA**. Magnetic and pneumatic are secondary.
- [ ] **Electrostatic parallel-plate force:** $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}$ — independent of $d$ for comb drives.
- [ ] **Comb-drive force:** $F_e = \dfrac{n \varepsilon_0 t V^2}{g}$ — **independent of lateral displacement** (key advantage).
- [ ] **Piezoelectric strain:** $S = d_{33} \cdot V/t_p$ — drives the inverse piezoelectric effect.
- [ ] **Bimorph tip deflection:** $\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{2(t_1 + t_2)}$ — for equal thickness and modulus.
- [ ] **Lever displacement amplification** trades force for stroke: $\delta_{out}/\delta_{in} = L_{out}/L_{in}$.
- [ ] **Gripping force is friction-limited:** $F_g \leq \mu F_{normal}$ — important for object slip criterion.
- [ ] **At microscale, surface forces dominate** over volume forces (inertia, gravity).
- [ ] **Capillary/adhesion force** $F_c = 4 \pi \gamma R \cos\theta$ must be overcome for object **release** (very critical for micro manipulation).
- [ ] **Electrostatic grippers** are **CMOS-compatible** — preferred for integrated microsystems.
- [ ] **Piezoelectric grippers** offer the highest force density and precision but suffer from **hysteresis (~15%)**.
- [ ] **Thermal grippers** provide the largest displacement but are **slow** (~seconds).
- [ ] **SMA (Nitinol) grippers** offer shape-memory recovery force but require **Joule heating**.
- [ ] Always convert dimensions to **metres (SI units)** before numerical substitution.
- [ ] **Compliant flexure hinges** (not conventional bearings) are used at micro scale to eliminate friction and wear.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT742 - INTRODUCTION TO MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Micro Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Micro Motors** are microfabricated rotary or linear electromechanical actuators that convert electrical, magnetic, thermal, or piezoelectric energy into controlled mechanical motion (rotation or translation) at the micrometer-to-millimeter scale. They are manufactured using **Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)** batch-fabrication processes derived from semiconductor technology, primarily silicon-based bulk and surface micromachining.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, micro motors are classified under the broader category of **MEMS actuators**, distinguished from sensors by their ability to *deliver usable mechanical work* (force × displacement) rather than merely transducing physical stimuli.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a ceiling fan shrunk to the size of a coin.** A normal fan uses electromagnetic coils (copper windings) and permanent magnets spinning at 300 RPM. A micro motor accomplishes the same rotation, but instead of copper wire coils, it uses *etched silicon electrodes* with gaps as small as 2 µm, or *integrated thin-film magnetic circuits*. Just as a conventional motor turns electricity into spinning motion to cool a room, a micro motor turns electricity into controlled rotation to drive a micro-gear, pump blood in a lab-on-chip, or rotate a micro-mirror in an optical switch.

The key insight: **scaling laws are unforgiving** — when you shrink a motor to the micron scale, electromagnetic forces collapse (because the magnetic volume scales as $L^3$) while electrostatic forces actually become dominant (because electric field strength scales as $1/L$). This is the fundamental design driver behind why most MEMS micro motors are *electrostatic*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **standard SI constants** govern micro motor design and are **mandatory** for KTU numerical answers:
> - **Vacuum permittivity:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m**
> - **Free-space permeability:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m**
> - **Air breakdown field:** $E_{breakdown} \approx 3 \times 10^6$ **V/m**
> - **Typical rotor diameter:** **100 µm – 5 mm**
> - **Typical operating speed:** **100 – 100,000 RPM**
> - **Typical output torque:** **nN·m – µN·m**
> - **Typical power output:** **nW – mW**

## 1.4 Classification Overview (KTU Module 1 Scope)

Micro motors are broadly grouped by their **energy transduction mechanism**:

| Energy Domain | Micro Motor Type | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Electric Field | Electrostatic (Variable Capacitance / Variable Reluctance) | Micro turbines, optical scanners |
| Magnetic Field | Electromagnetic / Magnetoelectric | Micro hard disk drives, micro robots |
| Piezoelectric | Piezoelectric ultrasonic motor | Watch movements, camera autofocus |
| Thermal | Thermal bimorph / Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) | Micro grippers, micro valves |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Are Micro Motors Difficult? — The Scaling Problem

The dominant challenge in micro motor design is the **unfavorable scaling of magnetic forces at small dimensions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnetic energy density** $u_B = \dfrac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$ remains *constant* with scaling, but the *total magnetic force* on a coil scales with the **cube of length** $F \propto L^3$. Meanwhile, **electrostatic energy density** $u_E = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2$ also remains constant, but for the same voltage across a smaller gap, $E$ becomes *larger*, giving electrostatic motors a **fundamental advantage** in the micro-scale regime.

This is why **electrostatic micro motors are the workhorse** of MEMS research and is the most heavily-asked topic in KTU Module 1.

## 2.2 The Electrostatic Variable-Capacitance (VC) Micro Motor

### 2.2.1 Working Principle — Step by Step

The **Variable Capacitance micro motor** (pioneered by **Fan, Tai, and Muller**, UC Berkeley, 1989) is a synchronous electric motor operating on the principle of **minimization of stored electrostatic energy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. The rotor is a **freely rotating conductive disk** (typically polysilicon) supported on a hub.
2. The stator consists of a **patterned array of stationary electrodes** arranged around the rotor periphery.
3. A **multi-phase AC voltage** (typically 3-phase, square-wave or sinusoidal) is applied to the stator electrodes in a precise sequence.
4. The voltage is timed so that the **electric field lines always pull the rotor toward the position of maximum capacitance** (i.e., where rotor electrodes most closely overlap stator electrodes).
5. As the rotor slides under the energized stator pole, capacitance increases; the next phase fires just before alignment, sustaining rotation.

The motor is **synchronous**: rotor position is locked to the stator excitation frequency. There are *no permanent magnets* in the classic VC design.

### 2.2.2 Torque Generation Mechanism

The instantaneous torque on the rotor is derived from the negative gradient of stored electrostatic co-energy with respect to angular position $\theta$:

$$\tau(\theta) = \frac{\partial W'_{elec}}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{V = \text{const}} = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{\partial C(\theta)}{\partial \theta}$$

The capacitance variation with angle for a typical VC motor with $N_r$ rotor poles and $N_s$ stator poles is:

$$C(\theta) = C_0 + C_1 \cos(N_s \theta)$$

Differentiating gives the torque expression:

$$\tau(\theta) = -\frac{1}{2} N_s C_1 V^2 \sin(N_s \theta)$$

The **peak torque** occurs at $\sin(N_s \theta) = 1$:

$$\tau_{peak} = \frac{1}{2} N_s C_1 V^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** This class of motor is the foundational example in KTU Module 1 because it directly couples *electrostatics* (a key MEMS physics topic) with *rotational dynamics*. The same torque principle underlies the **scratch-drive actuator** and the **levitated micromotor**.

## 2.3 The Variable-Reluctance (VR) Micro Motor

In a **Variable-Reluctance (VR) motor**, the rotor is a **ferromagnetic or permalloy disk** with salient poles, and the stator carries current-carrying windings. The rotor always tries to align with the path of **minimum magnetic reluctance** (i.e., maximum inductance).

$$\tau = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta}$$

VR micro motors are difficult to fabricate in silicon because integrating high-permeability magnetic materials and three-dimensional windings is incompatible with planar lithography.

## 2.4 The Side-Driven Electrostatic Motor (Detailed KTU Sub-Type)

The **side-driven (or wobbler) motor** has stator electrodes on the *side wall* of the rotor, not beneath it. The rotor is suspended above the substrate by a small air gap (typically 1–5 µm). The advantage is that the rotor can spin **continuously** (unlike the top-driven VC motor which often has angle limits).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Side-driven motors achieve continuous rotation because the rotor is free to sweep through the full 360° without mechanical interference from the stator. The trade-off is **lower torque density** because the side-wall electrode area is small.

## 2.5 Electromagnetic Micro Motors

For higher torque, **electromagnetic micro motors** use:
- Integrated planar coils (electroplated copper) on the stator
- Permanent magnet rotor (sputtered NdFeB or SmCo)
- Or vice versa (coil rotor, magnet stator)

$$\vec{F} = I \vec{L} \times \vec{B}$$

The Lorentz force per unit length on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field generates the tangential force that turns the rotor. Power output:

$$P_{out} = \tau \cdot \omega = K_t I \cdot \omega$$

where $K_t$ is the **torque constant** and $\omega$ is angular velocity in rad/s.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield for KTU Part B questions** on Micro Motors.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electrostatic force on capacitor plate | $F = \dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2 A = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2}$ | $\varepsilon_0$ in F/m, $V$ in V, $d$ in m, $A$ in m² | $V < V_{breakdown} = E_{bd} \cdot d$ |
| 2 | Electrostatic torque (VC motor) | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{2}V^2 \dfrac{\partial C(\theta)}{\partial \theta}$ | $\tau$ in N·m, $C$ in F, $\theta$ in rad | Synchronous operation |
| 3 | Peak VC torque | $\tau_{peak} = \dfrac{1}{2}N_s C_1 V^2$ | $N_s$ = stator pole count | When $\sin(N_s\theta) = 1$ |
| 4 | VR motor torque | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{2}i^2 \dfrac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta}$ | $i$ in A, $L$ in H, $\theta$ in rad | Current-limited mode |
| 5 | Lorentz force (e/m motor) | $\vec{F} = I \vec{L} \times \vec{B}$ | $F$ in N, $B$ in T | Steady DC current |
| 6 | Motor power output | $P_{out} = \tau \cdot \omega$ | $\tau$ in N·m, $\omega$ in rad/s, $P$ in W | $\omega < \omega_{max}$ |
| 7 | Back-EMF voltage | $V_{bemf} = K_e \cdot \omega$ | $K_e$ = back-EMF constant (V·s/rad) | At constant flux |
| 8 | Synchronous speed | $N_s^{rpm} = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | $f$ = excitation Hz, $P$ = pole pairs | VC and e/m synchronous |
| 9 | Force scaling law | $F_{mag} \propto L^2$ , $F_{elec} \propto L^0$ (at constant V) | $L$ = characteristic length | Favors electrostatic at small L |
| 10 | Frictional (viscous) torque | $\tau_f = \mu \dfrac{\omega r^3 L}{h}$ | $\mu$ = viscosity, $h$ = air gap | Dominant loss in micro motors |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of Micro Motors

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are micro motors important in real engineering?**

- **Microfluidics:** Driving peristaltic micropumps in lab-on-chip drug-delivery systems.
- **Optical MEMS (MOEMS):** Rotating micro-mirrors in optical cross-connects and barcode scanners.
- **Micro-robotics:** Providing propulsion for capsule endoscopes and micro aerial vehicles (MAVs).
- **Data storage:** Spinning micro hard disk platters in early micro disk drives (e.g., the IBM "Millipede" project).
- **Wearable electronics:** Tactile feedback in haptic devices using piezo micro motors.
- **Watchmaking:** Piezoelectric ultrasonic motors (e.g., Seiko Kinetic) for ultra-thin wristwatch movements.

The most famous historical example is the **MIT micro turbine** (Epstein & Senturia, 1997), a **5 mm-diameter gas turbine engine** running on compressed air at 1.2 million RPM — purely a thermal micro motor, not electrical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Peak Torque of a Variable-Capacitance Micro Motor

**Problem Setup:** A 12-pole, 3-phase electrostatic VC micro motor has a rotor–stator overlap area of $A = 4 \times 10^{-8}$ m², an air gap $d = 4$ µm, and is driven by a 100 V peak phase voltage. The motor uses silicon nitride as the dielectric ($\varepsilon_r = 7.5$). Determine the peak electrostatic torque per phase, and the total peak torque summed over all 3 phases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This exact derivation is a standard 7-mark Part B question in KTU ESE examinations. The student is expected to derive the expression, substitute numerical values with correct SI units, and present results in proper engineering notation.

### Step 1 — Identify the relevant capacitance geometry

The rotor–stator pair forms a **parallel-plate capacitor** at the angular position of maximum overlap. The capacitance is:

$$C_{max} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

### Step 2 — Identify the relevant capacitance variation

For a multi-pole motor, the capacitance varies sinusoidally. The amplitude $C_1$ of the variation is approximately **half** the difference between the maximum and minimum overlap capacitance. For a fully-overlapped and fully-un-overlapped geometry:

$$C_1 = \frac{C_{max} - C_{min}}{2} \approx \frac{C_{max}}{2} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{2d}$$

(assuming $C_{min} \ll C_{max}$, which is standard in well-designed VC motors).

### Step 3 — Write the peak torque expression

From the formula sheet:

$$\tau_{peak,1\phi} = \frac{1}{2} N_s C_1 V^2$$

Substitute $C_1$:

$$\tau_{peak,1\phi} = \frac{1}{2} N_s \left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{2d}\right) V^2 = \frac{N_s \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{4d}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute numerical values

Given:
- $N_s = 12$
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- $\varepsilon_r = 7.5$
- $A = 4 \times 10^{-8}$ m²
- $d = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $V = 100$ V

$$\tau_{peak,1\phi} = \frac{(12)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(7.5)(4 \times 10^{-8})(100)^2}{4 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}$$

Compute numerator:
- $(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(7.5) = 6.6405 \times 10^{-11}$
- $(6.6405 \times 10^{-11})(4 \times 10^{-8}) = 2.6562 \times 10^{-18}$
- $(2.6562 \times 10^{-18})(10000) = 2.6562 \times 10^{-14}$
- $(2.6562 \times 10^{-14})(12) = 3.1874 \times 10^{-13}$

Compute denominator:
- $4 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$

Final division:

$$\tau_{peak,1\phi} = \frac{3.1874 \times 10^{-13}}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.992 \times 10^{-8} \text{ N·m}$$

### Step 5 — Total peak torque for 3-phase excitation

For a balanced 3-phase drive with 120° electrical spacing, only **one phase produces peak torque at a time**, and the others are at intermediate values. The total instantaneous peak torque summed across all phases (synchronous operation) is:

$$\tau_{peak,total} = \tau_{peak,1\phi} = 1.992 \times 10^{-8} \text{ N·m} \approx 20 \text{ nN·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** This value (~20 nN·m) is **physically consistent** with reported experimental values for Berkeley-style VC motors of this size class, which typically deliver 10–100 nN·m. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Synchronous Speed Calculation

**Given:** A 3-phase VC micro motor with 12 stator poles is driven by a 50 Hz square-wave excitation.

**Step 1 — Identify the synchronous speed formula**

$$N_{sync}^{rpm} = \frac{120 f}{P}$$

where $P$ = number of pole *pairs* = $N_s / 2 = 12/2 = 6$.

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$N_{sync}^{rpm} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to rad/s**

$$\omega_{sync} = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi (1000)}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU valuation tip:** Examiners award 2 marks *just* for the correct identification that $P$ = pole *pairs* and not total poles. Writing $P = 12$ is a **common mistake** that costs the answer half its marks.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy and Power of a Side-Driven Micro Motor

**Given:** A side-driven electrostatic micro motor with rotor radius $r = 500$ µm, height $h = 10$ µm, and air gap $g = 2$ µm between rotor side wall and stator electrode. The applied voltage is 80 V. The motor rotates at 5000 RPM in air (viscosity $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s). Estimate the **viscous drag torque** and **mechanical power output**.

**Step 1 — Write the viscous drag torque formula for a rotating cylinder (Couette flow approximation)**

$$\tau_f = \mu \frac{\omega r^3 L}{g}$$

where $L$ = rotor height, $g$ = air gap.

**Step 2 — Convert RPM to rad/s**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi (5000)}{60} = 523.6 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values**

- $r^3 = (500 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-10}$ m³
- $L = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $g = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s

$$\tau_f = (1.8 \times 10^{-5}) \frac{(523.6)(1.25 \times 10^{-10})(10 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

Compute numerator:
- $(523.6)(1.25 \times 10^{-10}) = 6.545 \times 10^{-8}$
- $(6.545 \times 10^{-8})(10 \times 10^{-6}) = 6.545 \times 10^{-13}$
- $(1.8 \times 10^{-5})(6.545 \times 10^{-13}) = 1.178 \times 10^{-17}$

Divide by $g = 2 \times 10^{-6}$:

$$\tau_f = \frac{1.178 \times 10^{-17}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.89 \times 10^{-12} \text{ N·m} \approx 5.9 \text{ pN·m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute mechanical power**

$$P_{mech} = \tau_f \cdot \omega = (5.89 \times 10^{-12})(523.6) = 3.08 \times 10^{-9} \text{ W} \approx 3.1 \text{ nW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The viscous drag in MEMS is so dominant that *air* — not friction at the bearing — is usually the primary loss mechanism. This is why **vacuum-packaged** micro motors can achieve 10×–100× higher speeds and efficiencies.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Micro Motor Trade-off Table

| Design Criterion | Electrostatic VC | Electromagnetic | Piezoelectric | Thermal/SMA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fabrication compatibility with CMOS/MEMS** | Excellent | Poor (needs 3-D coils) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Output torque density** | Low (nN·m) | Moderate (µN·m) | Moderate (µN·m) | High (µN·m) |
| **Operating speed** | High (kRPM) | Moderate | Very Low (rpm) | Very Low |
| **Power consumption** | Very low | High | Low | High |
| **Continuous rotation possible?** | Yes (side-driven) | Yes | No (stepping/oscillatory) | Limited |
| **Vacuum requirement?** | No | No | No | No |
| **Voltage requirement** | 50–200 V | 3–12 V | 10–100 V | 1–5 V (with heating) |
| **KTU Module 1 exam weight** | **High** | Moderate | Moderate | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
