# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define a micro motor. List any two energy domains used to drive MEMS micro motors and give one application each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **micro motor** is a MEMS-fabricated rotary or linear actuator that delivers usable mechanical work at the micrometer scale, converting an input energy form into controlled mechanical motion.

| Energy Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Electrostatic** | Micro turbine for lab-on-chip gas analysis |
| **Electromagnetic** | Micro hard-disk drive spindle (historical) |
| **Piezoelectric** | Watch movement, camera lens autofocus |

*(Examiner's allocation: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for correctly tabulated domains and applications.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Why are electrostatic micro motors preferred over electromagnetic micro motors at the micron scale?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Electrostatic motors are preferred because:

1. **Scaling argument:** Electromagnetic force scales as $F \propto L^2$ (length squared) at constant current density, so torque collapses rapidly with miniaturization. Electrostatic force scales as $F \propto V^2/d^2$ and can be *increased* by reducing the air gap $d$ — which is a natural outcome of photolithographic shrinkage.

2. **Fabrication compatibility:** Electrostatic motors use *planar* conductive electrodes (polysilicon) that are fully compatible with standard surface-micromachining. Electromagnetic motors require 3-D wire windings or thick-film permanent magnets, which are extremely difficult to integrate.

3. **Power consumption:** Electrostatic motors draw negligible DC current (only displacement current), making them ideal for low-power applications.

*(Examiner's allocation: 2 marks for the scaling argument, 1 mark for fabrication compatibility.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a **Variable-Capacitance (VC) electrostatic micro motor**. Derive the expression for the peak torque produced by such a motor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Construction (3 marks):**
> - A **polysilicon rotor disk** is suspended above a **stator substrate** with an air gap of 2–5 µm.
> - The stator is patterned with an array of **equally spaced conductive poles** (typically 12 or 24 poles).
> - The rotor has a matching number of poles on its underside.
> - The rotor is supported on a central hub or levitated.
>
> **Working (2 marks):**
> - A **3-phase AC voltage** is applied to the stator poles in a precisely timed sequence.
> - The electric field exerts a **tangential attractive force** on the rotor poles, pulling the rotor toward the position of **maximum capacitance** (maximum overlap).
> - The rotor moves one pole-pitch per electrical cycle, producing continuous synchronous rotation.
>
> **Derivation (2 marks):**
> The instantaneous torque is:
> $$\tau = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{dC(\theta)}{d\theta}$$
> For $C(\theta) = C_0 + C_1 \cos(N_s \theta)$:
> $$\tau(\theta) = -\frac{1}{2} V^2 N_s C_1 \sin(N_s \theta)$$
> Peak torque when $\sin(N_s \theta) = 1$:
> $$\boxed{\tau_{peak} = \frac{1}{2} N_s C_1 V^2}$$

**Q (b)** A VC micro motor has 18 stator poles, overlap area $A = 6 \times 10^{-8}$ m², air gap $d = 3$ µm, and operates in air at 120 V. Calculate the **peak torque per phase**. Given $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute maximum capacitance.
> $$C_{max} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(6 \times 10^{-8})}{3 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.771 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F}$$
>
> **Step 2 (2 marks):** Determine $C_1$ (amplitude of variation).
> $$C_1 = \frac{C_{max}}{2} = 8.855 \times 10^{-14} \text{ F}$$
>
> **Step 3 (1 mark):** Write peak torque expression.
> $$\tau_{peak} = \frac{1}{2} N_s C_1 V^2$$
>
> **Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute numerical values.
> $$\tau_{peak} = \frac{1}{2}(18)(8.855 \times 10^{-14})(120)^2$$
> $$\tau_{peak} = (9)(8.855 \times 10^{-14})(14400)$$
> $$\tau_{peak} = 1.148 \times 10^{-8} \text{ N·m} \approx 11.5 \text{ nN·m}$$
>
> **Step 5 (1 mark):** Final answer with units.
> $$\boxed{\tau_{peak} \approx 11.5 \text{ nN·m}}$$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**Q (a)** Compare **electrostatic** and **electromagnetic** micro motors with respect to (i) scaling behavior, (ii) fabrication complexity, (iii) output torque, and (iv) typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (tabular form — examiner-recommended):**

| Criterion | Electrostatic Micro Motor | Electromagnetic Micro Motor |
|---|---|---|
| **Scaling behavior** | Force *increases* as gap shrinks — favorable for MEMS | Force $\propto L^2$ — collapses at small L |
| **Fabrication complexity** | Low — planar lithography only | High — needs 3-D coils or bonded magnets |
| **Output torque** | Low (nN·m range) | Moderate (µN·m range) |
| **Typical drive voltage** | High (50–200 V) | Low (3–12 V) |
| **Continuous rotation** | Yes (side-driven) | Yes |
| **Typical applications** | Optical MEMS, microfluidic pumps | Micro robots, hard-disk spindles |

*(Examiner's allocation: 1 mark per criterion row, 1 mark for overall structure and clarity.)*

**Q (b)** Explain the **side-driven electrostatic micro motor** with a schematic. Why is continuous rotation possible in this design but limited in the top-driven design? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Construction (2 marks):**
> - The rotor is a freely suspended **polysilicon disk** above the stator.
> - The **stator electrodes are placed on the side wall** of the rotor (not directly beneath it), with a small lateral air gap (typically 2 µm).
> - The rotor is supported on a central post, sometimes with electrostatic levitation to eliminate bearing friction.
>
> **Working (3 marks):**
> - When a voltage is applied between the rotor and a side electrode, an **in-plane electric field** develops.
> - The field exerts a **tangential force** on the rotor at the closest point of side-wall proximity.
> - The 3-phase excitation rotates the energized side electrode around the circumference, pulling the rotor continuously.
>
> **Why continuous rotation is possible (2 marks):**
> - In the **side-driven** design, the rotor can sweep through the **full 360°** because no stator electrode is in the path of rotation.
> - In the **top-driven** (top-or-bottom) design, the rotor stops or hesitates when its electrode is exactly aligned with the stator electrode (the maximum-capacitance position is a stable equilibrium, not a passing-through one). Continuous rotation requires **levitation** (Huang et al., 1991 Berkeley design).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing pole count with pole pairs:** In synchronous speed formula $N = 120f/P$, students often use total pole count instead of pole pairs. This halves the marks.
> 2. **Forgetting SI units in substitutions:** Writing $d = 3$ µm as "3" in the capacitance formula without converting to metres gives an answer off by $10^{12}$. Always write units at every step.
> 3. **Omitting the factor of ½ in the torque formula:** Many textbooks write $\tau = V^2 (dC/d\theta)$ — the KTU board accepts both, but full marks require using $\tau = (1/2)V^2 (dC/d\theta)$ as the standard co-energy derivation.
> 4. **Forgetting that VC motors need AC drive:** A common Part A error is describing a VC motor as "DC-driven". It is strictly a synchronous AC motor.
> 5. **Mixing up Variable Capacitance (VC) with Variable Reluctance (VR):** VC = electrostatic, no magnetic material, planar. VR = magnetic, needs ferromagnetic rotor. Examiners do **not** award partial credit for these being interchanged.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Micro Motors)**

- [ ] **Definition:** A micro motor is a MEMS-fabricated actuator delivering nN·m to µN·m torque at sub-millimeter scale.
- [ ] **Key fact:** **Electrostatic motors dominate** at the micro scale due to favorable scaling ($F_{elec} \propto 1/d^2$).
- [ ] **VC motor = variable capacitance, electrostatic, synchronous, multi-phase AC drive.** No permanent magnets.
- [ ] **VR motor = variable reluctance, magnetic rotor, current-driven, 3-D fabrication challenge.**
- [ ] **Side-driven VC motor = continuous rotation possible** (no stator in rotor path).
- [ ] **Top-driven VC motor = limited rotation / requires levitation** (rotor stops at aligned stable equilibrium).
- [ ] **Peak torque formula:** $\tau_{peak} = \dfrac{1}{2} N_s C_1 V^2$
- [ ] **Synchronous speed:** $N_{sync} = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ (use *pole pairs* $P$, not total poles).
- [ ] **Dominant loss mechanism in MEMS motors = viscous air drag**, NOT bearing friction.
- [ ] **Back-EMF:** $V_{bemf} = K_e \omega$, which limits maximum no-load speed.
- [ ] **Electromagnetic motors** use Lorentz force $\vec{F} = I\vec{L} \times \vec{B}$; require either planar coils or bonded permanent magnets.
- [ ] **Piezoelectric motors** are *stepping/oscillatory*, not continuously rotating; used in watches and camera autofocus.
- [ ] **Thermal/SMA motors** have highest torque density but lowest bandwidth (slow, high-power).
- [ ] **Famous landmark:** MIT micro turbine (Epstein 1997) = 5 mm diameter, 1.2 M RPM, gas-driven.
- [ ] **Famous landmark:** UC Berkeley VC motor (Fan, Tai, Muller 1989) = first practical batch-fabricated silicon micro motor.
- [ ] **Always carry** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, and the air breakdown field $E_{bd} \approx 3 \times 10^6$ V/m into the exam.
- [ ] **Convert all dimensions to metres** before substituting into any formula. This is the #1 cause of lost marks in KTU numericals.
- [ ] **Memorize the scaling law:** $F_{mag} \propto L^2$, $F_{elec} \propto 1/d^2$ — a guaranteed Part A question every year.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT742 - INTRODUCTION TO MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Micro Valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Micro Valve** is a Micro-Electro-Mechanical System (MEMS) device engineered to regulate, direct, or control the flow (start, stop, throttle, or redirect) of a gaseous or liquid fluid at microscale dimensions, typically operating in channels with characteristic hydraulic diameters ranging from **1 µm to 1000 µm**. Micro valves are foundational building blocks of any functional microfluidic network and are classified under the broader umbrella of **Microfluidic MEMS** or **Bio-MEMS** when applied to biomedical contexts.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Code: PEEVT742, Module 1), micro valves are positioned as critical active and passive components that bridge **mechanical actuation principles** with **fluid dynamics at low Reynolds numbers**, enabling precision control in applications such as drug delivery, lab-on-chip diagnostics, and micro-propulsion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEEVT742 – Module 1):**
> Micro valves fall under the broader module theme of *MEMS Actuators and Fluidic Components*. The examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between **active** and **passive** valve architectures, identify actuation mechanisms, and articulate the relationship between **fluidic resistance**, **response time**, and **power consumption**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Tiny Traffic Cop for Fluids**
> Imagine a microscopic traffic policeman standing at the entrance of a narrow alley. Depending on the situation, he can:
> - **Let cars pass** (valve OPEN — fluid flows freely),
> - **Block them completely** (valve CLOSED — zero flow), or
> - **Slow them down to a trickle** (valve THROTTLING — partial restriction).
>
> Now, scale that alley down to a channel thinner than a human hair, and you have a **micro valve** in a microfluidic chip. Instead of waving his hands, the "policeman" might be a tiny cantilever bending under an electrostatic voltage, a piezoelectric disk flexing under an applied current, or simply a flap pushed open by the pressure of the fluid itself.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The design and evaluation of micro valves rely on the following standard physical constants and metrics, which students are expected to recall during the KTU examination:

- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 25 °C (µ):** $\mathbf{0.89 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}}$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure (P_atm):** $\mathbf{101.325 \ \text{kPa}}$
- **Surface tension of water (γ):** $\mathbf{72 \ \text{mN/m}}$ at 25 °C
- **Density of water (ρ):** $\mathbf{1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3}$
- **Typical microvalve response time (τ):** $\mathbf{1 \ \text{ms} - 100 \ \text{ms}}$
- **Operating pressure range (ΔP):** $\mathbf{0.1 \ \text{kPa} - 100 \ \text{kPa}}$
- **Critical Reynolds number (Re_crit) for microfluidics:** $\mathbf{Re < 2300}$ (Laminar regime, virtually always for microchannels)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Re almost always laminar in microchannels?**
> Because the characteristic length scale $L$ is on the order of micrometers, the Reynolds number $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ becomes vanishingly small even for moderate velocities. Turbulence is virtually impossible to sustain, which is why **Stokes flow** and **Hagen-Poiseuille** type equations dominate the analysis.

## 1.4 Classification of Micro Valves — Quick Overview

| Classification | Actuation Energy | Typical Response | Key Advantage | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive (Check / Diffuser)** | Fluid pressure only | < 1 ms | No external power | One-way flow only |
| **Electromagnetic** | Lorentz / Magnetic force | 5 – 20 ms | High force, large strokes | High power, magnetic hysteresis |
| **Electrostatic** | Coulomb attraction | < 1 ms | Ultra-low power, fast | Requires high voltage, small force |
| **Piezoelectric** | Inverse piezoelectric effect | < 1 ms | High force, fast | Expensive, hysteresis |
| **Thermal (Bimetallic / SMA)** | Joule heating | 10 – 100 ms | Large displacement | Slow, high power dissipation |
| **Pneumatic** | Compressed gas | 10 – 50 ms | High force, simple | Requires external compressor |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Five-Stage Logic Chain

Every micro valve, regardless of its actuation mechanism, follows a universal five-stage functional chain that the KTU examiner frequently tests in ESE Part A questions:

1. **Stimulus Reception** — The valve detects a control input. This can be:
   - An applied voltage (electrostatic, piezoelectric),
   - A current pulse (thermal, electromagnetic),
   - A fluid pressure differential (passive check valves).

2. **Energy Transduction** — The stimulus is converted into mechanical work. For example, an electrostatic voltage $V$ applied across a parallel-plate capacitor of gap $g$ generates an attractive force $F_e$.

3. **Mechanical Displacement** — The transduced force deflects a structural element (membrane, cantilever, flap, or poppet) from its rest position. The displacement $w$ is governed by the stiffness $k$ of the structure: $w = F/k$.

4. **Flow Path Modulation** — The deflected element either:
   - **Opens** a sealed orifice (normally-closed valve),
   - **Closes** an open channel (normally-open valve), or
   - **Throttles** the flow to an intermediate conductance.

5. **Fluidic Response** — The resulting flow rate $Q$ is governed by the **fluidic resistance** $R_f$ of the now-modified channel geometry and the applied pressure differential $\Delta P$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering "Why" Insight:**
> The KTU examiner rewards students who can articulate the **Why** behind each transition. For instance, why is electrostatic actuation preferred for ultra-fast gas microvalves? Because gas has low viscosity → low actuation force requirement → electrostatic's high speed and low power can be fully exploited. Conversely, why are thermal bimetallic valves avoided in bio-MEMS? Because the $\Delta T$ required (often 50–200 °C) can denature proteins and damage living cells.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization for ESE / Internal Assessment**
> The following table consolidates every equation you are likely to be tested on under the topic of micro valves. All variables are defined in the footnotes beneath the table.

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Variables | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hagen-Poiseuille (circular microchannel) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi r^4}$ | $L$: length, $r$: radius, $\mu$: viscosity, $Q$: flow rate | Pressure drop across circular microchannels — used for liquid drug delivery systems |
| 2 | Rectangular microchannel (aspect ratio $\alpha = h/w$) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^3} \left[1 - \dfrac{192 h}{\pi^5 w} \tanh\!\left(\dfrac{\pi w}{2 h}\right)\right]^{-1}$ | $w$: width, $h$: height | Most common microchannel shape in lab-on-chip devices |
| 3 | Fluidic resistance | $R_f = \dfrac{\Delta P}{Q}$ | $R_f$: fluidic resistance (Pa·s/m³) | Analogous to electrical resistance; enables circuit-style microfluidic analysis |
| 4 | Reynolds number (micro) | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | $D_h$: hydraulic diameter, $v$: velocity | Confirms laminar regime; Re $\ll 1$ for most microflows |
| 5 | Hydraulic diameter | $D_h = \dfrac{4 A}{P_w}$ | $A$: cross-section, $P_w$: wetted perimeter | Converts non-circular channels to equivalent circular diameter |
| 6 | Electrostatic actuation force (parallel plate) | $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 g^2}$ | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $A$: area, $V$: voltage, $g$: gap | Core force equation for electrostatic microvalves |
| 7 | Electrostatic pressure | $P_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 V^2}{2 g^2}$ | — | Pressure equivalent for membrane deflection analysis |
| 8 | Spring constant of clamped circular membrane | $k = \dfrac{16 \pi E t^3}{3 a^2 (1-\nu^2)}$ | $E$: Young's modulus, $t$: thickness, $a$: radius, $\nu$: Poisson's ratio | Determines restoring force of diaphragm in microvalve |
| 9 | Membrane center deflection (uniform pressure) | $w_0 = \dfrac{P a^4}{64 D}$, where $D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | $D$: flexural rigidity | Used to size diaphragm for required stroke |
| 10 | Flow coefficient (industry standard) | $Q = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{\text{SG}}}$ | $C_v$: flow coefficient, SG: specific gravity | Empirical metric for valve sizing |
| 11 | Valve response time (RC model) | $\tau = R_f \cdot C_{\text{fluid}}$ | $C_{\text{fluid}}$: fluidic capacitance | Analogous to electrical RC time constant |
| 12 | Piezoelectric induced strain | $S = d_{33} E_{\text{field}}$ | $d_{33}$: piezoelectric strain constant | Displacement per unit electric field in piezo valves |
| 13 | Thermal expansion (bimetallic) | $\Delta w = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | $\alpha$: thermal expansion coefficient | Bimetallic/SMA thermal valve stroke |
| 14 | Capillary number (for droplet valves) | $\text{Ca} = \dfrac{\mu v}{\gamma}$ | $\gamma$: surface tension | Predicts droplet breakup in capillary microvalves |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Convention:**
> In all KTU answer scripts, always define variables immediately after first use, state units explicitly, and show the numerical value of constants (e.g., $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m) at least once. Examiners deduct marks for "naked" equations without variable definitions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Micro valves are the **circulatory and nervous system** of any modern microfluidic platform. Their utility spans:

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Programmable insulin pumps and targeted drug delivery use normally-closed microvalves that open only when a physiological trigger (e.g., elevated blood glucose) is sensed.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Microvalve-gated sample chambers in chemical sensors trap analytes for spectroscopic analysis, then purge them for the next cycle.
- **Space Micropropulsion:** Cold-gas thrusters on CubeSats use arrays of microvalves to deliver precise impulses of propellant (e.g., $N_2$, $SF_6$) for attitude control with sub-millinewton accuracy.
- **Lab-on-Chip Diagnostics:** COVID-19 PCR chips use tens of microvalves to route reagents through thermal cycling zones, replacing entire laboratory workflows on a single silicon chip.
- **Inkjet Printing:** Thermal-bubble and piezoelectric inkjet printheads are essentially microvalves that eject picoliter droplets on demand at frequencies exceeding 30 kHz.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fluidic Resistance of a Rectangular Microchannel

This is a **KTU high-priority derivation** that frequently appears in ESE Part B (7-mark sub-questions). The goal is to derive the **fluidic resistance** $R_f$ of a rectangular microchannel of width $w$, height $h$, and length $L$, assuming steady, laminar, incompressible flow with the no-slip condition on all four walls.

### Step 1: Governing Equation — Navier-Stokes Simplification

For steady, fully-developed, laminar flow in the $x$-direction (along the channel length) with no body forces, the Navier-Stokes equation reduces to a single scalar form:

$$
\mu \left( \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \right) = \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x}
$$

Here, $u(y,z)$ is the velocity profile, $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity, and $\partial P / \partial x$ is the constant pressure gradient along the channel.

### Step 2: Replace Pressure Gradient with a Constant

Since the pressure varies linearly from inlet to outlet, we have:

$$
\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = -\dfrac{P_{\text{out}} - P_{\text{in}}}{L}
$$

The negative sign indicates that pressure decreases in the flow direction. Substituting this constant into the governing equation:

$$
\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu L}
$$

### Step 3: Recognize the Poisson Equation Structure

This is a 2D Poisson equation with a constant source term. The right-hand side is independent of $y$ and $z$, so we can integrate directly:

$$
u(y,z) = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} (y^2 + z^2) + C_1 y + C_2 z + C_3
$$

### Step 4: Apply No-Slip Boundary Conditions

For a rectangular channel extending from $y = -h/2$ to $y = h/2$ and $z = -w/2$ to $z = w/2$, the no-slip condition requires $u = 0$ on all four walls:

- At $y = \pm h/2$: $u = 0$
- At $z = \pm w/2$: $u = 0$

Applying the boundary condition at $y = h/2$:

$$
0 = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \dfrac{h^2}{4} + C_1 \cdot \dfrac{h}{2} + C_2 z + C_3
$$

For this to hold for **all** $z$, we must have $C_2 = 0$. Similarly, applying at $z = w/2$ and exploiting symmetry gives $C_1 = 0$ when the channel is centered. After solving:

$$
C_3 = \dfrac{\Delta P h^2}{8 \mu L} \quad \text{and the velocity profile becomes parabolic.}
$$

### Step 5: Integrate the Velocity Profile to Obtain Volumetric Flow Rate

The volumetric flow rate $Q$ is obtained by integrating the velocity over the cross-sectional area:

$$
Q = \int_{-w/2}^{w/2} \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} u(y,z) \, dy \, dz
$$

Substituting and evaluating the double integral (this is a textbook integral; the closed-form result is given in the formula sheet):

$$
Q = \dfrac{w h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L} \left[ 1 - \dfrac{192 h}{\pi^5 w} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \dfrac{\tanh(n \pi w / 2 h)}{n^5} \right]
$$

For a wide channel where $w \gg h$, the bracketed term reduces to approximately $1$, recovering the well-known **parallel-plate approximation**:

$$
Q \approx \dfrac{w h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}
$$

### Step 6: Identify the Fluidic Resistance

By definition, $R_f = \Delta P / Q$, so:

$$
R_f = \dfrac{12 \mu L}{w h^3} \left[ 1 - \dfrac{192 h}{\pi^5 w} \tanh\!\left(\dfrac{\pi w}{2 h}\right) \right]^{-1}
$$

This is the **final, board-exam-ready form** that students must be able to write from memory.

> [!TIP]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:**
> The bracketed correction factor is often mistakenly placed in the numerator. Always remember: **$R_f$ increases when the channel gets narrower or longer**, and the correction factor is therefore in the denominator to slightly *reduce* the resistance for wide channels.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Active vs. Passive Microvalves

The KTU examiner often asks students to compare microvalve architectures in tabular form (typically a 7-mark question). The following matrix is your exam-ready reference:

| Performance Metric | Electromagnetic | Electrostatic | Piezoelectric | Thermal (Bimetal) | Passive (Check) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Actuation Voltage** | 1 – 24 V | 50 – 200 V | 10 – 100 V | 1 – 5 V | 0 V (pressure-driven) |
| **Force Output** | High (mN) | Low (µN) | High (mN) | Medium (µN–mN) | Reaction to ΔP |
| **Stroke Length** | 10 – 500 µm | 1 – 10 µm | 1 – 50 µm | 10 – 100 µm | Self-adjusting |
| **Response Time** | 5 – 20 ms | < 1 ms | < 1 ms | 10 – 100 ms | < 1 ms |
| **Power Consumption** | 100 mW – 1 W | < 1 µW (static) | 1 – 10 mW | 50 – 500 mW | 0 W |
| **Biocompatibility** | Limited (magnetic materials) | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (heat) | Excellent |
| **Fabrication Complexity** | Medium | Low | High | Medium | Very Low |
| **Best Use Case** | High-pressure gas | Low-pressure lab-on-chip | Precision dosing | Slow flow regulation | One-way backflow prevention |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A rectangular microchannel in a drug-delivery chip has width $w = 200 \ \mu\text{m}$, height $h = 50 \ \mu\text{m}$, and length $L = 10 \ \text{mm}$. Water ($\mu = 0.89 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) flows through it under a pressure differential $\Delta P = 5 \ \text{kPa}$. Compute (a) the volumetric flow rate, (b) the fluidic resistance, and (c) the average velocity.

**Solution:**

**(a) Volumetric Flow Rate:**

Since $w / h = 200/50 = 4$, the parallel-plate approximation has a small error. Using the exact series expansion (or the formula sheet entry):

$$
Q = \dfrac{w h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L} \left[ 1 - \dfrac{192 h}{\pi^5 w} \tanh\!\left(\dfrac{\pi w}{2 h}\right) \right]^{-1}
$$

Plugging in the numbers:

$$
Q = \dfrac{(200 \times 10^{-6})(50 \times 10^{-6})^3 (5000)}{12 (0.89 \times 10^{-3})(10 \times 10^{-3})} \cdot \text{correction}
$$

The leading term evaluates to:

$$
Q_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-4})(1.25 \times 10^{-13})(5000)}{12 (0.89 \times 10^{-3})(10^{-2})} = 1.17 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 1.17 \ \mu\text{L/s}
$$

The correction factor (computed from the exact series) is approximately $1.15$, so:

$$
Q \approx 1.02 \ \mu\text{L/s}
$$

**(b) Fluidic Resistance:**

$$
R_f = \dfrac{\Delta P}{Q} = \dfrac{5000}{1.02 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 4.9 \times 10^{12} \ \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s} / \text{m}^3
$$

**(c) Average Velocity:**

$$
v_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{1.02 \times 10^{-9}}{(200 \times 10^{-6})(50 \times 10^{-6})} = 0.102 \ \text{m/s}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** Compute $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 0.102 \cdot 6.67 \times 10^{-5}}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 7.6$. Since Re $\ll 2300$, the laminar assumption is fully justified. ✓
