```mermaid
    subgraph Electrical
        e1[Voltage V]
        e2[Current I]
        e3[Charge Q]
        e4[Field E]
    end
    subgraph Thermal
        t1[Temperature T]
        t2[Heat Q]
    end
    subgraph Magnetic
        mag1[Field B]
        mag2[Flux Φ]
    end

    m2 -- PIEZOELECTRIC direct --> e3
    e4 -- PIEZOELECTRIC converse --> m2
    m1 -- PIEZORESISTIVE --> e2
    mag1 -- MAGNETOSTRICTIVE --> m2
    m1 -- VILLARI inverse --> mag1
    t1 -- SEEBECK --> e1
    e2 -- PELTIER --> t2
    e2 -- THOMSON --> t2
    e4 -- ELECTROSTATIC --> m3
    e2 -- LORENTZ mag --> m3
    m2 -- STICTION VDW --> e3
```

---

## Diagram 2 — Electron-Emission Process Flow

This block diagram walks through the **four electron-emission pathways** based on the energy-delivery mechanism.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Metal Surface with Bound Electrons] --> B{Energy Source?}
    B -- Heat --> C[THERMIONIC Emission]
    B -- Photon --> D[PHOTOELECTRIC Emission]
    B -- High Electric Field --> E[FIELD Emission / Fowler Nordheim]
    B -- Primary Electron Impact --> F[SECONDARY Emission]
    C --> G[Vacuum or Active Region]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[MEMS Device Current / Signal]
```

---

## Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Capacitive MEMS Pressure Sensor

```mermaid
graph LR
    PressureInput --> B1[Diaphragm Mechanical Domain]
    B1 -- Deflection x --> B2[Capacitance Change Delta C]
    B2 -- C changes with x --> B3[Signal Conditioning ASIC]
    B3 -- Voltage Output --> B4[Digital Calibrated Output]
    subgraph Physics_Layer
        B1
        B2
    end
    subgraph Interface_Layer
        B3
        B4
    end
```

---

## Diagram 4 — Coupling Matrix of Engineering Science Domains

```mermaid
graph TB
    MECH[MECHANICAL: Stress, Strain, Force] -- Coupling --> ELEC[ELECTRICAL: V, I, Q, E]
    MECH -- Coupling --> THERM[THERMAL: T, Heat Flow]
    MECH -- Coupling --> MAG[MAGNETIC: B, H]
    ELEC -- Coupling --> THERM
    ELEC -- Coupling --> MAG
    THERM -- Coupling --> MAG
    style MECH fill:#E0F2FF,stroke:#000
    style ELEC fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#000
    style THERM fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#000
    style MAG fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#000
```

---

## Diagram 5 — Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Physical Effect for a MEMS Sensor

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Need: Convert Physical Quantity to Electrical Signal] --> Q1{Quantity Type?}
    Q1 -- Pressure / Force --> P1[Diaphragm + Piezoresistive]
    Q1 -- Pressure / Force --> P2[Diaphragm + Capacitive]
    Q1 -- Acceleration --> P3[Proof Mass + Capacitive]
    Q1 -- Acceleration --> P4[Proof Mass + Piezoresistive]
    Q1 -- Magnetic Field --> P5[Hall / Lorentz / Magnetostrictive]
    Q1 -- Temperature --> P6[Thermocouple / Seebeck]
    Q1 -- Acoustic / Vibration --> P7[Piezoelectric PMUT]
    Q1 -- Light --> P8[Photoelectric / Photodiode]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the piezoelectric effect. Distinguish between the direct and converse effects with one MEMS application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The **piezoelectric effect** is the linear electromechanical coupling in non-centrosymmetric crystals where mechanical stress produces electric charge (and vice versa).

- **Direct effect** (mechanical → electrical): Application of stress `T` produces electric displacement `D = dT`. *Example:* piezoelectric microphone / vibration sensor.
- **Converse effect** (electrical → mechanical): Application of electric field `E` produces strain `S = dE`. *Example:* piezoelectric inkjet printhead actuator or PMUT ultrasound transmitter.

*(Word count target: 50–80 words; key terms highlighted in bold.)*

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State Coulomb's law and explain why electrostatic actuation is preferred over electromagnetic actuation in many MEMS devices.**

**Model Answer:**

Coulomb's law:

$$F = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$$

**Reasons for preferring electrostatic actuation in MEMS:**
1. **Force scales as 1/d²** → at micron gaps, huge force densities are achievable with low voltages (< 30 V).
2. **No need for on-chip magnets/coils** → simpler fabrication, CMOS compatible.
3. **Lower power dissipation** (essentially zero steady-state current).
4. **Fast response** suitable for RF switching and resonant sensors.

Limitations: small displacements, pull-in instability above `V_PI`.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Each Part B question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the electrostatic force between the plates of a parallel-plate capacitor and obtain the **pull-in voltage** for a movable plate suspended by a linear spring. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **capacitive MEMS pressure sensor**. Show how the diaphragm deflection changes the capacitance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Derivation:**

Starting energy stored in capacitor:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d}$$

(assuming `ε_r = 1` for simplicity)

Force = negative gradient of energy:

$$F = -\frac{dU}{dd} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2}$$

For a movable plate suspended by spring `k`, equilibrium at deflection `x`:

$$k x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 (d_0 - x)^2}$$

Solving for V as a function of normalized deflection `y = x / d_0`:

$$V(y) = \sqrt{\frac{2 k d_0^3}{\varepsilon_0 A}} \cdot \sqrt{y (1-y)^2}$$

Maximize: `dV²/dy = 0` → `y = 1/3`

$$\boxed{V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}}$$

**Interpretation:** Above `V_PI`, no stable equilibrium exists and the plate snaps to the substrate. Designers must operate below this voltage (typical safety factor: 30–40% below `V_PI`).

**(b) — Capacitive Pressure Sensor:**

Block diagram:
**Pressure P → Diaphragm deflection x(d) → Capacitance change ΔC → Signal conditioning ASIC → Voltage output V_out.**

The diaphragm (typically polysilicon or metal) deflects under pressure. The gap between diaphragm and fixed back-plate changes from `d_0` to `d_0 − x`, and capacitance becomes:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - x}$$

Sensitivity:

$$S_C = \frac{\partial C / \partial P}{C} \propto \frac{1}{(d_0 - x)^2}$$

A differential pair configuration is typically used to cancel common-mode noise.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **piezoelectric effect** with constitutive equations. Define the coefficients `d`, `s^E`, and `ε^T`, and state two MEMS applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **Seebeck voltage** generated by a thermocouple. How is the Peltier effect related to the Seebeck effect? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Piezoelectric constitutive equations:**

Direct effect:
$$D = d\, T + \varepsilon^T E$$

Converse effect:
$$S = s^E T + d\, E$$

Where:
- `d` (m/V or C/N): piezoelectric charge coefficient — quantifies charge per unit force (direct) or strain per unit field (converse).
- `s^E` (Pa⁻¹): elastic compliance under constant electric field.
- `ε^T` (F/m): permittivity under constant stress.

**Two MEMS applications:**
1. **PMUT (Piezoelectric Micromachined Ultrasonic Transducer)** — used in medical imaging and fingerprint sensors (e.g., Qualcomm under-display).
2. **FBAR (Film Bulk Acoustic Resonator)** filters — used in RF front-ends of mobile phones.

**(b) — Seebeck voltage derivation:**

For two dissimilar metals A and B joined at junctions `T_h` and `T_c`:

$$V_{seebeck} = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} [\alpha_A(T) - \alpha_B(T)] \, dT \approx \alpha_{ab}\, \Delta T$$

**Kelvin relation linking Peltier and Seebeck:**
$$\Pi_{ab} = \alpha_{ab} \cdot T$$

This means a good Seebeck material (high `α`) is also a good Peltier material (efficient for cooling or heating). The Thomson coefficient is:
$$\tau = T \frac{d\alpha_{ab}}{dT}$$

These relations are essential for designing **thermoelectric microsensors** and **on-chip micro-Peltier coolers**.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ½ factor** in the electrostatic force equation: many students write `F = ε₀AV²/d²` instead of the correct `½ ε₀AV²/d²`. The half comes from energy differentiation.
> 2. **Confusing piezoelectric (`d`) and piezoresistive (`π`) coefficients** — they look similar but have different units (m/V vs Pa⁻¹) and different physics.
> 3. **Mixing up Seebeck and Peltier directions** — Seebeck converts ΔT → V; Peltier converts I → Q.
> 4. **Not specifying the sign convention** when using `F = −∂U/∂x` — examiners award marks for correctly stating that attractive force is positive in MEMS pull-in problems.
> 5. **Skipping the units** in final answers — KTU strictly deducts marks if units are absent in numerical answers.
> 6. **Not drawing the diagram** for capacitive sensor / piezoelectric effect questions — a 1-mark penalty is commonly applied.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Electrostatic force on a parallel-plate capacitor:** `F = ½ ε₀ε_r A V² / d²`
- [ ] **Pull-in voltage:** `V_PI = √(8 k d₀³ / 27 ε₀ A)` — plate snaps at `d₀/3` deflection
- [ ] **Capacitance of parallel plate:** `C = ε₀ε_r A / d` — increases as plates approach
- [ ] **Lorentz force:** `F = qE + qv × B` — electric part + magnetic cross product
- [ ] **Direct piezoelectric:** `D = dT + ε^T E`
- [ ] **Converse piezoelectric:** `S = s^E T + dE`
- [ ] **Piezoresistive:** `Δρ/ρ = πσ` — Si has π ~ 100× metals
- [ ] **Magnetostrictive:** `λ = cH`
- [ ] **Seebeck:** `V = α ΔT`
- [ ] **Peltier:** `Q = Π I` ; **Thomson:** `Q = τ I ΔT`
- [ ] **Kelvin relation:** `Π = αT` ; `τ = T dα/dT`
- [ ] **Bohr radius (n=1):** `r₁ ≈ 0.529 Å`
- [ ] **Thermionic (Richardson–Dushman):** `J = A_R T² exp(−Φ/k_B T)`
- [ ] **Photoelectric:** `K_max = hν − Φ`
- [ ] **Field emission (Fowler–Nordheim):** `J ∝ E² exp(−BΦ^{3/2}/E)` — tunneling
- [ ] **Electret** = permanently polarized dielectric (analogous to permanent magnet)
- [ ] **Units to remember:** e = 1.602×10⁻¹⁹ C ; ε₀ = 8.854×10⁻¹² F/m ; h = 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s
- [ ] **Always specify material property values** for silicon: Young's modulus ≈ 130 GPa, density ≈ 2330 kg/m³, Poisson's ratio ≈ 0.28

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Engineering Science for Microsystem Design**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), Course Outcomes CO3, with Bloom's levels L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), and L4 (Analyze) emphasized.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Atomic Structure of Matter - Ions & Ionization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT742 - INTRODUCTION TO MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Atomic Structure of Matter - Ions & Ionization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Atomic Structure of Matter — The KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> The **Atomic Structure of Matter** describes the organization of an atom as a nucleus composed of positively charged **protons** and electrically neutral **neutrons**, surrounded by negatively charged **electrons** distributed in discrete quantized energy levels (shells and sub-shells) governed by quantum mechanical rules.

Every MEMS device is fabricated from matter whose macroscopic mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties emerge directly from this microscopic architecture. The atomic structure dictates:

- **Electrical conductivity** (electron availability in the conduction band)
- **Chemical reactivity** (valence electron configuration)
- **Mechanical stiffness** (inter-atomic bond strength)
- **Optical behavior** (photon-electron interaction energies)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Atomic Sub-Particles for KTU Exams:**
>
> | Particle | Symbol | Charge (C) | Mass (kg) | Location |
> |----------|--------|------------|-----------|----------|
> | Proton   | p⁺     | +1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ | 1.6726 × 10⁻²⁷ | Nucleus |
> | Neutron  | n⁰     | 0          | 1.6749 × 10⁻²⁷ | Nucleus |
> | Electron | e⁻     | −1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ | 9.1094 × 10⁻³¹ | Orbits |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Solar System of a Building"

Imagine an atom as a **high-rise office building under construction**:

- The **foundation and central pillars** (nucleus) hold the entire structure. They are massive, tightly packed, and contain the protons and neutrons. Removing the foundation = nuclear destruction.
- The **floors and rooms** (electron shells) are populated by workers (electrons) who move between offices. The top-most floor (valence shell) is the most important — workers there can be easily "poached" by neighboring companies (chemical bonding).
- The **ground floor** (K-shell, n = 1) has limited capacity (2 electrons), the **first floor** (L-shell, n = 2) holds up to 8, and so on.

> **Why this matters for MEMS:** When a MEMS engineer uses **plasma etching** or **ion beam milling**, they are essentially "shooting workers" (electrons) out of atoms to create charged particles (ions) that can precisely carve micron-scale features into silicon.

### 1.3 What is an Ion?

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition of Ion (KTU Standard):**
> An **ion** is an atom or a group of atoms (a molecule) that has acquired a net electrical charge by either **gaining** or **losing** one or more electrons from its neutral state.

The two families of ions are:

1. **Cation** — A *positively charged* ion formed when a neutral atom **loses** one or more valence electrons.
   - *Example:* Na → Na⁺ + e⁻
2. **Anion** — A *negatively charged* ion formed when a neutral atom **gains** one or more electrons into its valence shell.
   - *Example:* Cl + e⁻ → Cl⁻

### 1.4 What is Ionization?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition of Ionization (KTU 2024):**
> **Ionization** is the physical process by which a neutral atom or molecule acquires a net charge through the **removal** (or **addition**) of one or more electrons, requiring the input of a specific minimum quantity of energy called the **Ionization Energy** (IE) or **Ionization Potential (IP)**.

This process is *not* spontaneous for isolated atoms — energy must be supplied. The amount of energy required is a fundamental atomic property.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bohr Model Foundation (Quick Recap for KTU)

The **Bohr model (1913)** is the conceptual backbone for understanding ionization in introductory KTU modules. Although modern MEMS physics uses quantum mechanics, the Bohr model remains the syllabus standard.

**Bohr's Postulates (key points only):**

1. Electrons orbit the nucleus in **stationary, stable circular orbits** without radiating energy.
2. The **angular momentum** of the electron is quantized:
   $$L = m_e v r = n\hbar, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$
3. Electrons absorb or emit energy only when **transitioning** between orbits:
   $$\Delta E = h\nu = E_{final} - E_{initial}$$

### 2.2 Ionization Energy — The Energy Cost of Creating an Ion

The **First Ionization Energy (IE₁)** is the minimum energy required to remove the **most loosely bound (outermost) electron** from a neutral gaseous atom in its ground state.

**For a Hydrogen atom** (single electron, the cleanest textbook case):

$$E_n = -\frac{13.6 \text{ eV}}{n^2} = -\frac{2.18 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}}{n^2}$$

When the electron is completely removed ($n \to \infty$), the atom becomes a proton (H⁺ ion). The energy required is:

$$IE_1 = E_\infty - E_1 = 0 - (-13.6 \text{ eV}) = \textbf{13.6 eV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **13.6 eV** is the **First Ionization Energy of Hydrogen** — one of the most frequently tested numerical values in KTU board exams. Memorize it.

### 2.3 Successive Ionization Energies

For multi-electron atoms, removing successive electrons requires **dramatically more energy** because each removal pulls from a shell closer to the nucleus, which is held more tightly by the positive nuclear charge.

For a generic atom X with atomic number Z:

$$X^{+(q-1)} \rightarrow X^{+q} + e^- \quad \text{(requires } IE_q \text{)}$$

| Element | IE₁ (eV) | IE₂ (eV) | IE₃ (eV) |
|---------|----------|----------|----------|
| H       | 13.60    | —        | —        |
| He      | 24.59    | 54.42    | —        |
| Li      | 5.39     | 75.64    | 122.45   |
| Na      | 5.14     | 47.29    | 71.64    |

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice the **abrupt jump** between IE₁ and IE₂ for Lithium. This is because the first electron is a *valence* electron (loosely held), while the second is a *core* electron (deeply held). This jump is the experimental proof of shell structure — a fact that justifies quantum mechanical energy levels.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Bohr Radius (ground state) | $a_0 = \dfrac{4\pi\varepsilon_0\hbar^2}{m_e e^2} = 5.29 \times 10^{-11}$ m | metres | Foundational length scale in MEMS |
| 2 | Energy of nth Bohr Orbit | $E_n = -\dfrac{13.6 \text{ eV}}{n^2} \cdot Z^2$ | eV | Ionization energy of H-like species |
| 3 | First Ionization Energy of H | $IE_1 = +13.6$ eV $= 2.18 \times 10^{-18}$ J | eV / J | Benchmark value |
| 4 | de Broglie Wavelength (electron) | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{m_e v} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2m_e KE}}$ | m | e-beam lithography in MEMS |
| 5 | Photoelectric Threshold | $KE_{max} = h\nu - \phi$ | eV | Photo-ionization source design |
| 6 | Field Ionization Condition | $E_{field} \gtrsim 10^{10}$ V/m | V/m | Field-ion microscope (FIM) in MEMS inspection |
| 7 | Plasma Density Relation | $n_i = \sqrt{\dfrac{n_e \, kT_e}{\varepsilon_0}}$ | m⁻³ | Capacitively coupled plasma etchers |
| 8 | Mean Free Path of Ion | $\lambda_{mfp} = \dfrac{kT}{\sqrt{2}\pi d^2 P}$ | m | Sputtering yield calculations |

> **MEMS Context:** Every single one of these formulas has a direct counterpart in a MEMS fabrication tool — ion implanters, e-beam writers, plasma etchers, FIB systems, and FIM inspection rigs all depend on the physics encoded above.

### 2.5 Ionization Mechanisms (Classified for KTU)

There are **four primary ionization mechanisms** that KTU expects you to know:

1. **Thermal Ionization** — High-temperature collisions (used in plasma torches, combustion).
2. **Electron Impact Ionization** — Energetic electrons collide with atoms (basis of mass spectrometers and ICP plasma sources).
3. **Photoionization** — A photon with energy $\geq IE$ ejects an electron (used in excimer lasers for MEMS photolithography).
4. **Field Ionization** — An extremely strong electric field pulls an electron out of the atom (used in Field Ion Microscopes and atomically sharp emitter tips).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy of the n-th Bohr Orbit

**Goal:** Derive the expression for the energy of an electron in the n-th stationary orbit of a hydrogen-like atom (atomic number Z).

**Step 1 — Coulomb's Law provides the centripetal force:**

The attractive Coulomb force between the nucleus (charge +Ze) and the electron (charge −e) at radius r balances the centrifugal force of orbital motion:

$$\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Ze \cdot e}{r^2} = \frac{m_e v^2}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Quantization of Angular Momentum (Bohr's postulate):**

$$m_e v r = n\hbar, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the velocity v:**

From Step 2:

$$v = \frac{n\hbar}{m_e r}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute v into the force balance equation (Step 1):**

$$\frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} = \frac{m_e}{r} \cdot \left(\frac{n\hbar}{m_e r}\right)^2$$

**Step 5 — Simplify by cancelling one r and one m_e:**

$$\frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} = \frac{n^2 \hbar^2}{m_e r^3}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the orbital radius r:**

$$r_n = \frac{4\pi\varepsilon_0 n^2 \hbar^2}{m_e Z e^2}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute numerical values to get the Bohr radius a₀:**

For hydrogen (Z = 1, n = 1):

$$a_0 = \frac{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \hbar^2}{m_e e^2} = 5.29 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}$$

So the general radius is:

$$r_n = a_0 \cdot \frac{n^2}{Z}$$

**Step 8 — Compute the total energy E_n:**

Total energy = Kinetic Energy + Potential Energy:

$$E_n = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2 - \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r_n}$$

From Step 1, $\frac{1}{2}m_e v^2 = \frac{Ze^2}{8\pi\varepsilon_0 r_n}$, so:

$$E_n = \frac{Ze^2}{8\pi\varepsilon_0 r_n} - \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r_n} = -\frac{Ze^2}{8\pi\varepsilon_0 r_n}$$

**Step 9 — Substitute the expression for r_n:**

$$E_n = -\frac{Z e^2 \cdot m_e Z e^2}{8\pi\varepsilon_0 \cdot 4\pi\varepsilon_0 n^2 \hbar^2} = -\frac{m_e Z^2 e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2 n^2}$$

**Step 10 — Evaluate numerically for hydrogen (Z = 1):**

$$E_1 = -13.6 \text{ eV}, \quad E_n = -\frac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$$

> **This is the canonical KTU result.** The negative sign indicates a bound state. The first ionization energy is the positive magnitude of E₁.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — de Broglie Wavelength of an Accelerating Electron

**Goal:** Show that an electron accelerated through a potential difference V acquires a de Broglie wavelength suitable for MEMS electron-beam lithography.

**Step 1 — Work-Energy Theorem:**

An electron (charge e) accelerated through potential V gains kinetic energy:

$$KE = eV$$

**Step 2 — Express KE in terms of momentum p:**

$$KE = \frac{p^2}{2m_e} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = \sqrt{2 m_e e V}$$

**Step 3 — Apply de Broglie's relation:**

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values ($h$, $m_e$, $e$):**

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ nm}$$

For V = 100 V:

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{10} = 0.1227 \text{ nm} = 1.227 \text{ Å}$$

> **MEMS Significance:** This sub-nanometre wavelength is what enables e-beam lithography to write sub-100 nm features in advanced MEMS/NEMS devices.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Trend Analysis of Ionization Energy

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask **"Explain the trend in IE across a period or down a group."** Use this matrix to structure your answer.

| Factor | Effect on IE | Reason |
|--------|--------------|--------|
| ↑ Atomic number (across a period) | IE increases | Greater nuclear charge pulls electrons tighter |
| ↑ Atomic radius (down a group) | IE decreases | Outer electron is farther from nucleus; more shielding |
| Half-filled / fully-filled subshells | IE anomalously high | Extra stability (e.g., Be, N, Mg, P) |
| Higher effective nuclear charge ($Z_{eff}$) | IE increases | Stronger pull on valence electrons |
| Greater shielding by inner electrons | IE decreases | Inner electrons cancel nuclear attraction |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A doubly-ionized helium ion (He²⁺, Z = 2) is in its ground state. Find (a) the energy required to remove the remaining electron, and (b) the wavelength of the photon emitted if the ion relaxes from n = 2 to n = 1.

**Solution (a):**

For a He⁺ ion (Z = 2):

$$E_n = -\frac{13.6 \cdot Z^2}{n^2} = -\frac{13.6 \cdot 4}{1} = -54.4 \text{ eV}$$

Energy to remove the electron:

$$IE = 0 - (-54.4) = \textbf{54.4 eV}$$

**Solution (b):**

Photon energy:

$$E_{photon} = E_2 - E_1 = -\frac{13.6 \cdot 4}{4} - (-54.4) = -13.6 + 54.4 = 40.8 \text{ eV}$$

Wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E} = \frac{1240 \text{ eV·nm}}{40.8 \text{ eV}} = \textbf{30.4 nm}$$

> **Answer:** 54.4 eV and 30.4 nm (in the extreme ultraviolet, suitable for next-gen MEMS lithography).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Atomic Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[ATOM] --> B1[NUCLEUS]
    A1 --> B2[ELECTRON CLOUD]
    B1 --> C1[PROTONS positive charge]
    B1 --> C2[NEUTRONS neutral]
    B2 --> D1[K SHELL n=1 max 2 e]
    B2 --> D2[L SHELL n=2 max 8 e]
    B2 --> D3[M SHELL n=3 max 18 e]
    D1 --> E1[VALENCE ELECTRONS chemistry driver]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[IONIZATION lose or gain electron]
    F1 --> G1[CATION positive ion]
    F1 --> G2[ANION negative ion]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Ionization Mechanism Matrix

```mermaid
graph TD
    H1[NEUTRAL ATOM] --> I1[IONIZATION TRIGGER]
    I1 --> J1[THERMAL high temperature collisions]
    I1 --> J2[ELECTRON IMPACT energetic electron collision]
    I1 --> J3[PHOTOIONIZATION UV or X-ray photon]
    I1 --> J4[FIELD IONIZATION strong electric field]
    J1 --> K1[POSITIVE ION plus free electron]
    J2 --> K1
    J3 --> K1
    J4 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[MEMS APPLICATION PLASMA ETCHING]
    K1 --> L2[MEMS APPLICATION ION IMPLANTATION]
    K1 --> L3[MEMS APPLICATION MASS SPECTROMETRY]
    K1 --> L4[MEMS APPLICATION FIELD ION MICROSCOPY]
```

### 4.3 Flow Diagram — Energy Levels in Hydrogen Showing Ionization Limit

```mermaid
graph LR
    M1[GROUND STATE n=1 minus 13.6 eV] --> M2[FIRST EXCITED n=2 minus 3.4 eV]
    M2 --> M3[SECOND EXCITED n=3 minus 1.51 eV]
    M3 --> M4[THIRD EXCITED n=4 minus 0.85 eV]
    M4 --> M5[IONIZATION LIMIT n=infinity 0 eV]
    M5 --> M6[FREE ELECTRON 13.6 eV above ground]
    M1 -.emits photon.-> M2
    M2 -.emits photon.-> M3
    M3 -.emits photon.-> M4
    M4 -.emits photon.-> M5
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Role of Ions in a Typical MEMS Fabrication Line

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1[RAW SILICON WAFER] --> O1[ION IMPLANTATION doping]
    N1 --> O2[PLASMA ETCHING structuring]
    N1 --> O3[SPUTTER DEPOSITION film growth]
    O1 --> P1[Ar plus ions plus P plus ions plus B plus ions]
    O2 --> P2[CF4 plus SF6 plus Cl plus plasma]
    O3 --> P3[Ar plus ions for momentum transfer]
    P1 --> Q1[FINISHED MEMS DEVICE]
    P2 --> Q1
    P3 --> Q1
```

### 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram — Cation vs Anion

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1[NEUTRAL SODIUM Na 11 electrons] --> R2[LOSES 1 VALENCE ELECTRON]
    R2 --> R3[Na plus CATION 10 electrons net plus 1]
    R4[NEUTRAL CHLORINE Cl 17 electrons] --> R5[GAINS 1 ELECTRON]
    R5 --> R6[Cl minus ANION 18 electrons net minus 1]
    R3 --> R7[IONIC BOND NaCl TABLE SALT]
    R6 --> R7
    R7 --> S1[MEMS EXAMPLE SALT PARTICLES IN WET ETCH BATHS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid block diagrams instead of physical drawings?**
> The atomic-scale phenomena in this topic cannot be rendered accurately as physical drawings. The block diagrams above capture the functional relationships, energy transitions, and process flow that KTU examiners reward with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define ionization energy. Why is the second ionization energy of magnesium greater than its first?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Ionization energy (IE)** is the minimum amount of energy required to remove the most loosely bound electron from one mole of neutral gaseous atoms in their ground state.

> In magnesium (Mg, Z = 12), the electron configuration is 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s². The **first** IE removes a 3s valence electron, which is far from the nucleus and shielded by inner electrons. The **second** IE attempts to remove an electron from the stable, fully-filled 2p⁶ sub-shell, which is held tightly by the high effective nuclear charge. Hence, **IE₂ ≫ IE₁**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between an ion and a neutral atom with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer:**

> A **neutral atom** has an equal number of protons and electrons, resulting in zero net charge. An **ion** is an atom that has gained or lost one or more electrons, acquiring a net positive (cation) or negative (anion) charge. **Example:** A neutral sodium atom (Na, 11 p⁺, 11 e⁻) becomes a sodium cation (Na⁺, 11 p⁺, 10 e⁻) after losing one valence electron.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 model]**

**(a)** With the help of Bohr's postulates, derive an expression for the energy of an electron in the n-th orbit of a hydrogen atom. Hence calculate the first ionization energy of hydrogen in eV. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain any three ionization mechanisms relevant to MEMS fabrication processes. Give one engineering application for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — State the postulates:**
- Electrons move in stationary circular orbits of quantized angular momentum $L = m_e v r = n\hbar$.
- The centripetal force is provided by Coulomb attraction:
$$\frac{m_e v^2}{r} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{e^2}{r^2}$$

**Step 2 — Derive r_n:** Eliminating v between the two equations gives

$$r_n = \frac{n^2 \varepsilon_0 h^2}{\pi m_e e^2} = a_0 n^2$$

where $a_0 = 5.29 \times 10^{-11}$ m.

**Step 3 — Derive E_n:** Total energy is the sum of kinetic and potential energy:

$$E_n = -\frac{m_e e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2 n^2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate E₁ (numerical):**

$$E_1 = -13.6 \text{ eV}$$

**Step 5 — First ionization energy:**

$$IE_1 = 0 - E_1 = +13.6 \text{ eV}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

1. **Electron Impact Ionization** — Energetic electrons (typically 50–100 eV) collide with neutral gas atoms, knocking out bound electrons. *Application:* **Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP) etchers** used to etch deep silicon trenches in MEMS.
2. **Photoionization** — A photon with energy ≥ IE strikes the atom and ejects an electron. *Application:* **Excimer laser (193 nm ArF)** sources for stepper-based photolithography of MEMS structures.
3. **Field Ionization** — A field of $E \gtrsim 10^{10}$ V/m at a sharp tip pulls the electron out quantum-mechanically (tunnelling). *Application:* **Field Ion Microscope (FIM)** for atomic-scale imaging of MEMS probe tips.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 model]**

**(a)** Derive the de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through a potential difference V. What is the wavelength when V = 150 V? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the trends in ionization energy across period 2 and group 1 of the periodic table. Explain the role of ions in semiconductor doping for MEMS. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Work-Energy Theorem:** $KE = eV$

**Step 2 — Momentum:** $p = \sqrt{2 m_e e V}$

**Step 3 — de Broglie wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}} = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ nm}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate for V = 150 V:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{150}} = \frac{1.227}{12.247} = \textbf{0.1002 nm} \approx 1.0 \text{ Å}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

**Trend across Period 2 (Li → Ne):**
IE generally **increases** left to right due to increasing nuclear charge (Z = 3 → 10) with no significant increase in shielding. Anomalous dips occur at **Al** (3p¹, easier to remove than 3s² of Mg) and **S** (3p⁴, easier than 3p³ of P which is half-filled and stable).

**Trend down Group 1 (Li → Cs):**
IE **decreases** as atomic radius increases. The outermost electron is progressively farther from the nucleus and better shielded by inner shells, so less energy is required for removal.

**Role of ions in semiconductor doping for MEMS:**
Controlled ion implantation of donor ions (e.g., **P⁺, As⁺, Sb⁺**) or acceptor ions (e.g., **B⁺, Ga⁺, In⁺**) into silicon creates n-type or p-type regions. These doped regions form piezoresistors, electrostatic actuator electrodes, and active transistor channels in integrated MEMS devices. The ion's energy (typically 10–200 keV) determines its penetration depth via the Lindhard-Scharff-Schiøtt (LSS) model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of Energy:** Energy of a bound electron is **negative**. The IE is the **positive** magnitude. Many students write IE = −13.6 eV — instant 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Units:** Always express IE in **eV** (preferred) **or** J — not both mixed. 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J.
> 3. **Trend Justification:** A trend statement **without** a justification (Z_eff, shielding, radius) earns only half marks.
> 4. **Bohr vs Quantum:** For a KTU introductory question, the **Bohr model is acceptable**. Do not over-engineer with full Schrödinger wavefunctions unless the question explicitly asks.
> 5. **Ion vs Isotope:** KTU sometimes tests this. An **ion** differs in *electron count*; an **isotope** differs in *neutron count*. Do not confuse.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Atomic Structure of Matter: Ions & Ionization**

- **Atom composition:** Protons (Z, +e) + Neutrons (N, 0) in nucleus; Electrons (Z, −e) in shells.
- **Charge number = Atomic number Z = number of protons = number of electrons in neutral atom.**
- **Mass number A = Z + N.**
- **Cation:** Atom that **lost** electrons → positive charge. **Anion:** Atom that **gained** electrons → negative charge.
- **First Ionization Energy (IE₁):** Energy to remove the *first* (outermost) electron from a gaseous neutral atom.
- **IE of Hydrogen = 13.6 eV = 2.18 × 10⁻¹⁸ J.** Memorize.
- **Bohr radius $a_0$ = 5.29 × 10⁻¹¹ m.** The fundamental length scale in MEMS.
- **Successive IEs increase sharply** once a shell is depleted.
- **Four ionization mechanisms:** Thermal, Electron-impact, Photo-, Field.
- **MEMS applications:** Plasma etching, ion implantation, e-beam lithography, FIM imaging, sputtering.
- **de Broglie wavelength of an electron:** $\lambda = \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{V(\text{in volts})}}$ nm — used to size MEMS e-beam columns.
- **Photoelectric equation:** $KE_{max} = h\nu - \phi$ — the basis of photo-ionization in MEMS photon sources.
- **Periodic trends:** IE **increases** across a period, **decreases** down a group.
- **Anomalies** at half-filled and fully-filled sub-shells due to extra exchange stability.
- **Always remember:** Negative energy = bound state; IE = positive energy needed to free the electron.
- **Conversion check:** 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J; 1 nm = 10⁻⁹ m; 1 Å = 10⁻¹⁰ m.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Molecular Theory of Matter & Intermolecular Forces - Doping of Semiconductors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT742 - INTRODUCTION TO MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Molecular Theory of Matter & Intermolecular Forces - Doping of Semiconductors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Molecular Theory of Matter — Formal Definition

The **Molecular Theory of Matter** postulates that all macroscopic substances are composed of extremely small, discrete particles called **molecules**, which are in constant, random motion. The observable macroscopic properties of matter (such as pressure, temperature, volume, elasticity, and phase) emerge from the collective behavior of these molecules and the forces acting between them.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For MEMS, this theory is the *philosophical foundation* — every micro-scale sensor and actuator ultimately relies on the predictable molecular and atomic behavior of silicon, polymers, and metals.

### Key Postulates of Molecular Theory of Matter
1. All matter consists of molecules.
2. Molecules are in continuous, random motion (kinetic energy ∝ absolute temperature).
3. Molecules attract each other when separated and repel when compressed (intermolecular forces).
4. The average kinetic energy of molecules determines the **temperature** of the substance.
5. Molecular collisions are perfectly elastic in an ideal gas approximation.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English
Imagine a packed Mumbai local train at peak hour. Each passenger is a "molecule." When the train is empty (low density), passengers move freely — this is a **gas**. When the train is half-full with some clustering, it resembles a **liquid**. When the train is so packed that no one can move, it is like a **solid**. The "coaches" themselves represent the intermolecular bonds (strong in solids, weak in gases). Apply heat → people wiggle and move more → phase change occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** **Avogadro's Number** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ molecules/mole is the universal bridge between microscopic and macroscopic worlds.

---

## 1.2 Intermolecular Forces (IMFs) — Formal Definition

**Intermolecular Forces** are the electrostatic forces of attraction or repulsion that act *between* neighboring molecules (as opposed to **intramolecular forces** which hold atoms *within* a molecule together). They determine physical properties such as boiling point, melting point, viscosity, surface tension, and solubility.

### Classification of Intermolecular Forces

| Force Type | Relative Strength | Origin | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ion–Dipole** | Strongest (~50–200 kJ/mol) | Ion + polar molecule | Na⁺ in H₂O |
| **Hydrogen Bonding** | Very Strong (~10–40 kJ/mol) | H bonded to F/O/N + lone pair | H₂O, DNA |
| **Dipole–Dipole (Keesom)** | Moderate (~5–20 kJ/mol) | Two permanent dipoles | HCl, CHCl₃ |
| **London Dispersion (van der Waals)** | Weakest (~0.5–40 kJ/mol) | Induced temporary dipoles | Noble gases, O₂, N₂ |
| **Metallic Bonding** | Variable (very strong) | Delocalized electron sea | Cu, Al, Au |

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English
Think of intermolecular forces like **friendship strength in a classroom**:
- **London Dispersion** = two shy kids who barely notice each other (weakest).
- **Dipole-Dipole** = two kids who actively chat because one always carries a magnet (polar).
- **Hydrogen Bond** = two best friends glued at the hip.
- **Ion-Dipole** = a popular kid with a magnetic personality drawing everyone in.
- **Metallic Bond** = the entire class sharing one giant pool of lunch money (sea of electrons).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For MEMS Devices:** Polymers used as structural layers (e.g., SU-8, PDMS) rely heavily on **van der Waals** and **hydrogen bonding** to determine their Young's modulus, elasticity, and adhesion to silicon substrates.

---

## 1.3 Doping of Semiconductors — Formal Definition

**Doping** is the deliberate, controlled introduction of specific impurity atoms (called **dopants**) into an intrinsic semiconductor crystal lattice to *modulate* its electrical conductivity by several orders of magnitude. The resulting semiconductor is called an **extrinsic semiconductor**.

### Types of Doping

- **n-type doping**: Add Group V elements (P, As, Sb) into Si/Ge → contributes free **electrons** as majority carriers.
- **p-type doping**: Add Group III elements (B, Ga, In) into Si/Ge → creates **holes** (absence of electrons) as majority carriers.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English
Imagine a perfectly clean classroom with exactly 30 students (intrinsic semiconductor — every seat is occupied, no movement). Now:
- **n-type**: Invite one extra student (P, As, Sb) from outside who has no designated seat → he walks around freely (free electron = majority carrier).
- **p-type**: Remove one student, leaving an empty chair (B, Ga, In with only 3 valence electrons cannot fill the 4th seat) → that empty chair is a "hole," and students shuffle around to fill it (hole = majority carrier).

The "extra free movement" is exactly the **increased electrical conductivity** we want in MEMS sensors, transistors, and piezoresistors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Dopant Concentration in MEMS:** $10^{14}$ to $10^{21}$ atoms/cm³ — orders of magnitude lower than host atoms ($5 \times 10^{22}$ atoms/cm³ for Si).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Molecular Theory — Detailed Logic Breakdown

### 2.1.1 The Three States of Matter — Molecular View

| State | Molecular Spacing | Molecular Motion | Compressibility | Shape/Volume |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Solid** | Tightly packed ($d \sim 10^{-10}$ m) | Vibrational only | Negligible | Fixed shape, fixed volume |
| **Liquid** | Loosely packed | Vibrational + rotational + translational | Very low | Variable shape, fixed volume |
| **Gas** | Far apart ($d \sim 10^{-7}$ m) | Free, random, high-speed | High | Variable shape & volume |

### 2.1.2 The Kinetic Theory Equation of an Ideal Gas

The foundational relationship between molecular motion and macroscopic observables:

$$
PV = \frac{1}{3} N m \overline{c^2}
$$

Where:
- $P$ = Pressure (Pa or N/m²)
- $V$ = Volume (m³)
- $N$ = Total number of molecules
- $m$ = Mass of one molecule (kg)
- $\overline{c^2}$ = Mean square molecular speed (m²/s²)

The **root mean square speed** is then:

$$
c_{rms} = \sqrt{\overline{c^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{3k_B T}{m}}
$$

Where $k_B$ is **Boltzmann's constant** = $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

### 2.1.3 Why This Matters for MEMS
Micromechanical pressure sensors and gas-flow sensors at micro-scale still obey gas kinetic theory. The **mean free path** $\lambda$ of gas molecules inside a MEMS cavity determines whether flow is **continuum** (Navier-Stokes valid) or **molecular** (free-molecular regime):

$$
\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, d^2 \, P}
$$

Where $d$ = molecular diameter, $P$ = pressure. This dictates the design of **MEMS Pirani gauges** and **Knudsen pumps**.

---

## 2.2 Intermolecular Forces — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 London Dispersion Force (van der Waals)
The weakest IMF, present in *all* molecules (polar and non-polar). Originates from instantaneous, fluctuating dipoles caused by momentary asymmetric electron distributions.

$$
F_{London} \propto \frac{\alpha_1 \alpha_2}{r^7}
$$

Where $\alpha$ = polarizability, $r$ = intermolecular separation.

**Why it matters:** Explains why noble gases liquefy under high pressure, why long hydrocarbon chains have higher boiling points, and crucially — governs **stiction** in MEMS (where microstructures adhere to substrates due to surface van der Waals attraction).

### 2.2.2 Dipole-Dipole (Keesom) Force
Acts between molecules with **permanent dipoles** (e.g., HCl, CO). Magnitude depends on dipole moment $\mu$:

$$
U_{dipole-dipole} \propto -\frac{\mu_1 \mu_2}{r^3}
$$

### 2.2.3 Hydrogen Bonding
Special, strong dipole-dipole interaction when H is bonded to highly electronegative atoms (**F, O, N**). Critical in:
- Water's anomalous high boiling point
- DNA double-helix stability
- Protein secondary structure

For MEMS biomolecular sensors and lab-on-chip devices, hydrogen bonding governs **bio-functionalization** of surfaces.

### 2.2.4 Lennard-Jones Potential
The standard mathematical model capturing both attractive (van der Waals) and repulsive (Pauli exclusion) forces:

$$
U(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]
$$

Where $\varepsilon$ = depth of potential well (J), $\sigma$ = distance at which $U = 0$.

---

## 2.3 Doping of Semiconductors — Detailed Logic

### 2.3.1 Intrinsic vs Extrinsic Semiconductors

**Intrinsic (pure) semiconductor:**
- Pure Si or Ge crystal.
- Electron concentration = hole concentration = $n_i$.
- At $T = 300$ K, $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³ for Si.
- Fermi level lies exactly mid-gap.

**Extrinsic (doped) semiconductor:**
- Contains deliberate impurities.
- Either n-type or p-type.

### 2.3.2 n-type Doping (Group V donor in Si)

Donor atoms (P, As, Sb) have **5 valence electrons**. Four form covalent bonds with neighboring Si atoms; the **fifth electron** is loosely bound and becomes free at room temperature.

$$
\text{Dopant concentration } N_D \approx 10^{15} \text{ to } 10^{19} \text{ atoms/cm}^3
$$

Majority carriers: electrons; Minority carriers: holes.

Fermi level shifts **closer to the conduction band**:

$$
E_F = E_C - k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)
$$

### 2.3.3 p-type Doping (Group III acceptor in Si)

Acceptor atoms (B, Ga, In) have **3 valence electrons**. They create a "missing bond" — a **hole** — which acts as a positive charge carrier.

Majority carriers: holes; Minority carriers: electrons.

Fermi level shifts **closer to the valence band**:

$$
E_F = E_V + k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_V}{N_A}\right)
$$

### 2.3.4 Charge Neutrality Equation

$$
n_0 + N_A^{-} = p_0 + N_D^{+}
$$

For a fully ionized, non-degenerate extrinsic semiconductor:

$$
n_0 \approx N_D \quad \text{(n-type)}, \quad p_0 \approx N_A \quad \text{(p-type)}
$$

### 2.3.5 Law of Mass Action (always holds)

$$
n_0 \, p_0 = n_i^2
$$

This relation is the cornerstone for computing minority carrier concentrations.

### 2.3.6 Doping Methods in MEMS Fabrication

| Method | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Ion Implantation** | Accelerated dopant ions bombard Si | Precise shallow junctions |
| **Diffusion** | High-temperature dopant gas exposure | Deep, graded junctions |
| **Epitaxy (in-situ doping)** | Doped layer grown during CVD/MBE | Uniform thin films |
| **Modulation Doping** | Dopants in spacer layers only | HEMTs, heterostructures |

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kinetic Theory Equation of an Ideal Gas

**Goal:** Show that $PV = \frac{1}{3} N m \overline{c^2}$

### Step 1 — Setup
Consider $N$ molecules of an ideal gas enclosed in a cube of side $L$ and volume $V = L^3$. Each molecule has mass $m$ and moves with velocity components $(c_x, c_y, c_z)$.

### Step 2 — Momentum Change on Wall Collision
When a molecule strikes a wall perpendicular to the x-axis elastically, its x-component of velocity reverses: $c_x \to -c_x$. The change in momentum (per collision) is:

$$
\Delta p_x = m(-c_x) - m(c_x) = -2 m c_x
$$

### Step 3 — Time Between Collisions
The molecule travels to the opposite wall and back over distance $2L$, so the time between successive collisions on the same wall is:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2L}{c_x}
$$

### Step 4 — Force Exerted by One Molecule
Force equals rate of change of momentum:

$$
F_{one} = \frac{\vert \Delta p_x \vert}{\Delta t} = \frac{2 m c_x}{2L / c_x} = \frac{m c_x^2}{L}
$$

### Step 5 — Force from All Molecules
Summing over all $N$ molecules striking the wall:

$$
F_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{m c_{x,i}^2}{L} = \frac{m}{L} \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{x,i}^2
$$

### Step 6 — Introduce Mean Square Velocity
Define the average over all molecules:

$$
\overline{c_x^2} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{x,i}^2
$$

Then:

$$
F_{total} = \frac{N m \overline{c_x^2}}{L}
$$

### Step 7 — Pressure Relation
Pressure = Force / Area = $F_{total} / L^2$:

$$
P = \frac{N m \overline{c_x^2}}{L^3} = \frac{N m \overline{c_x^2}}{V}
$$

### Step 8 — Use Isotropy of Velocity
For random motion: $\overline{c_x^2} = \overline{c_y^2} = \overline{c_z^2}$, and:

$$
\overline{c^2} = \overline{c_x^2} + \overline{c_y^2} + \overline{c_z^2} = 3 \overline{c_x^2}
$$

So: $\overline{c_x^2} = \frac{1}{3} \overline{c^2}$

### Step 9 — Final Result

$$
\boxed{PV = \frac{1}{3} N m \overline{c^2}}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** Pressure is a macroscopic manifestation of countless microscopic molecular collisions. Increasing temperature → higher $\overline{c^2}$ → higher pressure (Gay-Lussac's Law derived).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: RMS Speed from Ideal Gas Law

**Goal:** Derive $c_{rms} = \sqrt{3 k_B T / m}$

### Step 1 — Start from Ideal Gas Law

$$
PV = N k_B T
$$

### Step 2 — Equate with Kinetic Result

$$
N k_B T = \frac{1}{3} N m \overline{c^2}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\overline{c^2}$

$$
\overline{c^2} = \frac{3 k_B T}{m}
$$

### Step 4 — Take the Square Root

$$
c_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{3 k_B T}{m}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $N_2$ at $T = 300$ K, $m = 4.65 \times 10^{-26}$ kg:

$$
c_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{4.65 \times 10^{-26}}}
$$

$$
c_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1.243 \times 10^{-20}}{4.65 \times 10^{-26}}} = \sqrt{2.67 \times 10^{5}} \approx 517 \text{ m/s}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Minority Carrier Concentration in Doped Semiconductor

**Given:** Silicon doped with $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³ phosphorus atoms at $T = 300$ K. Intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³.

### Step 1 — Identify Majority Carrier
n-type, so majority carrier is electron: $n_0 \approx N_D = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³.

### Step 2 — Apply Law of Mass Action

$$
n_0 \cdot p_0 = n_i^2
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Minority Hole Concentration

$$
p_0 = \frac{n_i^2}{n_0} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}{10^{16}}
$$

$$
p_0 = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{20}}{10^{16}} = 2.25 \times 10^{4} \text{ cm}^{-3}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Fermi Level Position
Using $N_C = 2.8 \times 10^{19}$ cm⁻³ (for Si at 300 K):

$$
E_C - E_F = k_B T \ln\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)
$$

$$
E_C - E_F = 0.0259 \times \ln\left(\frac{2.8 \times 10^{19}}{10^{16}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(2800)
$$

$$
E_C - E_F = 0.0259 \times 7.94 = 0.206 \text{ eV}
$$

Since $E_g = 1.12$ eV for Si, $E_F$ lies **0.206 eV below the conduction band** — close to it, confirming n-type behavior.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Conductivity of Doped Silicon

**Given:** Si doped with $N_D = 10^{15}$ cm⁻³ donors. Electron mobility $\mu_n = 1350$ cm²/(V·s). Hole mobility $\mu_p = 480$ cm²/(V·s). $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³.

### Step 1 — Majority Carrier Density

$$
n_0 = N_D = 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}
$$

### Step 2 — Minority Carrier Density

$$
p_0 = \frac{n_i^2}{n_0} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{20}}{10^{15}} = 2.25 \times 10^{5} \text{ cm}^{-3}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare Carrier Contributions
Since $n_0 \gg p_0$, conductivity is dominated by electrons.

### Step 4 — Compute Conductivity

$$
\sigma = n_0 q \mu_n + p_0 q \mu_p
$$

$$
\sigma \approx (10^{15})(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1350) + (2.25 \times 10^{5})(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(480)
$$

$$
\sigma \approx 0.216 \text{ S/cm} + 1.73 \times 10^{-11} \text{ S/cm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma \approx 0.216 \text{ S/cm}}
$$

Compare with intrinsic Si: $\sigma_i = n_i q (\mu_n + \mu_p) \approx 4.43 \times 10^{-6}$ S/cm — **doping increases conductivity by a factor of $\sim 5 \times 10^{4}$**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Summary Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Intrinsic Si | n-type | p-type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Majority carriers | — | $n = p = n_i$ | electrons ($n_0$) | holes ($p_0$) |
| Minority carriers | — | — | holes ($p_0$) | electrons ($n_0$) |
| Fermi level position | $E_F$ | Mid-gap | Near $E_C$ | Near $E_V$ |
| Resistivity | $\rho$ | Very high ($\sim 2.3 \times 10^{5}$ Ω·cm) | Low | Low |
| Typical dopant | — | — | P, As, Sb | B, Ga, In |
| Majority concentration | — | $1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³ | $\approx N_D$ | $\approx N_A$ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define intermolecular forces. Distinguish between intramolecular and intermolecular forces with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Intermolecular forces are the electrostatic forces of attraction or repulsion acting between neighboring molecules. They are responsible for the physical properties of condensed matter such as boiling point, melting point, and surface tension.

| Feature | Intramolecular Forces | Intermolecular Forces |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Within a molecule (between atoms) | Between molecules |
| Strength | Very strong (100–1000 kJ/mol) | Weaker (0.5–50 kJ/mol) |
| Examples | Covalent bond in H₂, Ionic bond in NaCl | Hydrogen bonding in water, London dispersion in noble gases |
| Effect | Determines chemical identity | Determines physical state |

Intramolecular forces (e.g., O–H covalent bond inside a single H₂O molecule) hold atoms *together inside* the molecule. Intermolecular forces (e.g., hydrogen bonding *between* H₂O molecules) hold molecules *together* in bulk. Breaking intramolecular forces causes chemical change; breaking intermolecular forces causes phase change.

*(Marks distribution: Definition 1M, Distinction 1.5M, Example 0.5M)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is doping? Explain the formation of an n-type semiconductor with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

Doping is the process of introducing a small, controlled amount of impurity atoms (dopants) into an intrinsic semiconductor crystal to modify its electrical conductivity.

**Formation of n-type semiconductor:**
When a pentavalent impurity such as Phosphorus (P), Arsenic (As), or Antimony (Sb) is added to pure silicon (tetravalent), four of its five valence electrons form covalent bonds with neighboring Si atoms. The **fifth electron** is loosely bound and becomes a free conduction electron at room temperature. This creates an n-type semiconductor in which **electrons are the majority carriers** and holes are the minority carriers.

```
        Si           Si           Si
         \            |            /
          \           |           /
           Si ----- ( P ) ----- Si      ← extra free electron (e⁻)
          /           |           \
         /            |            \
        Si           Si           Si
```

Since each donor atom contributes one free electron, the electron concentration $n_0 \approx N_D$.

*(Marks distribution: Definition 1M, Explanation 1.5M, Diagram 0.5M)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme: Module 3 long-answer questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice**. You must answer **either Question A or Question B** in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Derive the kinetic theory expression for the pressure of an ideal gas. Show that the root mean square speed is given by** $c_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3k_BT}{m}}$ **(7 marks)**

**(b) What is doping? Explain p-type and n-type semiconductors with energy band diagrams. Calculate the conductivity of silicon doped with** $10^{16}$ **cm⁻³ phosphorus at 300 K.** Given: $\mu_n = 1350$ cm²/(V·s), $\mu_p = 480$ cm²/(V·s), $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Full Derivation (7 marks)**

Refer to Section 3.1 and 3.2 of these notes. The examiner expects the following sequential steps:

1. **Assumptions** of kinetic theory (1 mark):
   - Molecules are point masses in random motion.
   - Collisions are perfectly elastic.
   - No intermolecular forces except at collision.
   - Container dimensions >> molecular size.

2. **Momentum change** on wall collision: $\Delta p_x = -2 m c_x$ (1 mark)

3. **Force per molecule** + summing: $F = \frac{N m \overline{c_x^2}}{L}$ (1.5 marks)

4. **Pressure derivation**: $P = \frac{N m \overline{c_x^2}}{V}$ (1 mark)

5. **Isotropy argument**: $\overline{c_x^2} = \tfrac{1}{3} \overline{c^2}$ leading to $PV = \tfrac{1}{3} N m \overline{c^2}$ (1 mark)

6. **RMS derivation** from $PV = N k_B T$ leading to $c_{rms} = \sqrt{3 k_B T / m}$ (1.5 marks)

**Part (b) — Doping + Conductivity Calculation (7 marks)**

**Doping definition (1 mark):** Refer to Section 1.3.

**n-type band diagram (1.5 marks):**

```
    Ec ────────────────────
         |          |
         ↓ donor    |
         level      |
         (Ed)       |
                   |   ↑ Ef shifted up
                   |   |
    Ev ────────────|───|──────
                   ↑    ↑
                        Ef
```

Fermi level near conduction band; donor level $E_D$ just below $E_C$.

**p-type band diagram (1.5 marks):** Fermi level near valence band; acceptor level $E_A$ just above $E_V$.

**Conductivity calculation (3 marks):**

Step 1: $n_0 = N_D = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³
Step 2: $p_0 = n_i^2 / n_0 = (1.5 \times 10^{10})^2 / 10^{16} = 2.25 \times 10^4$ cm⁻³
Step 3: $\sigma = n_0 q \mu_n + p_0 q \mu_p \approx (10^{16})(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1350)$
$= 2.16$ S/cm

(Contribution from holes is negligible: $1.73 \times 10^{-11}$ S/cm)

**Final Answer:** $\sigma \approx 2.16$ S/cm

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) Explain in detail the various types of intermolecular forces. Discuss how Lennard-Jones potential models the interaction between two molecules.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the different methods of doping semiconductors used in MEMS fabrication. Explain how the Fermi level shifts in n-type and p-type materials.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Types of IMF + Lennard-Jones (7 marks)**

**Types of IMF (4 marks):**

1. **London Dispersion Forces (1 mark):** Weakest, present in all molecules; arise from instantaneous induced dipoles. Magnitude $\propto \alpha^2 / r^6$. Examples: noble gases, O₂, N₂.

2. **Dipole-Dipole (Keesom) Forces (1 mark):** Between permanent dipoles; magnitude $\propto \mu_1 \mu_2 / r^3$. Example: HCl.

3. **Hydrogen Bonding (1 mark):** Special strong dipole-dipole when H bonded to F, O, or N. Examples: H₂O, DNA. Energy: 10–40 kJ/mol.

4. **Ion-Dipole and Metallic Bonding (1 mark):** Ion-dipole in solutions; metallic bonding in metal lattices via delocalized electrons.

**Lennard-Jones Potential (3 marks):**

$$
U(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]
$$

- The $r^{-12}$ term models **short-range Pauli repulsion**.
- The $r^{-6}$ term models **long-range van der Waals attraction**.
- Equilibrium distance: $r_{eq} = \sigma \cdot 2^{1/6} \approx 1.122 \sigma$.
- Depth of potential well: $\varepsilon$ (energy to dissociate pair).

**Significance for MEMS (mention briefly):** Used in molecular dynamics simulations of polymer stiction, surface adhesion, and bio-MEMS functionalization.

**Part (b) — Doping Methods + Fermi Level (7 marks)**

**Doping Methods (4 marks):**

1. **Ion Implantation (1.5 marks):** Dopant ions accelerated (10–200 keV) and implanted into Si. Precise control of dose and depth; requires post-implantation annealing to repair lattice damage and activate dopants.

2. **Thermal Diffusion (1.5 marks):** Si wafer exposed to dopant vapor (e.g., POCl₃ for P) at 900–1100 °C. Dopants diffuse along concentration gradient. Used for deep junctions.

3. **In-situ Doping during Epitaxy (1 mark):** During CVD/MBE growth, dopant gas (e.g., diborane for B, phosphine for P) is introduced. Produces sharp, abrupt junctions. Used in HEMTs and SOI wafers.

**Fermi Level Shifts (3 marks):**

- **n-type:** $E_F = E_C - k_B T \ln(N_C / N_D)$. Shifts toward conduction band as $N_D$ increases.
- **p-type:** $E_F = E_V + k_B T \ln(N_V / N_A)$. Shifts toward valence band as $N_A$ increases.
- For **heavy doping** ($>10^{19}$ cm⁻³), $E_F$ enters the band → **degenerate semiconductor** (used in heavily doped contacts in MEMS).

---

> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Avoid These:**
>
> 1. **Confusing intermolecular with intramolecular forces** — leads to -2 marks immediately. Memorize: *intra* = inside one molecule; *inter* = between molecules.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners are strict. Always write "cm⁻³", "S/cm", "cm²/(V·s)", etc.
> 3. **Skipping the assumption list** in kinetic theory derivations — KTU wants the **postulates** listed before any derivation (lose 1 mark).
> 4. **Using $|x|$ in markdown answers** — Always use $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX or write "magnitude of $x$" in plain text.
> 5. **Drawing sloppy band diagrams** — A band diagram without labeled $E_C$, $E_V$, $E_F$ and arrows showing direction of shift is treated as incomplete (-1 mark).
> 6. **Neglecting minority carriers** in conductivity questions — Always show the *negligibility justification* using numerical comparison.
> 7. **Memorizing without understanding** — KTU frequently asks "explain why" or "what is the engineering significance" — these *require conceptual articulation*, not formula recall.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Print this section and revise 30 minutes before the exam — covers 90% of typical Module 3 questions.

- **Molecular Theory postulates:** discrete molecules, random motion, elastic collisions, temperature = average KE.
- **Kinetic Theory Equation:** $PV = \tfrac{1}{3} N m \overline{c^2}$ — derivable from momentum-flux argument.
- **RMS Speed:** $c_{rms} = \sqrt{3k_B T / m}$.
- **Mean Free Path:** $\lambda = k_B T / (\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 P)$ — important for MEMS vacuum/molecular flow regime.
- **IMF Hierarchy (weakest → strongest):** London dispersion < Dipole-Dipole < Hydrogen bond < Ion-dipole < Metallic (intra) < Ionic/Covalent (intra).
- **Lennard-Jones Potential:** $U(r) = 4\varepsilon[(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^6]$ — equilibrium at $r_{eq} = \sigma \cdot 2^{1/6}$.
- **Doping Definition:** Adding Group V (n-type) or Group III (p-type) impurities to Si/Ge.
- **Mass Action Law:** $n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$ — always true, regardless of doping.
- **Fermi level shift:** n-type → toward $E_C$; p-type → toward $E_V$.
- **Doping Methods:** Ion implantation (precise), Diffusion (deep), Epitaxy (sharp).
- **Typical Values to Memorize:**
  - $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹
  - $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K = $8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K
  - $n_i$(Si, 300 K) = $1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³
  - $E_g$(Si, 300 K) = $1.12$ eV
  - $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- **Conductivity:** $\sigma = n q \mu_n + p q \mu_p$ — always check which term dominates.
- **MEMS relevance:** Stiction (van der Waals), biofunctionalization (H-bond), piezoresistors (doping).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusion Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT742 - INTRODUCTION TO MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Diffusion Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) and semiconductor fabrication, the **Diffusion Process** refers to the controlled, thermally activated migration of dopant atoms (such as Boron, Phosphorus, or Arsenic) from a region of high concentration into the host semiconductor lattice (typically Silicon, or in MEMS contexts, polysilicon, SOI, or glass substrates) at elevated temperatures.

The process is governed by two fundamental transport equations discovered by Adolf Fick in **1855**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Fick's First Law of Diffusion (Steady State):**
> The flux of particles $J$ per unit area per unit time is proportional to the concentration gradient $\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$, with proportionality constant being the **diffusion coefficient** $D$.

$$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion (Time Dependent):**
> The rate of change of concentration at a point equals the divergence of the flux, yielding the diffusion equation. In 1-D:
> $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$
> 
> In 3-D (vector form):
> $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \nabla^2 C$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine dropping a single **drop of red ink** into a still glass of clear water. The ink does not stay as a blob; instead, the red color slowly spreads until the entire glass appears pale pink.

**Why does this happen?** The ink molecules are randomly colliding with water molecules. Statistically, more ink molecules move from regions of high concentration (where the drop landed) to regions of low concentration (the rest of the glass) than the reverse.

Now imagine the ink drop is "stuck" at the surface of a silicon wafer, and the wafer is heated to **1000 °C** inside a furnace. The same physical principle applies, but the ink atoms (dopants) move into the solid silicon crystal lattice instead of water. The higher the temperature, the more vigorous the vibration of the lattice, allowing dopant atoms to hop from one lattice site to another — this is the **diffusion process** used to form p-n junctions in MEMS and IC fabrication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Mapping:**
> - Ink drop $\rightarrow$ **Dopant source** (solid, liquid, or gas)
> - Glass of water $\rightarrow$ **Silicon wafer** (the diffusion medium)
> - Spreading color $\rightarrow$ **Dopant concentration profile** $C(x,t)$
> - Stirring / heating $\rightarrow$ **Thermal energy (Temperature $T$)** that activates diffusion

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following values are essential for MEMS / semiconductor diffusion calculations:

- **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{eV/K}$
- **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \ \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si at 300 K:** $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10} \ \text{cm}^{-3}$
- **Thermal oxidation reference temperature:** $T_{\text{ref}} \approx 1000 \ \text{°C}$
- **Solid solubility limit (e.g., P in Si at 1000 °C):** $C_s \approx 10^{21} \ \text{cm}^{-3}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Diffusion — Why Atoms Move

Diffusion in a crystalline solid like silicon occurs primarily via two atomic mechanisms:

1. **Substitutional Diffusion:** The dopant atom (e.g., B, P, As) occupies a lattice site that is momentarily vacant. Movement happens when it "hops" into a neighboring **vacancy** $V$. This requires a **vacancy formation energy** plus a **migration energy**.
2. **Interstitial Diffusion:** Small dopants (e.g., H, Li, Cu) squeeze through the spaces between lattice atoms. This is much faster than substitutional diffusion.

The temperature dependence of the diffusion coefficient $D$ follows the **Arrhenius relation**:

$$D = D_0 \ \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{kT}\right)$$

Where:
- $D_0$ = Pre-exponential (frequency) factor, in $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$
- $E_a$ = Activation energy in **eV**
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant
- $T$ = Absolute temperature in **Kelvin**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A rule of thumb: **Increasing the temperature by 10 °C roughly doubles the diffusion rate**, because of the exponential dependence on $1/T$.

## 2.2 Two Sequential Steps in IC/MEMS Diffusion

In a real semiconductor or MEMS fabrication line, dopant diffusion is performed in two stages:

### Step 1: Predeposition (Constant Surface Concentration)
The wafer is exposed to a **dopant-rich ambient** (gas, vapor, or solid source) at high temperature. The surface concentration $C_s$ is held constant at the **solid solubility limit**. The dopant diffuses *into* the wafer, and the total dose $Q$ increases with time.

**Boundary Conditions (BCs):**
- $C(0, t) = C_s$ (constant surface concentration)
- $C(\infty, t) = 0$ (semi-infinite wafer)
- $C(x, 0) = 0$ (initially undoped)

### Step 2: Drive-In (Limited Total Dose)
The external dopant source is removed. The wafer is reheated to redistribute the existing dose deeper into the wafer. The total dose $Q$ remains **constant** because no new dopants are added.

**Boundary Conditions (BCs):**
- $\left.\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right|_{x=0} = 0$ (no flux leaving the surface — sealed surface)
- $C(\infty, t) = 0$
- Initial condition: $C(x, 0) = f(x)$ from predeposition step

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables & Units | Typical Use |
|---|---------------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Fick's First Law | $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | $J$: flux ($\text{cm}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}$); $D$: diffusion coefficient ($\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$); $C$: concentration ($\text{cm}^{-3}$); $x$: position ($\text{cm}$) | Compute dopant flux at any depth |
| 2 | Fick's Second Law (1-D) | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | Same as above + $t$ in seconds | Time evolution of profile |
| 3 | Arrhenius Diffusion Coefficient | $D = D_0 \exp(-E_a / kT)$ | $D_0$ ($\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$), $E_a$ (eV), $T$ (K) | Determine $D$ at given $T$ |
| 4 | Predeposition Profile (erfc) | $C(x,t) = C_s \, \text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ | $C_s$: surface concentration; $\text{erfc}$: complementary error function | Dopant profile during predeposition |
| 5 | Total Dose (Predeposition) | $Q(t) = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \, C_s \sqrt{Dt}$ | $Q$ in $\text{cm}^{-2}$ | Total dopant atoms per unit area |
| 6 | Drive-In Profile (Gaussian) | $C(x,t) = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^2}{4Dt}\right)$ | $Q$ constant; $D, t$ for drive-in | Profile after drive-in |
| 7 | Junction Depth $x_j$ | $x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt} \, \text{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{C_B}{C_s}\right)$ | $C_B$: substrate background doping | Locate p-n junction |
| 8 | Sheet Resistance | $R_s = \dfrac{1}{q \mu_s \int_0^{x_j} C(x) \, dx}$ | $q$: electron charge ($1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C); $\mu_s$: sheet mobility | Used for MEMS resistor design |
| 9 | Solid Solubility Limit | $C_s = C_{s,\max}(T)$ (lookup table) | Empirical, $T$-dependent | Maximum achievable surface conc. |
| 10 | Mean Penetration Depth | $\bar{x} = \sqrt{\pi D t}$ | Length in $\text{cm}$ | Characteristic diffusion length |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Diffusion in MEMS Engineering

| Application | Diffusion Role |
|-------------|----------------|
| **p-n Junction Diodes** | Boron / Phosphorus diffusion forms anode / cathode regions. |
| **MEMS Piezoresistive Pressure Sensors** | Boron diffusion on silicon diaphragm creates strain gauges with high gauge factor. |
| **MEMS Capacitive Accelerometers** | Selective doping reduces parasitic resistance in capacitive readout electrodes. |
| **Solar Cells** | Phosphorus diffusion creates the n$^+$ emitter layer on a p-type Si wafer. |
| **CMOS Transistors** | Source/Drain and well formation rely on carefully controlled diffusion steps. |
| **Bio-MEMS / Lab-on-Chip** | Doped glass or doped silicon can be used as integrated heaters, sensors, or electrochemical electrodes. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In MEMS fabrication, diffusion is often replaced or supplemented by **ion implantation** because implantation provides **precise control** over the dopant dose and depth (via beam energy). However, **thermal annealing after implantation still uses diffusion** to electrically activate dopants and repair lattice damage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Fick's First Law to Fick's Second Law

We start with Fick's first law in 1-D:

$$J(x, t) = -D \, \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

We apply **conservation of mass** to an infinitesimal slab of the semiconductor of width $dx$ between $x$ and $x + dx$.

**Step 1: Flux entering the slab at $x$** is $J(x, t)$.
**Step 2: Flux leaving the slab at $x + dx$** can be expanded using a first-order Taylor series:

$$J(x + dx, t) \approx J(x, t) + \frac{\partial J}{\partial x} \, dx$$

**Step 3: Net rate of accumulation of dopant atoms in the slab:**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} \, dx = J(x,t) - J(x + dx, t) = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} \, dx$$

**Step 4: Cancel $dx$ on both sides:**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}$$

**Step 5: Substitute Fick's First Law into the divergence term:**

$$J = -D \, \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} \implies \frac{\partial J}{\partial x} = -D \, \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 6: For a constant diffusion coefficient $D$ (homogeneous medium), this becomes:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}}$$

> This is **Fick's Second Law** — the governing Partial Differential Equation (PDE) for the diffusion process. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Predeposition Solution (Constant Surface Concentration)

We solve Fick's Second Law with the following BCs:
- $C(0, t) = C_s$
- $C(\infty, t) = 0$
- $C(x, 0) = 0$

**Step 1: Use the Boltzmann substitution** $\eta = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$. With this substitution, the PDE transforms into an ODE:

$$\frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2} + 2\eta \, \frac{dC}{d\eta} = 0$$

**Step 2: Integrate once:**

$$\frac{dC}{d\eta} = A \, e^{-\eta^2}$$

**Step 3: Integrate again:**

$$C(\eta) = A \int_0^{\eta} e^{-u^2} du + B$$

**Step 4: Apply boundary conditions.** As $\eta \to \infty$, $C \to 0$, hence $A \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} + B = 0$. At $\eta = 0$, $C = C_s$, so $B = C_s$.

**Step 5: Solving for $A$ and $B$:**

$$B = C_s, \quad A = -\frac{2 C_s}{\sqrt{\pi}}$$

**Step 6: Substitute back. The Gaussian integral that appears is the complementary error function:**

$$\text{erfc}(\eta) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_{\eta}^{\infty} e^{-u^2} du$$

**Step 7: Final solution:**

$$\boxed{C(x, t) = C_s \, \text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)}$$

> This is the famous **erfc dopant profile** during predeposition diffusion. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation: Total Dose $Q$ Underneath the Surface (Predeposition)

**Step 1: The dose per unit area is defined as the integral of concentration over depth:**

$$Q(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty} C(x, t) \, dx$$

**Step 2: Substitute the erfc solution and use the substitution $u = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$, so $dx = 2\sqrt{Dt} \, du$:**

$$Q(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty} C_s \, \text{erfc}(u) \cdot 2\sqrt{Dt} \, du$$

**Step 3: Pull constants out and use the standard integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} \text{erfc}(u) \, du = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}}$:**

$$Q(t) = 2 C_s \sqrt{Dt} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}}$$

**Step 4: Final expression:**

$$\boxed{Q(t) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \, C_s \sqrt{Dt}}$$

> Note: $Q$ grows as $\sqrt{t}$ — diffusion slows down because the concentration gradient near the surface flattens with time. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation: Drive-In Solution (Limited Total Dose, Gaussian Profile)

After the source is removed, the BC becomes $\left.\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right|_{x=0} = 0$. The initial condition is the erfc profile from predeposition. The result for long drive-in times is:

$$\boxed{C(x, t) = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4Dt}\right)}$$

This is a **Gaussian profile** centered at the surface. Derivation outline:

**Step 1:** Use Laplace transforms on Fick's Second Law with the new BCs.
**Step 2:** The instantaneous source solution (delta function at $t=0$) is $C(x, t) = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^2}{4Dt}\right)$.
**Step 3:** Convolution of this with the predeposition initial profile yields the same Gaussian form for sufficiently long times.

> Drive-in diffusion is widely used in MEMS to create **deep, graded junctions** (e.g., for high-voltage piezoresistors). $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Junction Depth $x_j$

The p-n junction forms at the depth where the diffused dopant concentration equals the **background concentration** $C_B$ of the substrate.

### Case A: Predeposition (erfc profile)

$$C_s \, \text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x_j}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = C_B$$

Solving for $x_j$:

$$\boxed{x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt} \, \text{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\frac{C_B}{C_s}\right)}$$

### Case B: Drive-in (Gaussian profile)

$$\frac{Q}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x_j^2}{4Dt}\right) = C_B$$

Taking natural log and solving:

$$\boxed{x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt \, \ln\!\left(\frac{Q}{C_B \sqrt{\pi D t}}\right)}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A silicon wafer ($C_B = 10^{15} \ \text{cm}^{-3}$) is subjected to a phosphorus predeposition at $1000 \ \text{°C}$ for 1 hour. Given: $D = 3.85 \times 10^{-14} \ \text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ at 1000 °C, $C_s = 10^{21} \ \text{cm}^{-3}$. Find (a) $x_j$, (b) total dose $Q$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Compute the diffusion length:**

$$\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{3.85 \times 10^{-14} \ \text{cm}^2/\text{s} \times 3600 \ \text{s}} = \sqrt{1.386 \times 10^{-10}} \ \text{cm}$$

$$\sqrt{Dt} = 1.177 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{cm} = 0.1177 \ \mu\text{m}$$

**Compute the erfc argument:**

$$\frac{C_B}{C_s} = \frac{10^{15}}{10^{21}} = 10^{-6}$$

From erfc tables, $\text{erfc}^{-1}(10^{-6}) \approx 3.4$.

**Junction depth:**

$$x_j = 2 \times 1.177 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.4 = 8.0 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{cm} = 0.80 \ \mu\text{m}$$

**(b) Total dose:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \times 10^{21} \times 1.177 \times 10^{-5} = \frac{2}{1.772} \times 10^{21} \times 1.177 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$Q \approx 1.33 \times 10^{16} \ \text{cm}^{-2}$$

> Final answers: $x_j = 0.80 \ \mu\text{m}$, $Q = 1.33 \times 10^{16} \ \text{cm}^{-2}$. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Diffusion Furnace System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Gas Cabinet: N2 / O2 / Dopant Source] --> B[Mass Flow Controller MFC1]
    B --> C[Pre-heater Zone]
    C --> D[High Temperature Furnace 900-1200 C]
    D --> E[Wafer Boat with Si Wafers]
    E --> F[Drive-in or Predeposition Reaction]
    F --> G[Exhaust and Scrubber System]
    G --> H[Safety Interlock and Vent]
    D --> I[Thermocouple and PID Controller]
    I --> D
    F --> J[Cold Zone / Load Lock]
    J --> K[Output: Doped Wafer with C x t Profile]

    stepA[Dopant Source Solid Liquid Gas] --> C
    stepB[Quartz Liner Tube] --> D
    stepC[Profile Monitor SIMS or Spreading Resistance] --> K
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: From Source Atom to Active Junction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Dopant Source: Solid Liquid or Gas Phase] --> step2[Transport to Wafer Surface via Carrier Gas]
    step2 --> step3[Adsorption on SiO2 or Bare Si Surface]
    step3 --> step4[Surface Reaction: SiO2 Formation or Direct Incorporation]
    step4 --> step5[Bulk Diffusion: Ficks Second Law]
    step5 --> step6[Profile C x t Established]
    step6 --> step7[Annealing and Electrical Activation]
    step7 --> step8[Final: p-n Junction or MEMS Doped Region]

    nodeA[Predeposition Step erfc Profile] --> step5
    nodeB[Drive-in Step Gaussian Profile] --> step5
```

## 4.3 Diffusion Profile Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Predeposition Constant Surface Conc] --> P2[Profile: erfc function]
    P2 --> P3[Junction Depth via erfc inverse]

    Q1[Drive-in Limited Dose Q constant] --> Q2[Profile: Gaussian]
    Q2 --> Q3[Junction Depth via log relation]

    P3 --> R[Compare xj values]
    Q3 --> R
    R --> S[Choose based on MEMS device requirement]
```

## 4.4 Diffusion vs. Ion Implantation Comparison

| Aspect | Thermal Diffusion | Ion Implantation |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| Driving force | Concentration gradient | Kinetic beam energy |
| Direction | Isotropic (lattice-controlled) | Highly directional |
| Profile shape | erfc or Gaussian | Approximately Gaussian, with skew |
| Dose control | Indirect (via $C_s$, $t$) | Direct (current integration) |
| Depth control | Indirect (via $T$, $t$) | Direct (via beam voltage) |
| Damage | None | Severe lattice damage — needs anneal |
| Lateral spread | Yes (isotropic) | Minimal (directional) |
| MEMS use case | Doping thick structures, glass | Shallow, precise MEMS doping |

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram nodes above are deliberately labeled with raw uppercase alphanumeric text to ensure safe rendering in the Mermaid engine.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Define Fick's First and Second Laws of Diffusion and state the units of each parameter in the equations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Fick's First Law** states that the flux $J$ of diffusing particles per unit area per unit time is proportional to the concentration gradient:
$$J = -D \, \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

**Fick's Second Law** describes the time evolution of the concentration profile:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Units:**
