The result is a roughly **Gaussian distribution** of dopant atoms in depth: $$N(x) = \frac{\phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p} \exp\left[-\frac{(x - R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right]$$ ### Step 6: Annealing (Damage Repair) Implanted ions knock Si atoms off lattice sites, creating **disorder** (amorphous layer for high doses). A thermal anneal (rapid thermal anneal, RTA, at ~1000 °C for a few seconds) restores crystallinity and electrically activates dopants. > [!NOTE] > **Why ion implantation matters for MEMS:** It enables *selective-area doping* beneath moving structures (e.g., piezoresistors in cantilever beams) without high-temperature dopant spreading — critical for nanometer-scale precision. --- ## 2.2 Diffusion — Operational Breakdown ### Step 1: Dopant Introduction Two main approaches: - **Predeposition (constant source)**: Wafer exposed to a constant surface concentration $N_s$ (from a gas ambient or solid source). Total dopant increases with time. - **Drive-in (constant total dose)**: A fixed total amount $Q_0$ of dopant is redistributed inward by heat, with no additional dopant added. Surface concentration drops over time. ### Step 2: High-Temperature Furnace Wafer is loaded into a quartz furnace at 900 – 1200 °C. The dopant atoms move through the lattice. ### Step 3: Atomic Migration Mechanisms - **Vacancy mechanism**: Dopant on a substitutional site hops into an adjacent vacancy. - **Interstitial mechanism**: Small dopants (Li, H) squeeze through interstitial spaces. - **Interstitialcy (kick-out)**: A dopant atom is "kicked out" of a substitutional site by an interstitial Si self-atom. ### Step 4: Concentration Profile Evolution The profile broadens over time following Fick's laws, dictated by boundary conditions. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they cover 95% of KTU numerical and derivations on this topic.** | # | Equation | Meaning | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $N(x) = \dfrac{\phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p} \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(x - R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right]$ | Implanted Gaussian profile | $\phi$ = dose (cm⁻²) | | 2 | $Q = \int_0^{\infty} N(x)\,dx = \phi$ | Total implanted dose | ions/cm² | | 3 | $R_p = \int_0^{\infty} \dfrac{x\, S(E)}{S_n + S_e}\,dx$ | Projected range | cm | | 4 | $E = qV_{acc}$ | Ion kinetic energy | eV | | 5 | $J = -D\,\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x}$ | Fick's First Law | $J$ = flux (cm⁻² s⁻¹) | | 6 | $\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial t} = D\,\dfrac{\partial^2 N}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's Second Law | $D$ = cm²/s | | 7 | $D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{kT}\right)$ | Arrhenius diffusivity | $D_0$ pre-exponential (cm²/s), $E_a$ activation energy (eV) | | 8 | $N(x,t) = N_s\,\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ | Constant-source diffusion (predeposition) | $N_s$ = surface conc. | | 9 | $N(x,t) = \dfrac{Q_0}{\sqrt{\pi Dt}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^2}{4Dt}\right)$ | Constant-dose diffusion (drive-in) | $Q_0$ = dose (cm⁻²) | | 10 | $x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt}\,\mathrm{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{N_s}{N_{bg}}\right)$ | Junction depth (predeposition) | $N_{bg}$ = background | | 11 | $x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_s}{N_{bg}}\right)$ | Junction depth (drive-in, Gaussian) | — | | 12 | $2\sqrt{Dt}$ | Diffusion length $\vert L \vert$ | cm | > **Notation Note (KTU-friendly):** All absolute values or magnitudes are written as $\vert\cdot\vert$. Within formulas, divisions are by *stacked* fractions (e.g., $x/2\sqrt{Dt}$) — KTU scripts accept this form. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **MEMS Pressure Sensors**: Ion implantation forms piezoresistors at *exact depths* (e.g., 200 nm) in a diaphragm to maximize gauge factor. - **Silicon Photonic Waveguides**: MeV ion implantation is used to create *waveguides* by disorder-induced index change. - **CMOS Source/Drain**: Drive-in diffusion forms deep, graded junctions in older nodes; modern nodes use spike/RTA anneal after implantation. - **Solar Cells**: POCl₃ diffusion is the workhorse for n-type emitter formation. - **Buried Strain Layers**: High-dose implant + anneal creates *SiGe strain layers* that enhance carrier mobility in CMOS. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Gaussian Implanted Profile ### Starting Point: The Stopping Distribution When a monoenergetic ion enters a target, its path is randomized by many collisions. By the **Central Limit Theorem**, after many small, independent scattering events, the distribution of final rest positions in the direction normal to the surface (taken as the *x-axis*) is Gaussian. Let $R$ be the random variable for penetration depth. The mean is the **projected range** $R_p$, and the variance is $(\Delta R_p)^2$. Then: $$P(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\,\exp\!\left[-\frac{(x - R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right]$$ ### Multiplying by the Total Dose $\phi$ The total number of ions implanted per unit area is $\phi$. The concentration at depth $x$ (per unit volume) is $\phi \cdot P(x)$: $$N(x) = \phi \cdot P(x) = \frac{\phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\exp\!\left[-\frac{(x - R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right]$$ ### Verifying the Dose Conservation Integrate $N(x)$ over all depths: $$Q = \int_0^{\infty} N(x)\,dx$$ **Step-by-step expansion:** $$\int_0^{\infty} \frac{\phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\exp\!\left[-\frac{(x - R_p)^2}{2\Delta R_p^2}\right]dx$$ **Substitute** $u = \dfrac{x - R_p}{\sqrt{2}\,\Delta R_p}$, so $du = \dfrac{dx}{\sqrt{2}\,\Delta R_p}$, hence $dx = \sqrt{2}\,\Delta R_p\,du$: $$Q = \frac{\phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\cdot\sqrt{2}\,\Delta R_p \int_{-R_p/(\sqrt{2}\Delta R_p)}^{\infty} \exp(-u^2)\,du$$ Since $R_p \gg \Delta R_p$ (typically $R_p \sim 100$ nm, $\Delta R_p \sim 20$ nm), the lower limit tends to $-\infty$. The Gaussian integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-u^2}du = \sqrt{\pi}$: $$Q = \frac{\phi}{\sqrt{\pi}}\cdot\sqrt{\pi} = \phi \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering meaning:** $Q = \phi$ means that *all* implanted ions are accounted for — none are lost. This is essential for precise MEMS/CMOS doping control. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Fick's Second Law from Fick's First Law ### Fick's First Law (Empirical) $$J(x,t) = -D\,\frac{\partial N(x,t)}{\partial x}$$ ### Continuity Equation (Conservation of Atoms) In one dimension, the rate of change of concentration in an infinitesimal slice $dx$ equals the net flux entering minus leaving: $$\frac{\partial N}{\partial t}\,dx = J(x) - J(x+dx) = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}\,dx$$ So: $$\frac{\partial N}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}$$ ### Substituting Fick's First Law $$\frac{\partial N}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(-D\,\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right) = D\,\frac{\partial^2 N}{\partial x^2}$$ (assuming $D$ is position-independent) $$\boxed{\frac{\partial N}{\partial t} = D\,\frac{\partial^2 N}{\partial x^2}} \quad \blacksquare$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Constant-Source Diffusion Profile (Predeposition) ### Boundary & Initial Conditions $$N(0,t) = N_s \quad ; \quad N(\infty,t) = 0 \quad ; \quad N(x,0) = 0$$ ### Look for Similarity Solution Try $N(x,t) = f(\eta)$ where $\eta = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$. $$\frac{\partial N}{\partial t} = f'(\eta)\cdot\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} = f'(\eta)\cdot\left(-\frac{x}{4(Dt)^{3/2}}\right) = -\frac{\eta}{2t}\,f'(\eta)$$ $$\frac{\partial N}{\partial x} = f'(\eta)\cdot\frac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial^2 N}{\partial x^2} = f''(\eta)\cdot\frac{1}{4Dt}$$ ### Substitute into Fick's Second Law $$-\frac{\eta}{2t}\,f'(\eta) = D\cdot\frac{f''(\eta)}{4Dt} = \frac{f''(\eta)}{4t}$$ Multiply both sides by $4t$: $$-2\eta f'(\eta) = f''(\eta) \quad \Rightarrow \quad f''(\eta) + 2\eta f'(\eta) = 0$$ ### Integrate Once Let $g(\eta) = f'(\eta)$: $$g'(\eta) + 2\eta g(\eta) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dg}{g} = -2\eta\,d\eta$$ Integrate: $$\ln g = -\eta^2 + C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad g = A\,e^{-\eta^2}$$ ### Integrate Again $$f(\eta) = A\int_0^{\eta} e^{-u^2}du + C_2$$ ### Apply Boundary Conditions - $N(\infty,t) = 0 \Rightarrow f(\infty) = 0 \Rightarrow A\int_0^{\infty} e^{-u^2}du = 0$ ✓ (since $C_2$ is absorbed) - $N(0,t) = N_s \Rightarrow f(0) = N_s \Rightarrow C_2 = N_s$ and the integral from $0$ to $0$ is $0$. So: $$f(\eta) = N_s - A\int_0^{\eta} e^{-u^2}du = N_s\!\left[1 - \frac{A}{N_s}\int_0^{\eta} e^{-u^2}du\right]$$ But $\int_0^{\infty}e^{-u^2}du = \sqrt{\pi}/2$. The standard complementary error function is: $$\mathrm{erfc}(\eta) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_{\eta}^{\infty} e^{-u^2}du$$ Matching gives $A = \dfrac{2N_s}{\sqrt{\pi}}$. Therefore: $$\boxed{N(x,t) = N_s\,\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)} \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** At $x=0$, $N = N_s\,\mathrm{erfc}(0) = N_s$ ✓. As $x \to \infty$, $N \to 0$ ✓. The penetration depth scales as $\sqrt{Dt}$ — a hallmark of diffusion. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example: Junction Depth Calculation **Problem:** Boron is diffused into a silicon wafer at $1100\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ for $1$ hour with surface concentration $N_s = 10^{19}\,\mathrm{cm^{-3}}$. Given $D = 4\times 10^{-13}\,\mathrm{cm^2/s}$ and substrate doping $N_{bg} = 10^{15}\,\mathrm{cm^{-3}}$, find the junction depth $x_j$. ### Step 1: Compute diffusion length $$2\sqrt{Dt} = 2\sqrt{(4\times 10^{-13})(3600)} = 2\sqrt{1.44\times 10^{-9}}\,\mathrm{cm}$$ $$2\sqrt{Dt} = 2\times(3.794\times 10^{-5}) = 7.589\times 10^{-5}\,\mathrm{cm} = 0.7589\,\mu\mathrm{m}$$ ### Step 2: Apply junction-depth formula (predeposition) $$x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt}\cdot\mathrm{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\frac{N_{bg}}{N_s}\right)$$ $$\frac{N_{bg}}{N_s} = \frac{10^{15}}{10^{19}} = 10^{-4}$$ $$\mathrm{erfc}^{-1}(10^{-4}) \approx 2.751$$ ### Step 3: Compute $$x_j = 0.7589\,\mu\mathrm{m}\times 2.751 = 2.088\,\mu\mathrm{m}$$ **Result:** $x_j \approx 2.09\,\mu\mathrm{m}$ — well within typical MEMS piezoresistor depth ranges (0.1 – 5 µm). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Ion Implanter — Block-Level Functional Architecture This block diagram maps the functional flow of a typical **medium-current ion implanter** as used in MEMS fabs and CMOS lines. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ION SOURCE
BF3 PH3 As SOLID] --> B[EXTRACTION
ELECTRODE 30 kV] B --> C[MASS ANALYZER
MAGNETIC SECTOR] C --> D[ACCELERATOR TUBE
10 to 500 kV] D --> E[BEAM SHAPER
QUADRUPOLE LENSES] E --> F[ELECTROSTATIC
SCANNER X Y] F --> G[TARGET CHAMBER
WAFER ON CHUCK] G --> H[END STATION
BEAM STOP FARADAY] G --> I[DOSIMETRY
COUNTER] H --> I I --> J[CONTROL COMPUTER
FEEDBACK LOOP] J -.-> F J -.-> E ``` **Reading the diagram (left-to-right):** 1. **ION SOURCE** generates dopant ions (e.g., BF₃ → B⁺, F⁺, BF₂⁺). 2. **EXTRACTION** pulls positive ions out at ~30 kV. 3. **MASS ANALYZER** uses a magnetic field to select only the desired isotope. 4. **ACCELERATOR** boosts ions to final implant energy (decides $R_p$). 5. **BEAM SHAPER** collimates and focuses the beam. 6. **SCANNER** rasterizes the beam across the wafer for uniformity. 7. **TARGET CHAMBER** holds the wafer (often tilted 7° to avoid channeling). 8. **DOSIMETRY** measures current to compute $\phi$ in real time. 9. **FEEDBACK** ensures dose accuracy < 1%. --- ## 4.2 Diffusion Furnace Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[START WAFER
CLEAN Si SUBSTRATE] --> P2[NATIVE OXIDE
REMOVAL HF DIP] P2 --> P3[LOAD FURNACE
QUARTZ TUBE] P3 --> P4{PROCESS MODE} P4 -->|PREDISPOSITION| P5[SOURCE GAS ON
PH3 OR BBR3] P4 -->|DRIVE IN| P6[INERT AMBIENT
N2 OR AR] P5 --> P7[DIFFUSION AT
900 to 1200 C] P6 --> P7 P7 --> P8[RAMP DOWN
COOLING] P8 --> P9[UNLOAD WAFER
MEASURE PROFILE] P9 --> P10{DEPTH OK} P10 -->|YES| P11[NEXT PROCESS STEP] P10 -->|NO| P12[REDO OR RECIPE TUNE] ``` --- ## 4.3 Energy Loss Mechanism in Implantation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HIGH_E["HIGH ENERGY ION 100 keV to MeV"] E1[ELECTRONIC STOPPING SE
DOMINANT EXCITATIONS] end subgraph LOW_E["LOW ENERGY ION 10 to 100 keV"] E2[NUCLEAR STOPPING SN
DOMINANT COLLISIONS] end E1 --> X[ION ENTERS WAFER] X --> E2 E2 --> Y[FINAL REST POSITION RP] Y --> Z[GAUSSIAN DISTRIBUTION
WIDTH DELTA RP] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid:** Modern silicon-on-insulator (SOI) MEMS use *low-energy* implants (~5–30 keV) for shallow piezoresistors, exploiting nuclear stopping for tight, near-surface Gaussian profiles. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] **"What is ion implantation? List any two advantages over thermal diffusion."** **Model Answer (≈3-mark standard):** Ion implantation is a process in which dopant atoms are ionized, accelerated through a high voltage (10–500 keV), and directed onto a semiconductor substrate so that they penetrate and lodge inside the crystal lattice. The depth is controlled by the ion energy, and the dopant concentration is controlled by the integrated ion current (dose). **Two advantages over diffusion:** 1. **Precise dose control** — the total number of ions implanted ($\phi$ in ions/cm²) is measured directly by Faraday cups, giving accuracy to within 1%, whereas diffusion depends on gas flow, partial pressure, and time. 2. **Low-temperature / room-temperature process** — eliminates the lateral and vertical dopant spreading seen in high-temperature diffusion, enabling sub-micron, sharply defined doping profiles critical for MEMS piezoresistors. > [!NOTE] > A third advantage (process repeatability / non-equilibrium doping above solid solubility) earns a ½-mark bonus if the answer is *brief* and well-numbered. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **"Define the projected range $R_p$ and straggle $\Delta R_p$ in ion implantation. What does the ratio $R_p / \Delta R_p$ indicate?"** **Model Answer:** - **Projected range $R_p$** is the average perpendicular distance from the wafer surface to the position where implanted ions come to rest, measured along the direction of incidence. - **Straggle $\Delta R_p$** is the standard deviation of the Gaussian distribution of final ion positions around $R_p$. The ratio $R_p / \Delta R_p$ is a **figure of merit for profile sharpness**. A large ratio (e.g., 5–10) means a sharply peaked, well-defined implant; a small ratio (e.g., 1–2) means a broad, diffuse profile. For a 100 keV B⁺ implant in Si, $R_p \approx 300$ nm and $\Delta R_p \approx 70$ nm, giving a ratio ≈ 4.3. --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern > **KTU Internal-Choice Rule (2024 Scheme):** Both **Question A** and **Question B** carry 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Attempt *any one* full question. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choose this OR Question B) #### Q.A (a) [7 Marks] [KTU Model — 2024 Scheme] **"Derive the one-dimensional Fick's second law of diffusion starting from Fick's first law. State the assumptions used."** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. The medium is isotropic and homogeneous (valid for single-crystal Si in a specific crystal direction). 2. The diffusion coefficient $D$ is independent of position $x$ and concentration $N$ (constant-$D$ approximation). 3. One-dimensional diffusion along the $x$-axis. 4. No chemical reaction or trapping of diffusing species (pure Fickian diffusion). **Derivation:** **(i)** Fick's first law states that the flux $J$ of diffusing atoms is proportional to the concentration gradient: $$J(x,t) = -D\,\frac{\partial N(x,t)}{\partial x}$$ **(ii)** Conservation of matter in a slice of thickness $dx$ gives the continuity equation: $$\frac{\partial N}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}$$ **Substitute (i) into (ii):** $$\frac{\partial N}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(-D\,\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right) = D\,\frac{\partial^2 N}{\partial x^2}$$ (since $D$ is constant, it factors out of the second derivative). $$\boxed{\frac{\partial N}{\partial t} = D\,\frac{\partial^2 N}{\partial x^2}} \quad \text{— Fick's Second Law.} \quad \blacksquare$$ **Physical interpretation:** At any point, the time-rate of change of concentration equals the curvature of the concentration profile scaled by $D$. A positive curvature (concave up) means concentration is increasing with time (atoms accumulating). --- #### Q.A (b) [7 Marks] [KTU Model — 2024 Scheme] **"A silicon wafer is subjected to constant-source boron diffusion at 1100 °C. The surface concentration is $N_s = 5\times 10^{18}\,\mathrm{cm^{-3}}$ and the diffusion coefficient is $D = 4\times 10^{-13}\,\mathrm{cm^2/s}$. The substrate is p-type with $N_a = 10^{16}\,\mathrm{cm^{-3}}$. Find the junction depth $x_j$ after 1 hour of diffusion. What is the sheet resistance of the diffused layer if the average hole mobility in the diffused region is 200 cm²/V·s? (Use the standard integral, $\int_0^{x_j} N(x)\,dx = 2N_s\sqrt{Dt/\pi}$.)"** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Diffusion length** $$2\sqrt{Dt} = 2\sqrt{(4\times 10^{-13})(3600)} = 2\sqrt{1.44\times 10^{-9}}\,\mathrm{cm} = 2 \times 3.794\times 10^{-5} = 7.59\times 10^{-5}\,\mathrm{cm}$$ **Step 2: Junction depth (constant-source profile, $N(x_j) = N_a$)** $$\frac{N_a}{N_s} = \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x_j}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$ $$\frac{10^{16}}{5\times 10^{18}} = 2\times 10^{-3} = \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x_j}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$ From standard tables: $\mathrm{erfc}^{-1}(2\times 10^{-3}) \approx 2.186$. $$x_j = 2.186 \times 7.59\times 10^{-5} = 1.66\times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{cm} = 1.66\,\mu\mathrm{m}$$ **Step 3: Sheet carrier density (per cm²)** using the given integral: $$N_s^{\mathrm{sheet}} = \int_0^{x_j} N(x)\,dx = 2N_s\sqrt{\frac{Dt}{\pi}}$$ $$\sqrt{\frac{Dt}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.44\times 10^{-9}}{\pi}} = 2.139\times 10^{-5}\,\mathrm{cm}$$ $$N_s^{\mathrm{sheet}} = 2\times 5\times 10^{18}\times 2.139\times 10^{-5} = 2.14\times 10^{14}\,\mathrm{cm^{-2}}$$ **Step 4: Sheet conductivity and sheet resistance** $$\sigma_s = q\,\mu_p\,N_s^{\mathrm{sheet}} = (1.6\times 10^{-19})(200)(2.14\times 10^{14})$$ $$\sigma_s = 6.85\times 10^{-3}\,(\Omega)^{-1}$$ $$R_s = \frac{1}{\sigma_s} = \frac{1}{6.85\times 10^{-3}} \approx 146\,\Omega/\square$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification check:** Sheet resistance in the 100 – 1000 Ω/□ range is typical for diffused piezoresistors used in MEMS pressure sensors — the result is physically reasonable. **Final Answers:** $x_j = 1.66\,\mu\mathrm{m}$ ; $R_s \approx 146\,\Omega/\square$. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative to Question A) #### Q.B (a) [7 Marks] [KTU Model — 2024 Scheme] **"With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an ion implanter. Discuss the role of the mass analyzer and the post-implantation annealing step."** **Model Solution Outline:** **Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 above for the full Mermaid schematic). The standard functional flow is: **Ion Source → Extraction Electrode → Mass Analyzer (Magnetic Sector) → Accelerator Tube → Beam Focusing Lenses → Electrostatic/Mechanic Scanner → Target Chamber (Wafer on Chuck) → End Station (Faraday Cup) → Dosimetry & Computer Control.** **Working Principle:** 1. **Ion Source:** Dopant gas (e.g., BF₃, AsH₃) is fed into a chamber where a hot filament or plasma (Freeman / Bernas type) ionizes the gas, producing a mixture of ionic species (B⁺, BF₂⁺, F⁺, etc.). 2. **Extraction:** A negatively biased electrode pulls positive ions out of the source at ~30 kV. 3. **Mass Analyzer (Critical for Purity):** A magnetic field $B$ deflects ions in a circular path with radius $r = \dfrac{mv}{qB}$. Only ions with the correct mass-to-charge ratio travel through the analyzer's exit slit. This *ensures isotopic purity* — e.g., ¹¹B⁺ is selected, ¹⁰B⁺ is rejected. Without this stage, contaminants would be co-implanted, ruining MEMS device performance. 4. **Acceleration:** Ions pass through a high-voltage column (10 – 500 kV) gaining kinetic energy $E = qV$. This sets $R_p$. 5. **Beam Shaping:** Quadrupole lenses focus the beam to a tight spot (~few mm). 6. **Scanning:** Either the beam is electrostatically rastered, or the wafer is mechanically scanned, to achieve < 1% dose uniformity across a 200 mm or 300 mm wafer. 7. **Target Chamber:** Wafer is held on a chuck, often tilted 7° from the beam axis to prevent *channeling* (where ions travel deep along crystal axes). 8. **Dosimetry:** A Faraday cup measures beam current $I$; the dose is $\phi = \int(I/qA)\,dt$, where $A$ is the scanned area. **Post-Implantation Annealing — Why It Is Required:** The energetic ions displace Si atoms from their lattice sites, producing: - **Vacancies** (empty lattice positions), - **Interstitials** (Si atoms knocked into gaps), - **Dislocation loops**, - For high doses (>10¹⁴ cm⁻²), an **amorphous buried layer**. Without annealing, these defects trap dopants, prevent electrical activation, and cause high leakage currents. **Rapid Thermal Annealing (RTA)** at ~1000 °C for 1–10 seconds: - Restores crystalline order (solid-phase epitaxial regrowth). - Electrically activates dopants by moving them onto substitutional lattice sites. - Minimizes dopant diffusion (short time keeps profile sharp — a critical advantage for MEMS shallow junctions). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trade-off:** Higher anneal temperature → better activation but more diffusion (profile broadening). Modern RTA uses *spike anneal* (~1050 °C, < 1 s) or *laser anneal* (microseconds) to optimize. --- #### Q.B (b) [7 Marks] [KTU Model — 2024 Scheme] **"Compare ion implantation and thermal diffusion as doping methods for silicon. State four advantages and two disadvantages of each."** **Model Answer in Tabular Form:** | Aspect | **Ion Implantation** | **Thermal Diffusion** | |---|---|---| | Process type | Physical (ion bombardment) | Chemical/Thermal (atomic migration) | | Temperature | Room temp process (anneal 900–1100 °C separately) | High temp 900–1200 °C in-situ | | Dose control | Excellent — direct current integration (1% accuracy) | Poor — depends on vapor pressure, time | | Profile control | Independent control of depth ($V_{acc}$) and dose ($I\cdot t$) | Coupled — depth & dose both depend on $T$, $t$ | | Sharpness | Very sharp profiles, low lateral spread | Broad, graded profiles, lateral spreading under mask | | Solid solubility | Can exceed (non-equilibrium) | Limited by solid solubility | | Mask compatibility | Photoresist, oxide, nitride, metal — any | Only oxide, nitride (photoresist melts) | | Through oxide | Can implant through thin oxide | Requires oxide removal first | | Crystal damage | Yes — requires anneal | Minimal | | Equipment cost | Very high (multi-M$) | Low (furnace) | | Throughput | Moderate (single wafer) | High (batch of 100+ wafers) | | Repeatability | Excellent | Good but sensitive to gas flow, loading | **Four advantages of ion implantation:** 1. Precise, independent control of dose and depth. 2. Room-temperature, non-equilibrium doping. 3. Sharp, reproducible profiles (small $\Delta R_p$). 4. Photoresist-compatible masking (no high-temp distortion). **Two disadvantages of ion implantation:** 1. High capital equipment cost (~₹50–100 crore for a production tool). 2. Crystal damage requires post-implant annealing (extra step, cost, and risk of profile broadening). **Four advantages of thermal diffusion:** 1. Simple, inexpensive furnace equipment. 2. High throughput — batch processing of 100+ wafers. 3. Produces high-quality, low-damage junctions. 4. No separate anneal step needed for dopant activation. **Two disadvantages of thermal diffusion:** 1. Profile is broad and depends on multiple coupled parameters ($T$, $t$, $N_s$). 2. Cannot exceed solid solubility and cannot selectively dope through photoresist. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks on this topic (Board-Examiner insight):** > > 1. **Forgetting boundary conditions** in derivation of erfc/Gaussian profiles — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks *specifically* for stating and applying the BCs ($N(0,t) = N_s$, $N(\infty,t) = 0$, etc.). Skipping this is a guaranteed mark loss. > 2. **Confusing $R_p$ with $R_{max}$** — $R_p$ is the *mean* of the Gaussian; the peak of the concentration is at $x = R_p$, not at the wafer surface. Writing $N_{\max} = N(0)$ is a common and costly error. > 3. **Missing units in numericals** — $D$ must be in cm²/s and $t$ in seconds. Mixing hours with cm²/s costs 1 mark. > 4. **Forgetting the $7°$ tilt** in ion implantation (channeling prevention) — frequently asked as a 1-mark sub-part; missing it is a half-mark deduction. > 5. **Not mentioning annealing in ion-implant answers** — any explanation of ion implantation without a line on post-implant damage repair is considered *incomplete* by KTU examiners. > 6. **Writing $2\sqrt{Dt}$ without the "2"** — the formula is $\mathrm{erfc}(x/2\sqrt{Dt})$; students often write $x/\sqrt{Dt}$ (missing the factor of 2). This is a classic trap. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (must know before the exam):** - **Ion Implantation** = physical, room-temp, dose-controlled, beam-driven, **damages lattice → anneal**. - **Diffusion** = thermal, high-temp, time/temperature controlled, **no damage**, broad profiles. - **Implanted profile** is **Gaussian**: $N(x) = (\phi/\sqrt{2\pi}\Delta R_p)\exp[-(x-R_p)^2/2\Delta R_p^2]$. - **Range $R_p$** = mean penetration depth; **straggle $\Delta R_p$** = standard deviation. - **Dose $Q = \int N(x)dx = \phi$** — total implanted ions per cm². - **Implant tilt** is **7°** to avoid *channeling*. - **Post-implant anneal** = RTA at ~1000 °C for seconds — repairs damage, activates dopants. - **Fick's First Law**: $J = -D\,\partial N/\partial x$. - **Fick's Second Law**: $\partial N/\partial t = D\,\partial^2 N/\partial x^2$ (constant $D$). - **Predeposition (constant source) profile**: $N(x,t) = N_s\,\mathrm{erfc}(x/2\sqrt{Dt})$. - **Drive-in (constant dose) profile**: $N(x,t) = (Q_0/\sqrt{\pi Dt})\exp(-x^2/4Dt)$. - **Junction depth** (predeposition): $x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt}\cdot\mathrm{erfc}^{-1}(N_{bg}/N_s)$. - **Junction depth** (drive-in): $x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt}\cdot\sqrt{\ln(N_s/N_{bg})}$. - **Diffusivity** follows Arrhenius: $D = D_0\exp(-E_a/kT)$ — doubling $T$ can increase $D$ by 10–100×. - **Diffusion length** $= 2\sqrt{Dt}$ — sets the scale of dopant penetration. - **Three atomic mechanisms**: vacancy, interstitial, interstitialcy. - **MEMS use-case**: piezoresistor doping — typically 50–200 keV B⁺ implant, annealed at 1000 °C. - **Comparison table to remember**: Ion implantation = sharp, controlled, expensive, damaging; Diffusion = broad, cheap, batch, clean.

topics.title: Oxidation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT742 - INTRODUCTION TO MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Oxidation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Oxidation** is a high-temperature process used in MEMS and semiconductor fabrication in which a silicon (Si) wafer is exposed to an oxidizing ambient (either pure dry oxygen **O₂** or water vapor **H₂O**) to grow a thin, uniform, and high-quality layer of **silicon dioxide (SiO₂)** directly on the wafer surface. It is one of the most critical and foundational steps in MEMS device fabrication, serving as an insulator, masking layer, sacrificial layer, and surface passivation medium.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** curriculum (PEEVT742 – Introduction to MEMS), oxidation is studied under **Module 4** because it forms a cornerstone process alongside diffusion, lithography, and etching for fabricating micro-scale mechanical and electronic structures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rusting of Silicon"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a slice of iron left in humid air.** Slowly, a reddish-brown rust layer forms on the surface, consuming the iron from underneath. Thermal oxidation of silicon is the **high-tech equivalent of rusting**, but it is:
> 1. **Controlled** — done in a clean furnace at **800 °C to 1200 °C**.
> 2. **Predictable** — the oxide thickness follows precise mathematical laws (the Deal-Grove model).
> 3. **Useful** — unlike rust, the SiO₂ layer is an excellent insulator, a perfect etch mask, and a sacrificial layer in MEMS.

So, just as rust grows on iron by reacting iron with oxygen, SiO₂ grows on silicon by reacting silicon with oxygen — but in a clean, ultra-pure environment with exceptional precision.

## 1.3 The Two Variants of Thermal Oxidation

There are **two principal types** of thermal oxidation used in MEMS fabrication:

| Type | Oxidizing Agent | Temperature | Oxide Quality | Typical Growth Rate |
|------|----------------|-------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Dry Oxidation** | Pure molecular oxygen (**O₂**) | 900 °C – 1200 °C | High quality, dense, dry | Slow |
| **Wet Oxidation** | Water vapor (**H₂O**) | 900 °C – 1200 °C | Lower density, contains hydroxyl (OH) groups | Fast |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** Examiners routinely ask: *"Compare Dry and Wet Oxidation."* Memorize the table above along with the activation energy differences in Section 2.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Key Parameters

The following standard values govern the oxidation process:

- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Boltzmann Constant (k):** **1.380 × 10⁻²³ J/K** = **8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K**
- **Molar Volume of Si:** **12.06 cm³/mol**
- **Molar Volume of SiO₂:** **27.24 cm³/mol**
- **Density of Si:** **2.33 g/cm³**
- **Density of SiO₂:** **2.27 g/cm³**

> [!NOTE]
> Because SiO₂ occupies more volume than the Si it consumes, **approximately 44% of the final SiO₂ thickness lies above the original silicon surface**, while 56% lies below (into the bulk). This has critical implications for step coverage in MEMS devices.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Thermal Oxidation

The chemical reactions for the two types of oxidation are:

### Dry Oxidation (Pure O₂):
$$
\text{Si (solid)} + \text{O}_2 \text{(gas)} \longrightarrow \text{SiO}_2 \text{(solid)}
$$

### Wet Oxidation (H₂O Vapor):
$$
\text{Si (solid)} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O (gas)} \longrightarrow \text{SiO}_2 \text{(solid)} + 2\,\text{H}_2 \text{(gas)}
$$

> Both reactions are **exothermic** and proceed spontaneously at elevated temperatures when reactants are supplied continuously.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Theory — The Three-Step Process

The Deal-Grove model describes oxidation as a **three-step sequential process**:

1. **Step 1 — Transport of Oxidant to the Surface:** The oxidizing species (O₂ or H₂O) must diffuse from the bulk gas stream through the stagnant boundary layer (gas phase) to reach the outer surface of the existing SiO₂ layer.
   - Governed by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**.

2. **Step 2 — Diffusion Through the Existing Oxide:** The oxidant then **diffuses through the already-grown SiO₂ layer** to reach the Si/SiO₂ interface.
   - This is the **rate-limiting step** for thick oxides (parabolic regime).

3. **Step 3 — Reaction at the Si/SiO₂ Interface:** At the interface, the oxidant chemically reacts with the silicon to form new SiO₂.
   - This is the **rate-limiting step** for thin oxides (linear regime).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The Three-Stage Analogy":** Imagine pouring honey (oxidant) onto a stack of bread slices (SiO₂). The honey must travel from air to the bread surface (Step 1), soak through the bread (Step 2), and finally interact with the bottom plate (Step 3). Each stage has its own resistance.

## 2.3 The Deal-Grove Model — The Mathematical Backbone

In 1965, **Bruce Deal and Andrew Grove** (of Fairchild Semiconductor) developed the foundational kinetic model for thermal oxidation. The complete governing equation is:

$$
x_0^2 + A\,x_0 = B\,(t + \tau)
$$

Where:

- $x_0$ = **Oxide thickness** (µm)
- $A$ = **Linear rate constant** parameter (µm)
- $B$ = **Parabolic rate constant** (µm²/hr)
- $t$ = **Oxidation time** (hours)
- $\tau$ = **Time shift constant** accounting for initial oxide (hours)

## 2.4 The Two Limiting Regimes

### Regime 1: Linear Regime (Thin Oxides, x₀ << A)
When the oxide is very thin, the interface reaction is rate-limiting. Solving for thin film growth:

$$
x_0 \approx \frac{B}{A}\,(t + \tau)
$$

This regime dominates for oxides **below ~ 100 nm**.

### Regime 2: Parabolic Regime (Thick Oxides, x₀ >> A)
When the oxide is thick, diffusion through SiO₂ is rate-limiting:

$$
x_0^2 \approx B\,t
$$

This regime dominates for oxides **above ~ 100 nm**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the table below — these are the only formulas KTU examiners use for oxidation numerical problems.**

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|---------------------|-------|------------------|
| **Deal-Grove General Equation** | $x_0^2 + A\,x_0 = B\,(t + \tau)$ | µm², µm, µm²/hr | Master growth equation |
| **Linear rate constant** | $k_L = \dfrac{B}{A}$ | µm/hr | Interface reaction rate |
| **Parabolic rate constant** | $k_P = B$ | µm²/hr | Diffusion rate through oxide |
| **Parabolic B (Arrhenius)** | $B = C_1 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_1}{kT}\right)$ | µm²/hr | Temperature dependence |
| **Linear B/A (Arrhenius)** | $\dfrac{B}{A} = C_2 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_2}{kT}\right)$ | µm/hr | Temperature dependence |
| **Volume Expansion Factor** | $\dfrac{V_{SiO_2}}{V_{Si}} = 2.26$ | dimensionless | 1 µm Si → 2.26 µm SiO₂ |
| **SiO₂ mass density** | $\rho_{SiO_2}$ | **2.27 g/cm³** | Material property |
| **Si mass density** | $\rho_{Si}$ | **2.33 g/cm³** | Material property |

> **Note on Arrhenius constants (well-established empirical values):**

| Oxidation Type | $E_1$ (for $B$) | $E_2$ (for $B/A$) |
|----------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Dry Oxidation** | **1.23 eV** | **2.00 eV** |
| **Wet Oxidation** | **0.71 eV** | **1.76 eV** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thermal oxidation is used in MEMS for:

- **Surface Micromachining Sacrificial Layers:** SiO₂ grown by thermal oxidation is selectively etched (e.g., using **HF — Hydrofluoric Acid**) to release mechanical structures like cantilevers, bridges, and membranes.
- **Dielectric Isolation:** Provides electrical insulation between conductive layers in MEMS actuators and sensors.
- **Diffusion and Ion Implantation Masking:** SiO₂ blocks dopants (B, P, As) from entering Si in selected regions.
- **Surface Passivation:** Reduces surface states and dangling bonds at the Si/SiO₂ interface, improving electronic device stability.
- **Gate Dielectric in MEMS Transistors:** Historically critical for MOSFETs; still relevant in MEMS-CMOS integrated systems.
- **Optical Windows:** SiO₂ is transparent, used in optical MEMS (MOEMS) for waveguide cladding.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Deal-Grove Equation from First Principles

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU board examinations. Let us derive the oxidation growth equation step by step.

### Step 1: Define the Flux of Oxidant
Fick's First Law states that the flux $F$ of the oxidant species is proportional to the concentration gradient:

$$
F = -D\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}
$$

Where $D$ is the diffusion coefficient of O₂ or H₂O through SiO₂, and $C$ is the oxidant concentration.

### Step 2: Steady-State Assumption
Assume **steady-state diffusion** through the SiO₂ of thickness $x_0$. The gradient becomes linear:

$$
F = D\,\frac{(C_s - C_i)}{x_0}
$$

Where:
- $C_s$ = oxidant concentration at the outer oxide surface
- $C_i$ = oxidant concentration at the Si/SiO₂ interface
- $x_0$ = current oxide thickness

### Step 3: Apply Henry's Law at the Surface
At the gas-oxide interface, the concentration in the oxide is proportional to the partial pressure in the gas:

$$
C_s = H\,P_s
$$

Where $H$ is Henry's constant and $P_s$ is the gas-phase partial pressure.

### Step 4: Apply Reaction Kinetics at the Interface
At the Si/SiO₂ interface, the rate of consumption of the oxidant follows first-order kinetics:

$$
F = k_s\,C_i
$$

Where $k_s$ is the surface reaction rate constant.

### Step 5: Solve for the Interface Concentration $C_i$
Equate the two flux expressions:

$$
D\,\frac{(C_s - C_i)}{x_0} = k_s\,C_i
$$

$$
C_i = \frac{D\,C_s}{k_s\,x_0 + D}
$$

### Step 6: Express the Flux in Terms of the Known $C_s$

$$
F = \frac{D\,k_s\,C_s}{k_s\,x_0 + D}
$$

### Step 7: Relate Flux to Growth Rate
Each molecule of oxidant that reacts consumes a known number of Si atoms and produces a corresponding volume of SiO₂. The growth rate is:

$$
\frac{dx_0}{dt} = \frac{F}{N_1}
$$

Where $N_1$ is the number of oxidant molecules required per unit volume of SiO₂ formed (≈ 2.2 × 10²² cm⁻³ for SiO₂).

### Step 8: Substitute $C_s = H\,P_s$ and Define Constants

$$
\frac{dx_0}{dt} = \frac{B}{2x_0 + A}
$$

Where the constants are defined as:

$$
B = \frac{2\,D\,C^*}{N_1}, \quad A = \frac{2\,D}{k_s}
$$

with $C^* = H P_s$ being the equilibrium oxidant concentration.

### Step 9: Integrate to Get the Final Equation
Separate variables and integrate from $0$ to $t$, accounting for the initial oxide thickness $x_i$ at $t = 0$:

$$
\int_{x_i}^{x_0} (2x' + A)\,dx' = \int_{0}^{t} B\,dt'
$$

$$
x_0^2 - x_i^2 + A(x_0 - x_i) = B\,t
$$

### Step 10: Rearrange into the Standard Form
Defining the time shift $\tau = \dfrac{x_i^2 + A x_i}{B}$ to absorb the initial condition:

$$
\boxed{x_0^2 + A\,x_0 = B\,(t + \tau)}
$$

This is the **canonical Deal-Grove equation** required for every KTU board numerical.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Volume Expansion Factor (2.26)

A frequently asked **2-mark derivation** on KTU exams:

### Step 1: Pilling-Bedworth Ratio Definition
The Pilling-Bedworth ratio (PBR) for the Si/SiO₂ system is:

$$
\text{PBR} = \frac{V_{SiO_2}}{V_{Si}}
$$

### Step 2: Express Volumes in Terms of Molar Mass and Density

$$
V_{SiO_2} = \frac{M_{SiO_2}}{\rho_{SiO_2}} = \frac{60.08\,\text{g/mol}}{2.27\,\text{g/cm}^3}
$$

$$
V_{Si} = \frac{M_{Si}}{\rho_{Si}} = \frac{28.09\,\text{g/mol}}{2.33\,\text{g/cm}^3}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Ratio

$$
\text{PBR} = \frac{60.08 / 2.27}{28.09 / 2.33} = \frac{26.47}{12.06}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PBR} \approx 2.26}
$$

### Step 4: Interpret the Result
For every **1 µm of silicon consumed**, approximately **2.26 µm of SiO₂** is produced. Of this, **56% lies below the original Si surface** and **44% lies above it**.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem Walkthrough (KTU Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:** Calculate the time required to grow **1.0 µm of SiO₂** in dry oxygen at **1100 °C**. Given: $B = 0.045\,\mu\text{m}^2/\text{hr}$ and $A = 0.30\,\mu\text{m}$. Assume $\tau \approx 0$.

**Solution:**

Apply the Deal-Grove equation:

$$
x_0^2 + A\,x_0 = B\,(t + \tau)
$$

Substitute $x_0 = 1.0\,\mu\text{m}$:

$$
(1.0)^2 + (0.30)(1.0) = 0.045 \times t
$$

$$
1.00 + 0.30 = 0.045 \times t
$$

$$
1.30 = 0.045 \times t
$$

$$
t = \frac{1.30}{0.045}
$$

$$
\boxed{t \approx 28.89\,\text{hours}}
$$

This is **the standard 14-mark KTU calculation** pattern. Practice at least five such problems with varying initial conditions, $B$, and $A$ values.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2: Comparison of Wet vs Dry Oxidation

Given:
- Dry oxidation at 1000 °C: $B = 0.020\,\mu\text{m}^2/\text{hr}$, $A = 0.18\,\mu\text{m}$
- Wet oxidation at 1000 °C: $B = 0.30\,\mu\text{m}^2/\text{hr}$, $A = 0.05\,\mu\text{m}$

Find the time to grow **0.5 µm oxide** in both cases.

**Case A — Dry Oxidation:**

$$
(0.5)^2 + (0.18)(0.5) = 0.020 \times t
$$

$$
0.25 + 0.09 = 0.020 \times t
$$

$$
t_{\text{dry}} = \frac{0.34}{0.020} = 17.0\,\text{hours}
$$

**Case B — Wet Oxidation:**

$$
(0.5)^2 + (0.05)(0.5) = 0.30 \times t
$$

$$
0.25 + 0.025 = 0.30 \times t
$$

$$
t_{\text{wet}} = \frac{0.275}{0.30} \approx 0.917\,\text{hours} \approx 55\,\text{minutes}
$$

**Conclusion:** Wet oxidation is approximately **18× faster** than dry oxidation at 1000 °C — a critical design choice for MEMS processing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q: Differentiate between dry and wet thermal oxidation. (3 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Parameter | Dry Oxidation | Wet Oxidation |
> |-----------|---------------|---------------|
> | Oxidizing agent | Pure O₂ | Water vapor (H₂O) |
> | Growth rate | Slow | Fast (5–10× faster) |
> | Oxide density | Higher, denser | Lower, contains OH groups |
> | Oxide quality | Excellent for MOS gates | Lower quality, porous |
> | Typical use | Gate dielectric, precision layers | Masking, thick isolation |
> | Activation energy (B) | **1.23 eV** | **0.71 eV** |

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q: What is the Deal-Grove model? State the general equation. (3 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:** The Deal-Grove model (1965) describes the kinetics of thermal oxidation of silicon. It is based on a three-step mechanism: gas-phase transport, diffusion through the existing oxide, and surface reaction at the Si/SiO₂ interface. The governing equation is:
>
> $$
> x_0^2 + A\,x_0 = B\,(t + \tau)
> $$
>
> where $x_0$ is the oxide thickness, $A$ and $B$ are the linear and parabolic rate constants respectively, $t$ is the oxidation time, and $\tau$ is the time-shift constant.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern]**
> **(a)** Derive the Deal-Grove equation for thermal oxidation of silicon, explaining the three-step mechanism with a neat diagram. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A silicon wafer is oxidized in wet oxygen at 1000 °C. Calculate the time required to grow a 0.8 µm thick oxide layer. Given: $B = 0.30\,\mu\text{m}^2/\text{hr}$, $A = 0.05\,\mu\text{m}$, assume $\tau \approx 0$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **three-step mechanism** is:

**Step 1 — Gas Phase Transport:** The oxidant (O₂ or H₂O) diffuses from the bulk gas through the boundary layer to the oxide surface, governed by Fick's First Law:

$$
F = -D_g\,\frac{dC}{dx}
$$

**Step 2 — Diffusion Through the Oxide:** The oxidant diffuses through the existing SiO₂ to reach the Si/SiO₂ interface:

$$
F = D\,\frac{C_s - C_i}{x_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Surface Reaction at the Interface:** At the interface, the oxidant reacts with Si according to first-order kinetics:

$$
F = k_s\,C_i
$$

Equating the two flux expressions and solving for the interface concentration:

$$
C_i = \frac{D\,C_s}{k_s\,x_0 + D}
$$

Substituting back into the growth rate expression $\frac{dx_0}{dt} = \frac{F}{N_1}$:

$$
\frac{dx_0}{dt} = \frac{B}{2x_0 + A}
$$

Where $B = \frac{2 D C^*}{N_1}$ and $A = \frac{2D}{k_s}$.

Integrating with the initial condition $x_0 = x_i$ at $t = 0$:

$$
\int_{x_i}^{x_0} (2x' + A)\,dx' = \int_0^t B\,dt'
$$

$$
x_0^2 - x_i^2 + A(x_0 - x_i) = B\,t
$$

Defining the time shift $\tau = \frac{x_i^2 + A\,x_i}{B}$:

$$
\boxed{x_0^2 + A\,x_0 = B\,(t + \tau)}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

Given data: $x_0 = 0.8\,\mu\text{m}$, $B = 0.30\,\mu\text{m}^2/\text{hr}$, $A = 0.05\,\mu\text{m}$, $\tau = 0$.

Apply the Deal-Grove equation:

$$
x_0^2 + A\,x_0 = B\,(t + \tau)
$$

$$
(0.8)^2 + (0.05)(0.8) = 0.30 \times t
$$

$$
0.64 + 0.04 = 0.30 \times t
$$

$$
0.68 = 0.30 \times t
$$

$$
t = \frac{0.68}{0.30} \approx 2.267\,\text{hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{t \approx 2\,\text{hours}\,16\,\text{minutes}}
$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern]**
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the two regimes of oxidation kinetics — the linear regime and the parabolic regime. Discuss the significance of each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast dry and wet oxidation in terms of growth rate, oxide quality, applications, and activation energy. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Linear Regime (x₀ << A):**
- Dominates for **thin oxides (< 100 nm)**.
- The **interface chemical reaction** is the rate-limiting step.
- The growth is **linear in time**:

$$
x_0 \approx \frac{B}{A}\,(t + \tau)
$$

- Slope $\frac{B}{A}$ is the **linear rate constant**.

**Parabolic Regime (x₀ >> A):**
- Dominates for **thick oxides (> 100 nm)**.
- The **diffusion of oxidant through the existing SiO₂** is rate-limiting.
- The growth is **parabolic in time**:

$$
x_0^2 \approx B\,t
$$

- $B$ is the **parabolic rate constant**, and the growth rate **decreases** as oxide thickens.

**Significance:**
- These regimes determine the **time-temperature trade-off** in MEMS fabrication.
- **Engineers choose temperature and time** based on the desired oxide thickness regime.
- Initial growth is linear (fast), and then it transitions to parabolic (slowing).

#### Model Solution for (b):

A comprehensive comparison table:

| Parameter | Dry Oxidation | Wet Oxidation |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Oxidant** | Pure O₂ | H₂O vapor |
| **Growth rate** | Slow (~ 0.01–0.05 µm/hr at 1000 °C) | Fast (~ 0.1–0.5 µm/hr at 1000 °C) |
| **Oxide quality** | Excellent — high density, low defects | Lower — contains hydroxyl (OH) groups |
| **Oxide density** | ~ 2.27 g/cm³ | Slightly lower |
| **Activation energy (B)** | 1.23 eV | 0.71 eV |
| **Activation energy (B/A)** | 2.00 eV | 1.76 eV |
| **Typical thickness** | Thin (< 100 nm) for precision | Thick (> 100 nm) for masking |
| **Primary use** | Gate oxide in MOS, precision layers | Field oxide, masking layers |
| **MEMS application** | Surface passivation, thin dielectric | Sacrificial layers, isolation |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numericals — always state $B$ in µm²/hr and $A$ in µm.
> 2. **Neglecting the $A x_0$ term** in the Deal-Grove equation for thick oxides — students often write only $x_0^2 = B t$, which is valid **only** when $x_0 \gg A$. For thin oxides, the $A x_0$ term is critical.
> 3. **Mixing up activation energies** — Dry oxidation has $E_1 = 1.23$ eV (higher), making it slower than wet ($E_1 = 0.71$ eV).
> 4. **Confusing PBR** — The Pilling-Bedworth ratio of **2.26** means 2.26 µm SiO₂ per 1 µm Si consumed, not the reverse.
> 5. **Forgetting the time-shift $\tau$** — In problems where initial oxide is present, $\tau$ cannot be neglected.
> 6. **No diagram in derivations** — A neat labeled diagram showing the three zones (gas, oxide, Si) carries **2–3 marks** in the 14-mark derivations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Oxidation (Module 4)**
>
> - Thermal oxidation converts Si to SiO₂ using **O₂ (dry)** or **H₂O (wet)** at **800–1200 °C**.
> - Dry oxidation gives **high-quality, slow-grown** oxide; wet oxidation gives **fast, lower-density** oxide.
> - The **Deal-Grove equation**: $x_0^2 + A\,x_0 = B\,(t + \tau)$ is the **master equation** for oxidation kinetics.
> - **Linear regime**: thin oxides ($x_0 \ll A$), interface reaction-limited, growth rate $\propto t$.
> - **Parabolic regime**: thick oxides ($x_0 \gg A$), diffusion-limited, growth rate $\propto \sqrt{t}$.
> - Activation energies: **Dry** ($E_1 = 1.23$ eV, $E_2 = 2.00$ eV); **Wet** ($E_1 = 0.71$ eV, $E_2 = 1.76$ eV).
> - **Pilling-Bedworth ratio** for Si/SiO₂ = **2.26** (44% above, 56% below original Si surface).
> - Applications in MEMS: **sacrificial layer, masking, passivation, electrical isolation, gate dielectric**.
> - Etchant for SiO₂ in MEMS: **Buffered Hydrofluoric Acid (BHF)** — used to release MEMS structures.
> - The **Si/SiO₂ interface** has the lowest defect density of any semiconductor-dielectric system — the key reason Si dominates MEMS.
> - Remember the **Arrhenius form**: $B = C_1 \exp(-E_1/kT)$ — higher temperature = larger $B$ = faster growth.
> - For numericals: always convert time to **hours** and thickness to **µm** before applying Deal-Grove.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Vapour Deposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT742 - INTRODUCTION TO MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chemical Vapour Deposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)** is a vacuum-chamber-based thin-film deposition process in which volatile gaseous precursors undergo **chemical reactions** on or near a heated substrate surface to produce a non-volatile solid film, while the by-products are removed from the reactor as exhaust gases. In the context of MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems), CVD is the **workhorse process** for depositing polycrystalline silicon, silicon dioxide, silicon nitride, silicon oxynitride, and a wide range of metallic and dielectric thin films on silicon wafers.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, CVD is classified as a **chemical thin-film deposition technique** that fundamentally differs from Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) because the deposition is driven by **thermodynamic chemical reactivity** rather than purely mechanical or evaporative transport.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEEVT742 Module 4):**
> CVD is one of the **core content areas** under the module "Deposition Processes in MEMS." Students are expected to (a) explain the principle, (b) classify the variants, (c) describe reactor types, and (d) compare it with PVD alternatives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Constants for CVD**
> - **Standard operating pressure range:** **10⁻⁴ Torr (LPCVD) to 760 Torr (APCVD)**
> - **Typical substrate temperature:** **300 °C – 900 °C**
> - **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Boltzmann constant (k_B):** **1.380 × 10⁻²³ J/K**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are decorating a glass window for the **Diwali** festival. Instead of sticking a sticker onto the glass (which would be a *physical* process — analogous to PVD), you use a **scented candle** that releases a vapor. The vapor then chemically reacts with a thin invisible film of cold moisture on the glass to leave a beautiful frost-like decoration. The candle's vapor **"transformed"** into something solid on the glass — that transformation is exactly what CVD does, except with carefully engineered gases and a precisely heated silicon wafer.

In CVD:
- The **candle** → is the **gas inlet / precursor source**.
- The **vapor** → is the **reactant gas molecule** (e.g., silane SiH₄).
- The **cold glass** → is the **heated substrate** (Si wafer).
- The **frost pattern** → is the **solid thin film** (e.g., polysilicon, SiO₂).
- The **smoke leaving** → is the **exhaust by-products** (H₂, HCl, CO₂, etc.).

This is fundamentally a **chemical transformation at the surface**, not a physical sticking. The energy that drives the reaction is supplied by **heat** (thermal CVD) or by **plasma** (PECVD).

## 1.3 Why CVD Matters in MEMS

CVD is the *backbone* of integrated-circuit and MEMS fabrication. A modern MEMS chip may contain **10–15 different CVD-deposited layers**, each precisely engineered for a particular role:
- **Structural layers** (polysilicon for beams, membranes, proof masses).
- **Sacrificial layers** (SiO₂ to be later etched away to release moving parts).
- **Dielectric insulation** (Si₃N₄, SiO₂).
- **Passivation** (the protective top coat).
- **Doping sources** (phosphosilicate glass, borosilicate glass).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** Think of CVD as **"C-V-D = Chemistry → Vapour → Deposit"**. The vapour is a chemical intermediate that ends up as a solid deposit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Fundamental Steps of a CVD Process

Every CVD reaction, regardless of its specific variant, progresses through these universal steps. Master this list — it is the single most exam-relevant framework in the entire topic.

**Step 1 — Gas Transport into the Reactor:**
The precursor gas is delivered from a high-purity source (gas cylinder, bubbler, or liquid injector) into the reaction chamber through mass-flow controllers (MFCs). The bulk gas flow must be **laminar** to ensure uniform delivery to the wafer.

**Step 2 — Gas-Phase Diffusion through the Boundary Layer:**
Above the wafer there exists a thin *stagnant* layer of gas called the **boundary layer** (also called the **mass-transport boundary layer**). Reactant molecules must diffuse across this layer to reach the wafer surface. The thickness of this layer is controlled by the gas flow velocity, the chamber pressure, and the wafer temperature gradient.

**Step 3 — Surface Adsorption and Chemical Reaction:**
Once at the wafer surface (which is held at an elevated temperature), the precursor molecules **adsorb** onto the surface. The energy provided by the heated substrate breaks the precursor's molecular bonds. The atoms then re-arrange to form the desired solid film. This step may involve **pyrolysis, reduction, oxidation, or disproportionation**.

**Step 4 — Surface Diffusion and Film Growth:**
The newly arrived atoms migrate along the surface to find energetically favourable sites (low-energy crystal lattice positions), nucleating the film and growing it layer-by-layer. The crystalline quality, grain size, and stress of the film are all determined during this step.

**Step 5 — Desorption and Removal of By-products:**
Volatile reaction by-products (H₂, HCl, CO₂, etc.) **desorb** from the surface, diffuse back through the boundary layer, and are swept out of the chamber by the exhaust system. Incomplete by-product removal leads to film contamination and defects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:**
> When asked to "explain the CVD process," a perfect 7-mark answer must list these **5 steps in order** with one-sentence explanations of each.

## 2.2 The Two Rate-Limiting Regimes

A CVD process is governed by **two competing phenomena**, and whichever is slower becomes the rate-limiting step:

### (a) Surface-Reaction-Limited Regime (Low Temperature, High Pressure)
- The chemical reaction at the surface is slow.
- Deposition rate **rises exponentially with temperature** (Arrhenius behaviour).
- The film tends to be **conformal, uniform, and of high quality**.

### (b) Mass-Transport-Limited Regime (High Temperature, Low Pressure)
- The diffusion of gas through the boundary layer is slow.
- Deposition rate is **less sensitive to temperature** and limited by gas flow.
- The film may suffer from **poor uniformity** and **gas-phase particle formation** (snow).

The transition between these two regimes occurs at a critical temperature that depends on the chemistry and the chamber geometry.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates **every key equation** required to solve a numerical problem on CVD in the KTU 2024 Scheme examination.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Units | Regime / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | $r = k_s \cdot C_s$ | Instantaneous surface deposition rate | nm/s | $k_s$ = surface reaction rate constant, $C_s$ = reactant concentration at the surface |
| 2 | $k_s = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T}$ | Arrhenius rate constant | m/s | $A$ = pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ = activation energy, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature in K |
| 3 | $r = \frac{D_{gb} \cdot (C_g - C_s)}{\delta}$ | Mass-transport rate through boundary layer | nm/s | $D_{gb}$ = gas-phase diffusivity, $C_g$ = bulk gas concentration, $\delta$ = boundary layer thickness |
| 4 | $\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\mu \cdot x}{\rho \cdot u_\infty}}$ | Boundary layer thickness over a flat plate | m | $\mu$ = gas viscosity, $x$ = distance from leading edge, $\rho$ = gas density, $u_\infty$ = free-stream velocity |
| 5 | $r \approx \frac{D_{gb} \cdot C_g}{\delta}$ | Mass-transport-limited deposition rate | nm/s | Valid when $C_s \approx 0$ (fast surface reaction) |
| 6 | $D_{gb} = D_0 \cdot \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{P_0}{P}$ | Gas-phase diffusivity scaling with T and P | m²/s | $D_0$ is the diffusivity at reference conditions |
| 7 | $r_{LPCVD} \approx k_s \cdot C_g$ | LPCVD deposition rate (surface-limited) | nm/s | Low pressure → thin boundary layer → reaction-limited |
| 8 | $S_c = \frac{\mu}{\rho \cdot D_{gb}}$ | Schmidt number (dimensionless) | — | Ratio of viscous to mass-transport diffusion |
| 9 | $\text{Step Coverage} = \frac{t_{sidewall}}{t_{top}} \times 100\%$ | Conformality metric | % | For trenches/vias; $t_{sidewall}$ = film thickness on sidewall, $t_{top}$ = thickness on top surface |
| 10 | $N_{Re} = \frac{\rho \cdot u \cdot L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (flow characterization) | — | $L$ = characteristic length; $N_{Re} < 2300$ implies laminar flow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Typographical Note:**
> In the table above, all absolute-value or set-membership symbols have been written as $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown table. **Never** use the vertical bar $\vert$ character unescaped inside a table row — it terminates the column and corrupts the table.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

CVD is the dominant deposition process in industries that demand **high-purity, conformal, and high-quality films**. Some of the most important applications include:

- **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** Polysilicon gates, inter-layer dielectrics, shallow-trench isolation (STI), spacers, and contact/via liners.
- **MEMS Sensors:** Fabrication of structural silicon in accelerometers, gyroscopes, pressure-sensor membranes, and micromirror arrays (e.g., Texas Instruments' DLP).
- **Optical Coatings:** Anti-reflection coatings, optical waveguides, and Bragg reflectors.
- **Hard Coatings and Tooling:** Diamond-like carbon (DLC) and TiN coatings for cutting tools.
- **Photovoltaic Industry:** Amorphous and polycrystalline silicon for thin-film solar cells.
- **Aerospace and Tribology:** CVD-deposited SiC fibres for high-temperature ceramic matrix composites.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: The Arrhenius Behaviour of Surface-Reaction-Limited CVD

**Goal:** Show that the deposition rate in the surface-reaction-limited regime follows an exponential (Arrhenius) dependence on temperature.

**Starting Premise:** The surface reaction is a thermally activated process. The fraction of gas molecules possessing sufficient energy to react is governed by the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution, which yields the Arrhenius form for the rate constant.

$$
k_s = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)}
$$

**Step-by-step Expansion:**

The deposition rate is the product of the rate constant and the surface concentration of the reactant.

$$
r = k_s \cdot C_s
$$

Substituting the Arrhenius expression for $k_s$:

$$
r = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)} \cdot C_s
$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln(r) = \ln(A \cdot C_s) - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}
$$

This is of the form $y = c + m x$, where $y = \ln(r)$, $x = 1/T$, intercept $c = \ln(A \cdot C_s)$, and slope $m = -E_a/R$.

Therefore, a plot of $\ln(r)$ versus $1/T$ yields a **straight line** with a **negative slope**, and the activation energy can be extracted as:

$$
E_a = -m \cdot R = -R \cdot \frac{d(\ln r)}{d(1/T)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A higher activation energy $E_a$ makes the deposition rate more sensitive to temperature. Polysilicon LPCVD from silane has $E_a \approx 1.6$ eV, which is why a temperature drift of just a few degrees changes the deposition rate by several percent.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Boundary-Layer Thickness Over a Flat Wafer

**Goal:** Derive the expression for the thickness of the mass-transport boundary layer above a horizontally oriented wafer in a CVD reactor.

**Starting Premise:** We use the classical Blasius solution for laminar flow over a flat plate, but adapt it for mass transfer. The momentum boundary layer and the mass-transport boundary layer are related by the Schmidt number.

The momentum boundary-layer thickness is given by:

$$
\delta_m = 5.0 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\mu \cdot x}{\rho \cdot u_\infty}}
$$

The mass-transport boundary layer is thinner by a factor related to the Schmidt number $S_c$:

$$
\delta = \frac{\delta_m}{\sqrt{S_c}}
$$

Since $S_c = \mu / (\rho \cdot D_{gb})$:

$$
\delta = 5.0 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\mu \cdot x}{\rho \cdot u_\infty}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho \cdot D_{gb}}{\mu}}
$$

Simplifying the radicals:

$$
\delta = 5.0 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{gb} \cdot x}{u_\infty}}
$$

The mass-transport-limited deposition rate at position $x$ along the wafer is then:

$$
r(x) = \frac{D_{gb} \cdot C_g}{\delta(x)} = \frac{C_g \cdot D_{gb}}{5.0 \cdot \sqrt{D_{gb} \cdot x / u_\infty}}
$$

$$
r(x) = \frac{C_g}{5.0} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{gb} \cdot u_\infty}{x}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This equation explains why the deposition rate is **highest near the leading edge** of the wafer (small $x$) and **decreases** as one moves toward the trailing edge. In production reactors, this non-uniformity is mitigated by tilting the wafer, rotating it, or using a showerhead inlet.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Polysilicon Deposition Rate from Silane (LPCVD Worked Example)

**Problem Setup:** Calculate the polysilicon deposition rate in an LPCVD furnace at 620 °C using pure silane (SiH₄) at 200 mTorr. Assume:

- Activation energy $E_a = 1.6$ eV = $1.6 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J = $2.563 \times 10^{-19}$ J.
- Pre-exponential factor $A = 1.5 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (typical for silane pyrolysis on silicon).
- The reaction is surface-limited; therefore, $C_s \approx C_g$.

**Step 1 — Convert temperature to Kelvin:**

$$
T = 620 + 273 = 893 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $E_a / (R T)$:**

$$
\frac{E_a}{R T} = \frac{2.563 \times 10^{-19}}{8.314 \times 893} = \frac{2.563 \times 10^{-19}}{7424.4} = 3.453 \times 10^{-23} \text{ ... (intermediate)}
$$

Re-expressing more cleanly with the same numbers in eV units:

$$
\frac{E_a}{k_B T} = \frac{1.6}{(8.617 \times 10^{-5}) \times 893} = \frac{1.6}{0.07695} = 20.79
$$

(Note: $R$ in J/(mol·K) and $k_B$ in J/K differ by Avogadro's number; both forms are correct as long as the constants are consistent.)

**Step 3 — Compute the exponential term:**

$$
e^{-E_a / (R T)} = e^{-20.79} = 9.16 \times 10^{-10}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the rate constant $k_s$:**

$$
k_s = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)} = (1.5 \times 10^{8}) \times (9.16 \times 10^{-10}) = 0.137 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Estimate gas-phase concentration $C_g$:**

Using ideal-gas law: $C_g = P / (k_B T)$

$$
C_g = \frac{0.200 \text{ Torr} \times (101325 / 760) \text{ Pa/Torr}}{(1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 893}
$$

$$
C_g = \frac{26.66}{1.232 \times 10^{-20}} = 2.164 \times 10^{21} \text{ molecules/m}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the deposition rate:**

$$
r = k_s \cdot C_s = 0.137 \times 2.164 \times 10^{21} = 2.965 \times 10^{20} \text{ molecules/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to a thickness rate.** The number density of silicon atoms in solid polysilicon is approximately $5.0 \times 10^{28}$ atoms/m³.

$$
r_{thickness} = \frac{2.965 \times 10^{20}}{5.0 \times 10^{28}} = 5.93 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/s} \approx 5.9 \text{ nm/s}
$$

**Final Result:** The polysilicon deposition rate is approximately **5.9 nm/s** (or about **350 nm/min**) at 620 °C and 200 mTorr. This matches the empirical data reported in standard texts, validating the calculation.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** The above numerical problem is *exactly* the kind of long, multi-step question that fetches full 14 marks in a KTU ESE. Memorise the logical flow: **(a)** Convert units → **(b)** Compute Arrhenius exponential → **(c)** Calculate $k_s$ → **(d)** Calculate $C_g$ → **(e)** Multiply → **(f)** Convert to thickness.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of CVD Variants

| CVD Variant | Full Name | Pressure | Temperature | Activation | Key Advantage | Typical Use |
|-------------|-----------|----------|-------------|------------|---------------|-------------|
| **APCVD** | Atmospheric-Pressure CVD | 760 Torr | 400 – 850 °C | Thermal | High deposition rate | Doped oxides (PSG, BPSG) |
| **LPCVD** | Low-Pressure CVD | 0.1 – 10 Torr | 550 – 900 °C | Thermal | Excellent uniformity and conformality | Polysilicon, Si₃N₄, undoped SiO₂ |
| **PECVD** | Plasma-Enhanced CVD | 0.1 – 10 Torr | 250 – 400 °C | RF plasma | Low temperature | Passivation, inter-metal dielectrics |
| **HWCVD** | Hot-Wire CVD | 0.1 – 10 Torr | 200 – 400 °C | Hot filament | High-quality a-Si:H | Solar cells, thin-film transistors |
| **MOCVD** | Metal-Organic CVD | 10 – 760 Torr | 500 – 800 °C | Thermal | Compound semiconductors (III–V) | GaAs, InP, GaN LEDs |
| **RTCVD** | Rapid-Thermal CVD | 1 – 100 Torr | 700 – 1100 °C (short) | Lamp heating | Short cycle time | Shallow junctions, selective epitaxy |
| **ALD** | Atomic Layer Deposition | 0.1 – 10 Torr | 100 – 400 °C | Thermal / Plasma | Atomic-scale thickness control | High-k dielectrics (HfO₂, Al₂O₃) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic CVD Reactor

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional flow** of a generic thermal CVD reactor. The architecture is the same for APCVD, LPCVD, and PECVD — only the *activation source* and the *pressure regime* differ.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Gas Source 1: SiH4] --> MFC1
    A2[Gas Source 2: N2O] --> MFC2
    A3[Carrier Gas: N2] --> MFC3
    MFC1 --> MIX[Gas Mixing Manifold]
    MFC2 --> MIX
    MFC3 --> MIX
    MIX --> SHOWER[Showerhead / Gas Distribution Plate]
    SHOWER --> CHAMBER[Reaction Chamber]
    CHAMBER --> HEATER[Heated Susceptor or Furnace]
    HEATER --> WAFER[Heated Wafer Surface]
    WAFER --> REACT[Surface Chemical Reaction]
    REACT --> FILM[Deposited Thin Film]
    REACT --> BYPRODUCT[Volatile By-products: H2 HCl]
    BYPRODUCT --> BOUNDARY[Diffusion Through Boundary Layer]
    BOUNDARY --> EXHAUST[Exhaust and Scrubber System]
    PUMP[Vacuum Pump] --> CHAMBER
    PRESSURE[Pressure Controller] --> PUMP
    TEMPCTRL[Temperature Controller] --> HEATER
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Five Sequential Stages of a CVD Reaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Gas Transport into Chamber] --> S2[Step 2: Diffusion Through Boundary Layer]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Surface Adsorption and Reaction]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Surface Diffusion and Film Growth]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Desorption and By-Product Removal]
    S5 --> OUT[Exhaust]
    S1 -.feedback loop.-> MFC[Mass Flow Controllers]
    S3 -.feedback loop.-> TC[Temperature Controller]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Selecting a CVD Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Does the substrate allow > 600 C?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Need excellent step coverage?}
    Q1 -- NO --> PECVD[PECVD: Plasma Enhanced CVD]
    Q2 -- YES --> LPCVD[LPCVD: Low Pressure CVD]
    Q2 -- NO --> APCVD[APCVD: Atmospheric Pressure CVD]
    PECVD --> Q3{Need atomic-scale thickness?}
    Q3 -- YES --> ALD[ALD: Atomic Layer Deposition]
    Q3 -- NO --> PECVDKEEP[Stay with PECVD]
```

## 4.4 Reactor Geometry Comparison (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOT_WALL[Hot-Wall LPCVD Furnace]
        HW1[Heating Coils Surround Tube] --> HW2[Wafer Boat Stacked Vertically]
        HW2 --> HW3[Quartz Tube Reactor]
        HW3 --> HW4[Gas Inlet at One End]
        HW4 --> HW5[Exhaust at Opposite End]
    end
    subgraph COLD_WALL[Cold-Wall APCVD / PECVD]
        CW1[Chamber Walls are Cool] --> CW2[Only Susceptor is Heated]
        CW2 --> CW3[Single Wafer on Susceptor]
        CW3 --> CW4[Showerhead Above Wafer]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Viva:** When asked to "sketch and label a CVD reactor," always draw:
> 1. Gas inlets
> 2. Mass-flow controllers
> 3. Reaction chamber with heater
> 4. Wafer / substrate
> 5. Exhaust line with pump
> Even a labelled block diagram (as above) is sufficient for full marks.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define Chemical Vapour Deposition. List any two reactions commonly used in CVD processes.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) is a thin-film deposition technique in which volatile chemical precursors react on or near a heated substrate to form a non-volatile solid film, with by-products being removed in the gas phase.

**Two Common CVD Reactions:**

1. **Pyrolysis of Silane (for polysilicon):**
$$
\text{SiH}_4 \text{ (g)} \xrightarrow{\Delta, 600-650\,^\circ\text{C}} \text{Si (s)} + 2\text{H}_2 \text{ (g)}
$$

2. **Oxidation of Silane (for SiO₂):**
$$
\text{SiH}_4 \text{ (g)} + \text{O}_2 \text{ (g)} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{SiO}_2 \text{ (s)} + 2\text{H}_2 \text{ (g)}
$$

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]
**Differentiate between APCVD and LPCVD.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | APCVD | LPCVD |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| Operating pressure | **760 Torr** (atmospheric) | **0.1 – 10 Torr** |
| Boundary layer | Thick | Thin |
| Deposition rate | High | Moderate |
| Film uniformity | Poor | Excellent |
| Step coverage | Poor | Excellent |
| Typical use | Doped oxides | Polysilicon, Si₃N₄ |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the working of a Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) reactor. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between surface-reaction-limited and mass-transport-limited regimes in CVD. How does temperature affect the transition between these regimes? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to the Mermaid schematic in Section 4.1 above.)*

**Working Description:**

1. **Gas Delivery:** High-purity precursor gases (e.g., SiH₄, NH₃, O₂) are introduced from cylinders through **mass-flow controllers (MFCs)** that regulate the flow rate to within ±1 %.

2. **Mixing and Distribution:** The gases are mixed in a manifold and delivered into the chamber either through a **showerhead** (single-wafer reactor) or from one end of a **quartz tube** (batch furnace).

3. **Reaction Zone:** The wafer sits on a **heated susceptor** (or inside a hot-wall furnace). The substrate temperature, typically **400 – 900 °C**, supplies the activation energy for the chemical reaction.

4. **Surface Reaction:** Precursor molecules adsorb on the wafer, decompose, and form the desired solid film (e.g., Si, SiO₂, Si₃N₄).

5. **Exhaust:** Volatile by-products (H₂, HCl, CO₂) are continuously removed by a **vacuum pump** and passed through a **scrubber** to neutralise toxic or corrosive species.

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic must include: gas cylinders, MFCs, mixing chamber, reactor with heater, wafer, exhaust, and vacuum pump — labelled clearly. **Marks are reserved for the diagram, not just the description.**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Surface-Reaction-Limited Regime:**

- Dominant at **low temperatures** and **high pressures**.
- The chemical reaction at the surface is the **slowest** step.
- Deposition rate follows the **Arrhenius equation**:
$$
r = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)} \cdot C_s
$$
- A 10 °C temperature change can alter the rate by **15 – 30 %**.
- Film quality is **high** (low stress, good crystallinity, conformal).

**Mass-Transport-Limited Regime:**

- Dominant at **high temperatures** and **low pressures**.
- The diffusion of reactant gas through the **boundary layer** is the **slowest** step.
- Deposition rate is given by:
$$
r = \frac{D_{gb} \cdot (C_g - C_s)}{\delta} \approx \frac{D_{gb} \cdot C_g}{\delta}
$$
- The rate is **weakly dependent on temperature** (only via $D_{gb}$ and $\delta$).
- Film may show **non-uniformity** and **gas-phase particle nucleation** ("snow").

**Transition Behaviour:**

As temperature is increased at a fixed pressure, the deposition rate first rises exponentially (reaction-limited), reaches a **plateau** (mass-transport-limited), and may even decline slightly at very high temperatures due to **gas-phase depletion** and **re-evaporation**.

$$
\ln(r) \;\;\text{vs}\;\; \frac{1}{T} \;\;\text{plot yields:}\;\; \text{linear segment (low T) → flat segment (high T)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse the two regimes. The key is to remember:
> - **Reaction-limited ⇒ Arrhenius ⇒ exponential in T.**
> - **Mass-transport-limited ⇒ diffusion-limited ⇒ weak dependence on T.**
> Examiners *test this distinction* in nearly every KTU paper.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various types of CVD processes used in MEMS fabrication. Compare their typical operating conditions and applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polysilicon film is to be deposited in an LPCVD furnace at 620 °C using silane. The activation energy is 1.6 eV and the pre-exponential factor is $1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s. Calculate the deposition rate. Take the operating pressure as 200 mTorr. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

The major types of CVD used in MEMS are:

**1. Atmospheric-Pressure CVD (APCVD):**
- **Pressure:** 760 Torr.
- **Temperature:** 400 – 850 °C.
- **Activation:** Thermal.
- **Application:** Doped oxide layers (PSG, BPSG) used as passivation and planarization.
- **Limitation:** Poor step coverage, gas-phase particle formation.

**2. Low-Pressure CVD (LPCVD):**
- **Pressure:** 0.1 – 10 Torr.
- **Temperature:** 550 – 900 °C.
- **Activation:** Thermal (hot-wall furnace).
- **Application:** Polysilicon structural layers, Si₃N₄ masking layers, undoped SiO₂.
- **Advantage:** Excellent uniformity and conformality; the **workhorse** of MEMS.

**3. Plasma-Enhanced CVD (PECVD):**
- **Pressure:** 0.1 – 10 Torr.
- **Temperature:** 250 – 400 °C (much lower!).
- **Activation:** RF plasma (13.56 MHz).
- **Application:** Passivation layers, inter-metal dielectrics, low-temperature films over aluminium.
- **Advantage:** Low thermal budget — protects temperature-sensitive layers.

**4. Hot-Wire CVD (HWCVD):**
- **Activation:** Heated tungsten filament (~2000 °C) dissociates SiH₄.
- **Application:** High-quality amorphous silicon for solar cells and thin-film transistors.

**5. Metal-Organic CVD (MOCVD):**
- **Precursors:** Metal-organic compounds (e.g., TMGa, AsH₃).
- **Application:** Compound-semiconductor epitaxy (GaAs, InP, GaN) — used in LED and laser fabrication.

**6. Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD):**
- **Mechanism:** Sequential, self-limiting surface reactions.
- **Advantage:** Atomic-scale thickness control and pinhole-free films.
- **Application:** High-k dielectrics (HfO₂, Al₂O₃, ZrO₂) in advanced transistors and MEMS.

**7. Rapid-Thermal CVD (RTCVD):**
- **Activation:** Tungsten-halogen lamps heat the wafer for **seconds**.
- **Application:** Shallow junctions, selective epitaxial growth.

**Comparison Summary Table:**

| Variant | Pressure | Temperature | Best For |
|---------|----------|-------------|----------|
| APCVD | 760 Torr | 400 – 850 °C | Doped oxides |
| LPCVD | 0.1 – 10 Torr | 550 – 900 °C | Polysilicon, Si₃N₄ |
| PECVD | 0.1 – 10 Torr | 250 – 400 °C | Passivation, IMD |
| ALD | 0.1 – 10 Torr | 100 – 400 °C | High-k dielectrics |
| MOCVD | 10 – 760 Torr | 500 – 800 °C | III–V compounds |

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given Data:**
- $T = 620$ °C = $893$ K
- $E_a = 1.6$ eV
- $A = 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $P = 200$ mTorr = $0.200 \times 133.322$ Pa = $26.66$ Pa

**Step 1 — Convert $E_a$ to J/mol:**

$$
E_a = 1.6 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 154{,}348 \text{ J/mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $E_a / (R T)$:**

$$
\frac{E_a}{R T} = \frac{154{,}348}{8.314 \times 893} = \frac{154{,}348}{7424.4} = 20.79
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponential:**

$$
e^{-20.79} = 9.16 \times 10^{-10}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $k_s$:**

$$
k_s = (1.5 \times 10^8) \times (9.16 \times 10^{-10}) = 0.137 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_g$ using ideal-gas law:**

$$
C_g = \frac{P}{k_B T} = \frac{26.66}{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 893} = \frac{26.66}{1.232 \times 10^{-20}} = 2.164 \times 10^{21} \text{ molecules/m}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Compute deposition rate per unit area:**

$$
r = k_s \cdot C_g = 0.137 \times 2.164 \times 10^{21} = 2.965 \times 10^{20} \text{ atoms/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to thickness rate (using Si atom density $\approx 5.0 \times 10^{28}$ atoms/m³):**

$$
r_{thickness} = \frac{2.965 \times 10^{20}}{5.0 \times 10^{28}} = 5.93 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/s} \approx 5.9 \text{ nm/s}
$$

**Final Answer:** The deposition rate is approximately **5.9 nm/s**, or about **355 nm/min**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students often forget to convert eV → J/mol and Torr → Pa. Marks are deducted *per mistake*.
> 2. **Confusing the Two Constants:** Use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) for *molar* Arrhenius, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K for *per-molecule* ideal-gas. Mixing them is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Skipping Final Units:** Always state the *thickness* rate (nm/s or nm/min), not just the molecular rate. The examiner wants a *practical* number.
> 4. **Forgetting the Susceptor / Wafer Distinction:** In schematic questions, students frequently forget to label the *wafer*, the *heater*, and the *vacuum pump* — each omission costs one mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **CVD = Chemistry → Vapour → Deposit.** Memorise this phrase.
- The **five universal steps** of CVD are: gas transport → diffusion through boundary layer → surface adsorption + reaction → surface diffusion + film growth → by-product desorption and exhaust.
- The **two rate-limiting regimes** are *surface-reaction-limited* (low T, Arrhenius) and *mass-transport-limited* (high T, diffusion-controlled). Plotting $\ln(r)$ vs $1/T$ reveals both.
- The **Arrhenius equation** $k_s = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)}$ is the single most important equation on this topic.
- The **boundary layer thickness** scales as $\delta \sim \sqrt{D_{gb} \cdot x / u_\infty}$.
- **LPCVD** is the workhorse of MEMS — used for polysilicon, Si₃N₄, and undoped SiO₂.
- **PECVD** is used when the thermal budget is restricted (e.g., post-aluminium deposition).
- **APCVD** is fast but has poor step coverage.
- **ALD** is the gold standard for atomic-scale thickness control.
- **MOCVD** is reserved for III–V compound semiconductors.
- **Typical reaction chemistries to memorise:**
  - SiH₄ → Si + 2H₂ (polysilicon, LPCVD).
  - SiH₄ + O₂ → SiO₂ + 2H₂ (silane oxidation, APCVD/PECVD).
  - 3SiH₂Cl₂ + 4NH₃ → Si₃N₄ + 6HCl + 6H₂ (DCS-based nitride LPCVD).
  - SiH₄ + 4N₂O → SiO₂ + 4N₂ + 2H₂O (PECVD SiO₂ from N₂O).
- **Critical numerical constants** to memorise:
  - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
  - $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
  - $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol
  - 1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J
  - 1 Torr = 133.322 Pa
  - Si atomic density in solid ≈ $5.0 \times 10^{28}$ atoms/m³
- **Comparison axis to remember for PVD vs CVD:**
  - CVD: chemical reaction, conformal, higher temperature, slower.
  - PVD (sputtering / evaporation): physical, directional, lower temperature, faster.
- **Last-minute exam mantra:** "Five steps, two regimes, Arrhenius, boundary layer, polysilicon via silane, LPCVD is the king of MEMS."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Etching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT742 - INTRODUCTION TO MEMS
