> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Etching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Etching** is a fundamental subtractive microfabrication process used in MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) and semiconductor manufacturing to selectively remove material from a substrate (typically silicon, glass, or a thin film) using chemical, physical, or a combination of chemical-physical mechanisms. The process is governed by a **pre-deposited etch mask** (photoresist, SiO₂, Si₃N₄, or metal) that protects selected regions while exposed areas are dissolved or sputtered away.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, etching is formally classified into two primary branches:
1. **Wet Etching** – Liquid-phase chemical dissolution
2. **Dry Etching** – Vapor-phase or plasma-assisted physical/chemical removal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Etching forms the core of Module 4 because it directly determines the **sidewall profile**, **critical dimension (CD) control**, and **aspect ratio** of every MEMS structure (cantilevers, membranes, channels, and comb drives).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of etching like **carving a sculpture out of a marble block**:
- The **marble block** = your silicon wafer (the bulk material).
- The **protective clay coating** you apply on parts you want to keep = the **etch mask** (photoresist or hard mask).
- The **acid or chisel** that removes the exposed marble = the **etchant or plasma**.
- The **speed of removal** = **etch rate** (measured in nm/min or µm/min).
- The **invisible sideways biting under the mask** = **undercut** (a critical problem in MEMS).

In **wet etching**, imagine dripping lemon juice (acid) onto chalk — it dissolves in all directions equally (**isotropic**). In **dry etching**, imagine a sandblaster (ions) shooting straight down — it cuts a clean vertical hole (**anisotropic**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Etching Matters in MEMS:** MEMS devices like pressure sensors, accelerometers, and microfluidic chips rely on precisely etched cavities, diaphragms, and through-wafer vias. Without accurate etching, the mechanical properties (spring constant, resonant frequency) of the device would be impossible to reproduce.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Unit | Typical MEMS Value |
|---|---|---|
| Etch Rate | nm/min or µm/min | 100 – 5000 |
| Selectivity | dimensionless ratio | 10:1 to 1000:1 |
| Undercut | µm | < 0.5 (target) |
| Aspect Ratio | depth / width | 1:1 to 50:1 |
| Uniformity | ± % across wafer | ± 2% to ± 5% |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Etching Techniques

### A. Wet Chemical Etching

Wet etching uses **liquid chemical solutions** to dissolve the exposed material. The process can be further divided based on the directionality of material removal.

#### A.1 Isotropic Wet Etching
- Removes material **equally in all directions**.
- Etch rate is **identical** along all crystal planes.
- **Example:** HNA (Hydrofluoric-Nitric-Acetic acid) for silicon, buffered HF for SiO₂.
- Produces **significant undercut** beneath the mask edges.
- **Profile Shape:** Circular/curved sidewalls.

#### A.2 Anisotropic Wet Etching
- Etch rate **depends on crystallographic orientation**.
- Exploits the differing atomic densities of crystal planes.
- **Example:** KOH (Potassium Hydroxide), TMAH (Tetramethylammonium Hydroxide), EDP (Ethylenediamine Pyrocatechol) on silicon.
- **Key Property:** {111} planes etch ~100× slower than {100} or {110} planes.
- **Profile Shape:** V-shaped grooves (54.74° sidewalls) or vertical walls.

### B. Dry Etching (Plasma-Based)

Dry etching uses **gas-phase reactants and/or energetic ions** in a low-pressure plasma environment.

#### B.1 Physical Dry Etching — Ion Beam Etching (IBE) / Sputter Etching
- Pure momentum transfer from inert gas ions (typically Ar⁺) to substrate atoms.
- **Highly anisotropic** but **low selectivity**.
- Used for inert materials (gold, platinum, magnetic alloys).

#### B.2 Chemical Dry Etching — Plasma Etching
- Uses **reactive gas species** (e.g., CF₄, SF₆) that chemically react with the substrate to form volatile products.
- Typically **isotropic** if purely chemical.
- **Example:** Si + 4F → SiF₄ (gas, volatile).

#### B.3 Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)
- **Combination** of chemical reactivity and physical ion bombardment.
- **Most widely used** dry etching technique in MEMS.
- Achieves good anisotropy with reasonable selectivity.
- **Example Process:** SF₆ + O₂ plasma for silicon.

#### B.4 Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) — Bosch Process
- Alternates between **etching (SF₆)** and **passivation (C₄F₈)** cycles.
- Produces **near-vertical sidewalls** with aspect ratios up to **50:1**.
- Scalloping effect (small undulations) on sidewalls is characteristic.
- Critical for **through-silicon vias (TSV)** and high-aspect-ratio MEMS.

## 2.2 Critical Performance Parameters

| Parameter | Definition | Engineering Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Etch Rate (ER)** | Material thickness removed per unit time | Determines process throughput |
| **Selectivity (S)** | Ratio of etch rates between target material and mask | Mask integrity and feature fidelity |
| **Anisotropy (A)** | Degree of directional preference | Sidewall verticality and CD control |
| **Uniformity** | Variation in etch depth across wafer | Yield and device consistency |
| **Undercut (U)** | Lateral etching beneath the mask | Critical dimension error |
| **Aspect Ratio** | Depth-to-width ratio of etched feature | Determines etch mechanism |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Etch Rate | $ER = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}$ | Depth removed per unit time | nm/min or µm/min |
| Selectivity | $S = \dfrac{ER_{\text{target}}}{ER_{\text{mask}}}$ | Ratio of substrate to mask etch rate | dimensionless |
| Anisotropy | $A = 1 - \dfrac{ER_{\text{lateral}}}{ER_{\text{vertical}}}$ | 0 = isotropic, 1 = fully anisotropic | dimensionless |
| Isotropic Undercut | $U = ER_{\text{lateral}} \times t$ | Sideways etch under mask | µm |
| Etch Depth (Anisotropic) | $d = \dfrac{W_{0}}{2} \cdot \tan(54.74^\circ)$ | V-groove depth from mask opening on (100) Si | µm |
| Lateral Etch (Isotropic) | $X_f = 2 \cdot ER \cdot t$ | Total horizontal etching | µm |
| Aspect Ratio | $AR = \dfrac{d}{w}$ | Depth / width of feature | dimensionless |
| Concentration Effect (Wet) | $ER \propto C^n$ | Empirical rate vs etchant concentration | varies |
| Arrhenius Relation | $ER = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Temperature dependence of etch rate | nm/min |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Tip:** The **anisotropy equation** $A = 1 - (ER_{\text{lateral}} / ER_{\text{vertical}})$ is a **favourite KTU question**. Remember: $A = 1$ means perfect vertical etch (RIE/DRIE), $A = 0$ means perfectly isotropic (HNA).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Etching enables the fabrication of:
- **Pressure sensor diaphragms** (backside KOH etching of silicon)
- **Microfluidic channels** (DRIE on glass/silicon)
- **Comb-drive accelerometers** (DRIE for high aspect ratio)
- **Through-Silicon Vias (TSV)** for 3D packaging
- **MEMS microphones and gyroscopes** (precise release etching)
- **Bio-MEMS cantilevers** for DNA/Protein detection
- **RF MEMS switches** (gold etching using KI/I₂)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Etch Depth in Anisotropic Wet Etching of (100) Silicon

When silicon with **(100) orientation** is etched in KOH through a rectangular mask opening of width $W_0$, the {111} planes act as etch stops because they etch ~100× slower than {100}.

**Step 1: Identify the etching planes.**
- The exposed surface is the (100) plane (top of wafer).
- The fast-etching planes are {100} family (vertical and horizontal).
- The slow-etching planes are {111} family (inclined at 54.74°).

**Step 2: Geometric relationship.**
The {111} planes intersect the (100) surface at an angle of **54.74°**. This is the well-known angle between the (100) and (111) planes in cubic silicon.

**Step 3: Trigonometric formulation.**
Consider a cross-section through the mask opening. As the etch proceeds, two sloped {111} planes meet at the bottom.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(54.74^\circ) &= \frac{\text{etch depth } (d)}{\text{half of mask opening } (W_0 / 2)} \\[6pt]
d &= \frac{W_0}{2} \cdot \tan(54.74^\circ) \\[6pt]
\tan(54.74^\circ) &\approx 1.414 \;\;(\text{exact value: } \sqrt{2}) \\[6pt]
d &= \frac{W_0}{2} \cdot \sqrt{2} = \frac{W_0}{\sqrt{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical example.**
If $W_0 = 200\,\mu m$, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{200}{2} \times 1.414 \\[4pt]
d &= 100 \times 1.414 \\[4pt]
d &= 141.4\;\mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **maximum etch depth** before the V-groove is fully formed and etching effectively stops (self-limiting).

## 3.2 Derivation: Selectivity Calculation

**Step 1: Define the scenario.**
Suppose you are etching silicon in KOH with a SiO₂ mask. Given:
- $ER_{\text{Si}} = 1.0\;\mu m / min$
- $ER_{\text{SiO}_2} = 0.02\;\mu m / min$

**Step 2: Apply selectivity formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{ER_{\text{Si}}}{ER_{\text{SiO}_2}} \\[6pt]
S &= \frac{1.0}{0.02} \\[6pt]
S &= 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result.**
A selectivity of **50:1** means for every 1 µm of silicon etched, only 0.02 µm of SiO₂ mask is consumed. If you need to etch 50 µm of silicon, your SiO₂ mask must be at least 1 µm thick to survive.

**Step 4: Mask thickness requirement.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{mask}} &= \frac{d_{\text{desired}}}{S} \times \text{safety factor} \\[6pt]
t_{\text{mask}} &= \frac{50}{50} \times 1.5 = 1.5\;\mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

A safety factor of 1.5× is standard in KTU-grade process design.

## 3.3 Derivation: Anisotropy Index

**Step 1: Definition.**
The anisotropy index quantifies the directional preference of an etching process.

$$
A = 1 - \frac{ER_{\text{lateral}}}{ER_{\text{vertical}}}
$$

**Step 2: Case analysis.**

**Case 1: Isotropic Wet Etching (e.g., HNA on Si)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ER_{\text{lateral}} &= ER_{\text{vertical}} = 1\;\mu m/min \\[4pt]
A &= 1 - \frac{1}{1} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $A = 0$ → perfectly isotropic, equal undercut and vertical etching.

**Case 2: Anisotropic Wet Etching (e.g., KOH on Si)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ER_{\text{vertical}} &= 1.4\;\mu m/min \\[4pt]
ER_{\text{lateral}} &= 0.014\;\mu m/min \\[4pt]
A &= 1 - \frac{0.014}{1.4} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $A = 0.99$ → highly anisotropic, near-vertical sidewalls.

**Case 3: DRIE (Bosch Process)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ER_{\text{vertical}} &= 3\;\mu m/min \\[4pt]
ER_{\text{lateral}} &= 0.003\;\mu m/min \\[4pt]
A &= 1 - \frac{0.003}{3} = 0.999
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $A = 0.999$ → near-perfectly anisotropic, suitable for 50:1 aspect ratio structures.

## 3.4 Derivation: Undercut in Isotropic Etching

**Step 1: Setup.**
A mask of width $W_0$ is used for isotropic wet etching. The etch proceeds downward and sideways at the same rate.

**Step 2: At time t, the etch front forms a circular arc.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Vertical depth:} \quad & d = ER \times t \\[4pt]
\text{Lateral undercut:} \quad & U = ER \times t \\[4pt]
\text{Total undercut (both sides):} \quad & U_{\text{total}} = 2 \cdot ER \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Effective feature width after undercut.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{effective}} &= W_0 - 2U \\[4pt]
W_{\text{effective}} &= W_0 - 2 \cdot ER \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical example.**
Given $W_0 = 100\;\mu m$, $ER = 0.5\;\mu m / min$, $t = 30\;\text{min}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= 0.5 \times 30 = 15\;\mu m \\[4pt]
W_{\text{effective}} &= 100 - 2(15) = 100 - 30 = 70\;\mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** A **30% reduction** in feature width due to undercut — a major concern in KTU design problems.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Etching Techniques

| Property | Wet Isotropic | Wet Anisotropic | RIE | DRIE (Bosch) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Directionality | Isotropic | Anisotropic | Anisotropic | Highly Anisotropic |
| Etch Rate (Si) | 0.1 – 2 µm/min | 0.5 – 1.5 µm/min | 0.1 – 1 µm/min | 2 – 10 µm/min |
| Selectivity (vs SiO₂) | High (>100) | Very High (>200) | Moderate (20–50) | Moderate (50–100) |
| Aspect Ratio | < 1:1 | < 1:1 (V-groove) | < 5:1 | Up to 50:1 |
| Undercut | Large | Small (V-shape) | Minimal | Minimal |
| Equipment Cost | Low | Low | High | Very High |
| Vacuum Required | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| CMOS Compatible | Limited | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Typical Use | Bulk Si, glass | Diaphragms, V-grooves | General MEMS | TSV, comb drives |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Etching Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ETCHING] --> B[Wet Etching]
    A --> C[Dry Etching]
    B --> B1[Isotropic]
    B --> B2[Anisotropic]
    B1 --> B1a[HNA for Si]
    B1 --> B1b[BHF for SiO2]
    B2 --> B2a[KOH on Si]
    B2 --> B2b[TMAH on Si]
    B2 --> B2c[EDP on Si]
    C --> C1[Physical]
    C --> C2[Chemical]
    C --> C3[Reactive Ion Etching RIE]
    C1 --> C1a[Ion Beam Milling]
    C1 --> C1b[Sputter Etching]
    C2 --> C2a[Plasma Etching]
    C2 --> C2b[Barrel Etcher]
    C3 --> C3a[RIE]
    C3 --> C3b[DRIE Bosch Process]
    C3b --> C3b1[Etch Cycle SF6]
    C3b --> C3b2[Passivation Cycle C4F8]
```

## 4.2 Etch Profile Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ISO[Isotropic Wet Etch]
        A1[Mask] --> A2[Exposed Si]
        A2 --> A3[Circular undercut profile]
    end
    subgraph ANI[Anisotropic Wet Etch KOH 100 Si]
        B1[Mask] --> B2[Exposed Si 100 plane]
        B2 --> B3[V groove 54.74 degree sidewall]
    end
    subgraph DRIE[DRIE Bosch]
        C1[Mask] --> C2[Exposed Si]
        C2 --> C3[Vertical sidewall aspect 50 to 1]
    end
```

## 4.3 Bosch DRIE Process Cycle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1 SF6 Plasma Etch] --> P2[Si isotropically removed small amount]
    P2 --> P3[Step 2 C4F8 Passivation]
    P3 --> P4[Teflon like polymer coats all surfaces]
    P4 --> P5[Step 3 SF6 Ion Bombardment]
    P5 --> P6[Bottom polymer removed ions directional]
    P6 --> P7[Sidewall polymer remains protects lateral etch]
    P7 --> P8{Repeat cycle 100 to 500 times}
    P8 -->|Yes| P1
    P8 -->|No| P9[Final deep vertical structure with scallops]
```

## 4.4 RIE System Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[RF Power Supply 13.56 MHz] --> R2[Match Network]
    R2 --> R3[Upper Electrode Cathode]
    R4[Process Gases SF6 O2] --> R3
    R3 --> R5[Plasma Generation Chamber]
    R5 --> R6[Wafer Substrate Anode]
    R6 --> R7[Vacuum Pump]
    R5 --> R8[Reactive Ions Vertical Bombardment]
    R8 --> R9[Volatile Byproducts SiF4 Exit]
    R9 --> R7
```

## 4.5 Mask Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1{What material to etch} --> M2[Silicon]
    M1 --> M3[SiO2]
    M1 --> M4[Metal Al Au]
    M2 --> M5{Required aspect ratio}
    M5 -->|less than 1 to 1| M6[KOH Anisotropic Wet]
    M5 -->|1 to 1 to 10 to 1| M7[RIE Dry]
    M5 -->|more than 10 to 1| M8[DRIE Bosch]
    M3 --> M9[BHF Wet]
    M4 --> M10[Wet acid or IBE]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Etching. List any four wet etchants used for silicon and specify which one is anisotropic.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Etching** is a subtractive microfabrication process in which material is selectively removed from a substrate using chemical (wet) or physical-chemical (dry) means, controlled by a pre-patterned mask. (1 mark)

**Four wet etchants for silicon:**
1. **HNA (HF + HNO₃ + CH₃COOH)** – Isotropic
2. **KOH (Potassium Hydroxide)** – Anisotropic
3. **TMAH (Tetramethylammonium Hydroxide)** – Anisotropic
4. **EDP (Ethylenediamine Pyrocatechol)** – Anisotropic (1 mark)

**KOH is the most common anisotropic wet etchant** for silicon, exploiting the ~100× slower etch rate on {111} planes compared to {100} planes. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is selectivity in etching? Why is it a critical parameter in MEMS fabrication?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Selectivity (S)** is defined as the ratio of the etch rate of the target material to the etch rate of the mask material: $S = ER_{\text{target}} / ER_{\text{mask}}$. (1.5 marks)

**Why it is critical:**
1. **Mask integrity:** Low selectivity causes the mask to erode, leading to loss of feature fidelity and contamination. (0.75 mark)
2. **Process predictability:** High selectivity allows the engineer to know exactly how deep the material will be etched before the mask fails. (0.75 mark)

For example, in DRIE, a SiO₂ mask of 1 µm can protect silicon during ~50 µm of etching if selectivity is 50:1.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **isotropic and anisotropic wet etching** processes of silicon. Compare their etch profiles and discuss the role of {111} planes in anisotropic etching. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A (100) silicon wafer is patterned with a square opening of side **400 µm** and etched in KOH at 80°C. Calculate: (i) the maximum etch depth before self-stoppage, (ii) the time required if the etch rate is **1.2 µm/min**, and (iii) the etch rate of the SiO₂ mask if the selectivity is **60:1**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Isotropic Wet Etching:**
- Uses chemical solutions like **HNA** (HF + HNO₃ + CH₃COOH) or **BHF** for SiO₂.
- The etchant attacks the silicon **equally in all directions** (lateral = vertical etch rate).
- Produces a **circular/curved undercut** beneath the mask edges.
- The lateral etch distance equals the vertical etch depth.

**Anisotropic Wet Etching:**
- Uses alkaline solutions like **KOH, TMAH, EDP**.
- Etch rate is **crystallographic orientation-dependent**.
- The {111} planes etch ~100× slower than {100} planes because of higher atomic density and lower density of dangling bonds.
- Sidewalls terminate on {111} planes, producing **V-shaped grooves** with 54.74° wall angles on (100) wafers.
- **Self-stopping** occurs when all exposed {100} surfaces are consumed by slow {111} planes.

**Diagram explanation:**
- Isotropic: shows semicircular undercut beneath mask.
- Anisotropic: shows V-groove with 54.74° slope, {111} planes meeting at the bottom.

**Role of {111} planes:**
- Act as natural **etch stops** due to slow etch rate.
- Their inclination to (100) surface is fixed at **54.74°**.
- Determine the geometry of diaphragms, V-grooves, and pyramidal pits.
- Enable precision in pressure sensor fabrication.

**(Marks distribution: 1.5 Isotropic + 2.0 Anisotropic + 1.5 {111} role + 2.0 Diagrams)**

#### Model Solution (b):

**(i) Maximum etch depth:**

For a square opening of side $W_0 = 400\,\mu m$ on (100) Si, the V-groove forms completely when the {111} planes meet at the center.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{max}} &= \frac{W_0}{2} \cdot \tan(54.74^\circ) \\[6pt]
d_{\text{max}} &= \frac{400}{2} \times 1.414 \\[6pt]
d_{\text{max}} &= 200 \times 1.414 \\[6pt]
d_{\text{max}} &= 282.8\;\mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Time required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{d_{\text{max}}}{ER_{\text{Si}}} \\[6pt]
t &= \frac{282.8}{1.2} \\[6pt]
t &= 235.7\;\text{min} \approx 3.93\;\text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) SiO₂ mask etch rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{ER_{\text{Si}}}{ER_{\text{SiO}_2}} \\[6pt]
ER_{\text{SiO}_2} &= \frac{ER_{\text{Si}}}{S} = \frac{1.2}{60} \\[6pt]
ER_{\text{SiO}_2} &= 0.02\;\mu m / min
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mask consumption during this etch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mask loss} &= ER_{\text{SiO}_2} \times t = 0.02 \times 235.7 \\[4pt]
\text{Mask loss} &= 4.71\;\mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

So the SiO₂ mask must be at least **5 µm thick** (with safety factor).

**(Marks distribution: 2 + 2 + 3 = 7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Describe the **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** process with a schematic. Explain the **Bosch process** for Deep RIE and discuss its application in fabricating high-aspect-ratio MEMS structures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **anisotropy index** for an etching process with $ER_{\text{vertical}} = 2.5\;\mu m / min$ and $ER_{\text{lateral}} = 0.025\;\mu m / min$. Comment on the suitability of this process for fabricating a MEMS comb-drive with 20 µm wide, 200 µm deep trenches. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Reactive Ion Etching (RIE):**
- RIE is a **dry etching** technique that combines **chemical reactivity** of plasma-generated species with **physical ion bombardment**.
- The wafer is placed on the **powered electrode (cathode)**; the counter-electrode is grounded.
- **RF power (13.56 MHz)** creates a plasma of process gas (e.g., SF₆ + O₂ for silicon).
- A **self-bias DC voltage** develops on the cathode, accelerating positive ions **vertically** toward the wafer.
- The result: **directional (anisotropic) etching** with reasonable selectivity.

**Schematic (as described in Section 4.4):** RF source → match network → cathode → plasma chamber → wafer on cathode → grounded anode → vacuum pump.

**Bosch Process (DRIE):**
- Invented by **Robert Bosch GmbH** to achieve **near-vertical sidewalls** with aspect ratios up to **50:1**.
- Uses **alternating cycles** of two gases:
  1. **Etch cycle (SF₆):** Isotropic chemical etching of silicon (depth ~1 µm per cycle).
  2. **Passivation cycle (C₄F₈):** Deposits a Teflon-like polymer film on all surfaces.
- During the next etch cycle, **ion bombardment removes the polymer only from horizontal (bottom) surfaces**, leaving vertical sidewalls protected.
- Repeating the cycle 100–500 times produces deep, vertical trenches.
- The process leaves a **scalloped sidewall texture** (typical amplitude 50–200 nm).

**Applications in MEMS:**
- **Comb-drive accelerometers** – high-aspect-ratio fingers for large electrostatic area.
- **Gyroscopes** – deep trenches for high mass and inertial sensitivity.
- **Through-Silicon Vias (TSV)** for 3D IC stacking.
- **Microfluidic channels** with high depth-to-width ratio.
- **RF MEMS** – precise vertical structures for capacitive switches.

**(Marks distribution: 2 RIE + 2 Bosch process + 1 schematic + 2 applications)**

#### Model Solution (b):

**Anisotropy Index Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 1 - \frac{ER_{\text{lateral}}}{ER_{\text{vertical}}} \\[6pt]
A &= 1 - \frac{0.025}{2.5} \\[6pt]
A &= 1 - 0.01 \\[6pt]
A &= 0.99
\end{aligned}
$$

**Required aspect ratio for the comb-drive:**

$$
AR_{\text{required}} = \frac{\text{depth}}{\text{width}} = \frac{200}{20} = 10:1
$$

**Comment on Suitability:**
- An anisotropy of **0.99 (99%)** is **excellent** and characteristic of a **DRIE-grade process** (Bosch process level).
- The required aspect ratio of **10:1** is well within the capabilities of DRIE (which routinely achieves 30:1–50:1).
- **Lateral etch (undercut) over the full process time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{200\;\mu m}{2.5\;\mu m/\text{min}} = 80\;\text{min} \\[4pt]
U_{\text{per side}} &= 0.025 \times 80 = 2.0\;\mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

- This **2 µm undercut** on each side is acceptable (only 10% of the 20 µm feature width).
- The process is **suitable** for fabricating the comb-drive. To improve fidelity, a hard mask (SiO₂ or metal) should be used due to the long etch time.

**(Marks distribution: 2.5 formula + 1.5 calculation + 3 suitability analysis)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Anisotropy:** Students often write $A = (ER_{\text{vertical}} / ER_{\text{lateral}}) - 1$ — this is WRONG. The correct formula is $A = 1 - (ER_{\text{lateral}} / ER_{\text{vertical}})$. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **V-Groove Angle:** Many students write 45° instead of **54.74°**. This is a very common KTU trap. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing µm/min and nm/min without conversion. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Confusing Wet vs Dry Etching Directionality:** KOH is **anisotropic** (wet); HNA is **isotropic** (wet). RIE and DRIE are **anisotropic** (dry). Loss: **2 marks**.
> 5. **Skipping Safety Factor in Mask Thickness:** KTU examiners reward explicit mention of a safety factor (typically 1.5×). Loss: **1 mark**.
> 6. **No Diagram for Etch Profiles:** Always draw a labeled cross-section showing the mask, etch front, and undercut/V-groove. Loss: **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Etching** is a subtractive process; it is the **complement of deposition/lithography** in MEMS fabrication.
- **Wet etching** uses liquid chemicals; **dry etching** uses plasma/vapor.
- **Isotropic** etching = equal etch rate in all directions; **Anisotropic** = direction-dependent.
- **HNA** is isotropic; **KOH, TMAH, EDP** are anisotropic wet etchants.
- **{111} planes** in silicon etch ~100× slower than {100} planes → natural etch stop.
- The angle between (100) and (111) planes is **54.74°**, not 45°.
- V-groove depth: $d = (W_0 / 2) \times \tan(54.74^\circ) = W_0 / \sqrt{2}$.
- **Etch Rate** = $\Delta d / \Delta t$ (units: µm/min or nm/min).
- **Selectivity** = $ER_{\text{target}} / ER_{\text{mask}}$ (dimensionless, must be > 10 for practical use).
- **Anisotropy Index** = $1 - (ER_{\text{lateral}} / ER_{\text{vertical}})$.
- **Undercut** in isotropic etching = $ER \times t$ per side; total undercut doubles it.
- **RIE** combines chemical plasma etching with physical ion bombardment for anisotropy.
- **DRIE / Bosch process** alternates SF₆ etch and C₄F₈ passivation → aspect ratios up to 50:1.
- **DRIE leaves scallops** on sidewalls — a hallmark of Bosch process.
- **Mask materials:** Photoresist (low selectivity, easy to strip), SiO₂ (high selectivity, common), Si₃N₄ (very high selectivity), metals (Al, Cr — for harsh etches).
- **Aspect Ratio Dependent Etching (ARDE):** Etch rate drops at high aspect ratios due to limited transport of reactants — important in deep DRIE.
- **Stiction** is a release-etch concern for free-standing MEMS structures.
- **CMOS compatibility:** TMAH and DRIE are CMOS-compatible; KOH contains mobile ions (Na⁺, K⁺) that can damage CMOS — a key KTU point.
- **Safety & process economics:** Wet etching is **cheap and simple**; dry etching is **expensive but precise**.
- **Typical KTU application links:** Pressure sensor diaphragm (KOH), Comb-drive (DRIE), Microfluidic channels (DRIE/glass etching), Cantilever release (isotropic Si etch).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Micro Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT742 - INTRODUCTION TO MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview of Micro Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Micro Manufacturing (Micromanufacturing)** is defined as the fabrication of products and components with features, tolerances, and/or critical dimensions measurable at the **micrometer scale (typically 1 µm to 1000 µm)**, encompassing a family of advanced processes that create Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS), microsensors, microactuators, and micro-scale functional structures.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus, micro manufacturing is broadly classified into three functional categories:

| Classification | Definition | Typical Feature Size |
|----------------|------------|---------------------|
| **Micro-precision Manufacturing** | Production of high-precision micro-components with ultra-tight tolerances. | 1 µm – 999 µm |
| **MEMS Manufacturing** | Integrated fabrication of mechanical and electronic elements on a common substrate. | 0.1 µm – 100 µm |
| **Micro-scale Manufacturing** | Mass production of miniature parts with relaxed tolerances. | > 100 µm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 4 emphasizes the *need for micro manufacturing*, the *comparison with conventional macro-manufacturing*, and an *overview of key process technologies* — bulk micromachining, surface micromachining, LIGA, and non-silicon processes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> Think of micro manufacturing as **"building a watch movement inside a grain of sand."** A traditional machinist uses a lathe or milling machine to cut steel blocks into gears. In contrast, a MEMS engineer **etches away** material chemically, **deposits** thin films layer-by-layer (like a 3D printing process at atomic scale), or uses **X-rays to mold plastic into metallic shapes** — producing moving mechanical devices that fit on the tip of a needle.

Imagine sculpting a miniature cathedral:
- **Conventional Manufacturing** = A stonemason using a hammer and chisel on a large block.
- **Micro Manufacturing** = A craftsman using corrosive acid droplets, X-ray shadows, and atomic-layer deposition tools to grow the cathedral from sand one atom at a time.

## 1.3 Why Micro Manufacturing? — The Driving Forces

The transition from macro- to micro-scale manufacturing is driven by four fundamental engineering demands:

1. **Miniaturization** — Portable devices (smartphones, implants) require tiny components.
2. **Functionality per Volume** — More functions packed in less space (IoT, lab-on-chip).
3. **Performance Enhancement** — At micro-scale, mechanical resonance frequencies, thermal response times, and electrical capacitances scale favorably (e.g., stiffness reduces by a factor of $N^2$ if dimensions scale by $1/N$).
4. **Cost Reduction via Batch Fabrication** — Hundreds of devices fabricated simultaneously on a single wafer (parallel processing reduces cost-per-unit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (Bolded Constants):** The semiconductor industry's reference wafer diameter is **200 mm (8-inch)** or **300 mm (12-inch)**. A single 300 mm wafer can yield **>10,000 MEMS devices**, demonstrating the power of **batch fabrication economics**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comparison: Conventional vs. Micro Manufacturing

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Focus:** This comparison is a **frequently asked 7-mark question** in KTU examinations (Part B Module 4). Memorize the table below.

| Parameter | Conventional Manufacturing | Micro Manufacturing |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Feature Size** | mm to meters | µm to nm |
| **Tolerance** | ±10 µm to ±0.1 mm | ±0.01 µm to ±1 µm |
| **Process Energy** | Mechanical (cutting, forming) | Chemical, optical, thermal, ion-based |
| **Material Removal** | Subtractive (machining) | Subtractive (etching) + Additive (deposition) |
| **Tooling** | Macro tools (drill bits, end mills) | Masks, photoresist, etchants, deposition chambers |
| **Production Volume** | Low to medium | Ultra-high (batch/wafer-level) |
| **Cost Driver** | Labor + material | Capital equipment + cleanroom |
| **Aspect Ratio** | Low to moderate (< 10:1) | Very high (> 100:1 possible in DRIE/LIGA) |
| **Material Range** | Most metals, plastics | Primarily silicon; also polymers, ceramics, metals |

## 2.2 Classification of Micro Manufacturing Processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Micro manufacturing processes fall into **three primary families**, each forming a foundation for the rest of Module 4 in KTU 2024.

### A. Silicon-Based Micromachining
- **Bulk Micromachining** — Subtractive etching of the silicon *substrate itself* to form 3D structures (e.g., pressure sensor diaphragms, microchannels).
- **Surface Micromachining** — Building mechanical structures using *thin films deposited* on the substrate, then selectively *releasing* the movable parts by sacrificial layer etching.

### B. Non-Silicon (Specialty) Micromachining
- **LIGA** (Lithographie, Galvanoformung, Abformung — German for *lithography, electroplating, molding*).
- **Micro-EDM** (Micro-Electrical Discharge Machining).
- **Laser Micromachining** (UV laser ablation, femtosecond lasers).

### C. Hybrid / Additive Microfabrication
- **3D Printing / Micro-stereolithography**.
- **Micro-injection molding** of thermoplastics.
- **Soft lithography** (PDMS-based).

## 2.3 Fundamental Scaling Laws — The Physics of Miniaturization

When a dimension is scaled by a factor of $1/N$, all other physical quantities scale according to power laws. This is the **mathematical foundation** that explains why micro devices behave fundamentally differently from their macro counterparts.

### Derivation Setup

Let original linear dimension be $L_0$. New dimension $L = L_0 / N$, where $N > 1$ (miniaturization factor).

### A. Area Scaling
Area is a 2D quantity:

$$A = L^2 = \left(\frac{L_0}{N}\right)^2 = \frac{A_0}{N^2}$$

### B. Volume Scaling
Volume is a 3D quantity:

$$V = L^3 = \left(\frac{L_0}{N}\right)^3 = \frac{V_0}{N^3}$$

### C. Surface-to-Volume Ratio Scaling
The ratio $S/V$ scales as:

$$\frac{S}{V} = \frac{L^2}{L^3} = \frac{1}{L} = \frac{N}{L_0}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** As size decreases, the **surface-to-volume ratio increases inversely with length**. This is why **micro heat exchangers** and **microfluidic devices** are so efficient — surface-driven forces (friction, heat transfer, surface tension) dominate over body forces (inertia, gravity).

### D. Mass and Inertia Scaling
Mass $m$ depends on volume and material density $\rho$:

$$m = \rho \cdot V = \frac{m_0}{N^3}$$

### E. Stiffness (Spring Constant) Scaling
For a beam under bending, stiffness $k$ depends on cross-section area and length:

$$k = \frac{E \cdot w \cdot t^3}{L^3}$$

When all linear dimensions scale by $1/N$:

$$k_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{E \cdot (w_0/N) \cdot (t_0/N)^3}{(L_0/N)^3} = \frac{E w_0 t_0^3}{L_0^3} \cdot \frac{N^4}{N^4} = k_0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surprising Result:** When *all* dimensions scale uniformly, the spring constant is **invariant**. But when only one dimension scales (e.g., thinning a cantilever), $k$ drops by $N^3$. This makes MEMS structures **inherently compliant**.

### F. Natural Frequency Scaling
The natural frequency of a spring-mass system is:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

Substituting scaled $k$ and $m$:

$$\omega_{n,\text{scaled}} = \sqrt{\frac{k_0}{m_0 / N^3}} = \sqrt{N^3 \cdot \frac{k_0}{m_0}} = N^{3/2} \cdot \omega_{n,0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Natural frequency scales as $N^{3/2}$. Halving all dimensions ($N=2$) increases resonance frequency by a factor of $\approx 2.83$. This is why **MEMS gyroscopes and resonators** can operate in the **kHz to MHz range** with millimeter-sized chips.

### G. Electrostatic Force Scaling
Electrostatic force depends on area and inverse square of gap:

$$F_e = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{d^2}$$

When linear dimensions scale by $1/N$:

$$F_{e,\text{scaled}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A_0 V^2}{N^2 \cdot d_0^2} = \frac{F_{e,0}}{N^2}$$

Meanwhile, mass drops by $N^3$. So the **force-to-weight ratio** scales as:

$$\frac{F_e}{mg} \propto \frac{1/N^2}{1/N^3} = N$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Electrostatic actuation becomes *more efficient* at micro-scale. This is why **capacitive MEMS actuators** (comb drives, parallel-plate actuators) dominate over electromagnetic actuation in microsystems — the latter scales poorly.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Scaling Factor (when $L \to L/N$) | Engineering Implication |
|---------|---------|-----------------------------------|------------------------|
| $A = L^2$ | Cross-sectional area | $1/N^2$ | Drag, friction forces decrease |
| $V = L^3$ | Volume | $1/N^3$ | Mass and inertia shrink rapidly |
| $S/V \propto 1/L$ | Surface-to-volume ratio | $N$ | Surface effects dominate at small scale |
| $m = \rho V$ | Mass | $1/N^3$ | Low inertia → fast response |
| $k$ (uniform scaling) | Stiffness | $1$ (invariant) | Compliance increases relatively |
| $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency | $N^{3/2}$ | Higher frequencies achievable |
| $F_e = \varepsilon_0 A V^2 / (2d^2)$ | Electrostatic force | $1/N^2$ | $F/m$ improves at micro-scale |
| $P_{\text{drag}} \propto \mu A v$ | Viscous drag | $1/N^2$ | Damping rises; air damping critical |
| $R_e = \rho v L / \mu$ | Reynolds number | $1/N$ | Micro flows are **laminar** |
| $\tau_{\text{thermal}} \propto L^2 / \alpha$ | Thermal time constant | $1/N^2$ | Faster thermal response |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ (permittivity of free space), $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu$ in $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $\alpha$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Mass-Produced Wafer Yield Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Problem Style):**
> A 150 mm diameter silicon wafer is used to fabricate a MEMS pressure sensor. Each die (single device) occupies a rectangular area of $3 \text{ mm} \times 4 \text{ mm}$. Assuming the wafer has a flat edge of 50 mm length (non-usable) and a 3 mm edge exclusion zone, calculate the total number of good dies per wafer.

### Step 1: Convert wafer diameter to usable area
The wafer diameter is $D = 150$ mm. The edge exclusion zone is $e = 3$ mm on all sides. Therefore, the usable diameter is:

$$D_{\text{usable}} = D - 2e = 150 - 2(3) = 144 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2: Compute usable wafer area (treating as full circle)
The usable circular area is:

$$A_{\text{usable}} = \frac{\pi D_{\text{usable}}^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (144)^2}{4}$$

Let us evaluate this:

$$A_{\text{usable}} = \frac{\pi \times 20736}{4} = \pi \times 5184 = 16286.016 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 3: Account for the flat edge
The wafer's primary flat has length $L_f = 50$ mm. The area of the flat is approximately a chord cut from the circle. However, since the edge exclusion already covers 3 mm from any edge including the flat, **we conservatively subtract a strip of area** of length $L_f$ and width $e$:

$$A_{\text{flat loss}} = L_f \times e = 50 \times 3 = 150 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 4: Net usable area
$$A_{\text{net}} = A_{\text{usable}} - A_{\text{flat loss}} = 16286.016 - 150 = 16136.016 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 5: Area of a single die
Each die occupies:

$$A_{\text{die}} = 3 \text{ mm} \times 4 \text{ mm} = 12 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 6: Number of dies per wafer (theoretical)
$$N_{\text{dies}} = \frac{A_{\text{net}}}{A_{\text{die}}} = \frac{16136.016}{12} = 1344.67$$

Since the number of complete dies must be an integer, we take:

$$N_{\text{dies}} = 1344 \text{ dies/wafer}$$

### Step 7: Account for defect density (optional follow-up)
If the defect density is $D_0 = 0.5 \text{ defects/cm}^2$ and the die area is $0.12 \text{ cm}^2$, the die yield is given by:

$$Y = e^{-D_0 \cdot A_{\text{die}}}$$

Substituting:

$$Y = e^{-0.5 \times 0.12} = e^{-0.06} = 0.9418$$

So the **good die count** is:

$$N_{\text{good}} = N_{\text{dies}} \times Y = 1344 \times 0.9418 = 1265.8 \approx 1266 \text{ good dies}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer:** Approximately **1266 functional MEMS pressure sensors** can be produced from a single 150 mm wafer, demonstrating the cost advantage of wafer-level batch fabrication.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Micro Manufacturing Process

The following decision matrix helps choose the appropriate micro manufacturing process for a given application — a common KTU design question.

| Process | Material | Min. Feature | Aspect Ratio | Cost | Typical Application |
|---------|----------|--------------|--------------|------|---------------------|
| **Bulk Micromachining (KOH/EDP)** | Si (100), (110) | ~1 µm | 1:1 to 20:1 | Low | Pressure sensors, accelerometers |
| **Surface Micromachining** | Polysilicon, SiO₂, Si₃N₄ | ~0.5 µm | 1:1 to 5:1 | Medium | Gyroscopes, RF MEMS switches |
| **DRIE (Deep RIE)** | Si | ~0.1 µm | 1:100 | High | Comb drives, inkjet heads |
| **LIGA** | Metals, plastics | ~0.5 µm | 1:100+ | Very High | Micro gears, optical components |
| **Micro-EDM** | Conductive metals | ~5 µm | 1:10 | High | Micro holes, molds |
| **Laser Ablation** | Any | ~10 µm | 1:5 | Medium | Polymer microfluidics |
| **Soft Lithography** | PDMS, polymers | ~1 µm | 1:10 | Low | Bio-MEMS, lab-on-chip |
| **3D Micro-printing** | Photopolymers, metals | ~5 µm | 1:20 | Medium | Prototyping, scaffolds |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Etch Rate Ratio and Selectivity

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Derivation Standard:** Show every algebraic step.

Consider a wet chemical etch where an etchant attacks a target material at rate $R_t$ and the mask (or underlying layer) at rate $R_m$. The **selectivity** $S$ is defined as:

$$S = \frac{R_t}{R_m}$$

### Step 1: Time to etch target material of thickness $d_t$
The time required to etch through the target is:

$$t_t = \frac{d_t}{R_t}$$

### Step 2: Amount of mask consumed during this time
The mask thickness removed is:

$$\Delta d_m = R_m \times t_t = R_m \cdot \frac{d_t}{R_t} = \frac{d_t}{S}$$

### Step 3: Minimum mask thickness required
For a robust process, the mask must retain at least 10% of its original thickness $d_{m,0}$ after the etch. So:

$$\Delta d_m \leq 0.9 \cdot d_{m,0}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{d_t}{S} \leq 0.9 \cdot d_{m,0}$$

Solving for minimum selectivity:

$$S_{\text{min}} = \frac{d_t}{0.9 \cdot d_{m,0}}$$

### Step 4: Worked Example
If $d_t = 400 \text{ µm}$ (silicon wafer thickness) and the SiO₂ mask is $d_{m,0} = 1 \text{ µm}$:

$$S_{\text{min}} = \frac{400}{0.9 \times 1} = 444.4$$

So the etchant must have selectivity **> 444** for the mask to survive — a key reason **KOH etchant is favored** for silicon, since its Si:SiO₂ selectivity is **~400:1** to **1000:1** depending on concentration and temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Micro Manufacturing Process Family

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the hierarchy of micro manufacturing processes as defined in the KTU Module 4 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Micro Manufacturing]:::root --> B[Silicon Based]:::branch
    A --> C[Non Silicon]:::branch
    A --> D[Hybrid Additive]:::branch

    B --> E[Bulk Micromachining]:::sub
    B --> F[Surface Micromachining]:::sub
    B --> G[DRIE Deep RIE]:::sub
    B --> H[Wafer Bonding]:::sub

    E --> E1[Anisotropic Wet KOH EDP]:::leaf
    E --> E2[Isotropic Wet HNA]:::leaf
    E --> E3[DRIE Bosch Process]:::leaf

    F --> F1[Sacrificial Layer Etching]:::leaf
    F --> F2[Poly Si Structural Layer]:::leaf
    F --> F3[Release Process HF]:::leaf

    C --> I[LIGA Process]:::sub
    C --> J[Micro EDM]:::sub
    C --> K[Laser Ablation]:::sub
    C --> L[Micro Injection Molding]:::sub

    I --> I1[X Ray Lithography]:::leaf
    I --> I2[Electroplating Ni Cu Au]:::leaf
    I --> I3[Plastic Molding]:::leaf

    D --> M[Stereolithography]:::sub
    D --> N[Soft Lithography PDMS]:::sub
    D --> O[Two Photon Polymerization]:::sub

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef branch fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sub fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000000,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Generic MEMS Fabrication Workflow

> [!TIP]
> The Mermaid block diagram below illustrates the canonical **top-down** MEMS fabrication sequence — a process flow that students should be able to draw in KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Substrate Cleaning]:::step --> S2[Step 2: Thermal Oxidation]:::step
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Photoresist Coating]:::step
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Photolithography UV Exposure]:::step
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Development Pattern Transfer]:::step
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Etching Wet or Dry]:::step
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Photoresist Stripping]:::step
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Thin Film Deposition]:::step
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Doping or Metallization]:::step
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Dicing and Packaging]:::step
    S10 --> S11[Final MEMS Device]:::final

    classDef step fill:#d9e1f2,color:#000000,stroke:#2f5496,stroke-width:1px
    classDef final fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Scaling Effects Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Physical Quantity at Macro Scale]:::root --> S1[Linear Dimension L]:::q
    P --> S2[Area L squared]:::q
    P --> S3[Volume L cubed]:::q
    P --> S4[Surface to Volume Ratio 1 over L]:::q
    P --> S5[Mass proportional to V]:::q
    P --> S6[Natural Frequency increases]:::q
    P --> S7[Electrostatic F over m improves]:::q

    S1 --> R1[Shrinks as 1 over N]:::res
    S2 --> R2[Shrinks as 1 over N squared]:::res
    S3 --> R3[Shrinks as 1 over N cubed]:::res
    S4 --> R4[Grows as N]:::res
    S5 --> R5[Shrinks as 1 over N cubed]:::res
    S6 --> R6[Grows as N to the 3 over 2]:::res
    S7 --> R7[Grows as N]:::res

    classDef root fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef q fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000,stroke:#bf8f00,stroke-width:1px
    classDef res fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:1px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Define micro manufacturing. List any two differences between conventional and micro manufacturing.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Micro manufacturing is the fabrication of components and devices whose critical dimensions are in the micrometer range (1 µm – 1000 µm), typically using lithography, etching, deposition, and bonding techniques adapted from the semiconductor industry.

**Two Differences:**

| S.No. | Conventional Manufacturing | Micro Manufacturing |
|-------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Feature size: mm to meters; tolerance ±10 µm or larger | Feature size: µm; tolerance as low as ±0.01 µm |
| 2 | Mechanical energy-based processes (cutting, forging) | Chemical, optical, ion-based processes (etching, lithography) |

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for each difference = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Mention any three driving forces that necessitate micro manufacturing in modern engineering.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Miniaturization of products** — demand for portable, implantable, and handheld devices.
2. **Enhanced performance** — micro-scale devices exhibit faster response (thermal time constant $\tau \propto L^2$), higher resonance frequencies ($\omega_n \propto N^{3/2}$), and improved force-to-mass ratios.
3. **Cost reduction through batch fabrication** — wafer-level parallel processing produces thousands of devices per batch, drastically reducing per-unit cost.

*(1 mark per point = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B questions carry 14 marks each and offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Compare conventional and micro manufacturing with respect to feature size, tolerance, process energy, production volume, and cost driver. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the generic steps involved in MEMS fabrication using surface micromachining. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Conventional Manufacturing | Micro Manufacturing |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Feature Size** | mm to meters | 1 µm – 1000 µm |
| **Tolerance** | ±10 µm to ±100 µm | ±0.01 µm to ±1 µm |
| **Process Energy** | Mechanical (cutting, forming) | Chemical, optical, ion, thermal |
| **Production Volume** | Low to medium | Ultra-high (wafer-level batches) |
| **Cost Driver** | Labor and material | Capital equipment and cleanroom infrastructure |
| **Material** | Wide variety (metals, plastics) | Mostly Si, glass, polymers; some metals |
| **Tooling** | Macro tools (cutters, dies) | Masks, photoresist, etchants |

*(1 mark per major row, total 7 marks)*

**(b) Surface Micromachining Block Diagram (7 Marks):**

The canonical surface micromachining process flow consists of these steps:

1. **Substrate Preparation** — Start with a clean silicon wafer (1 mark).
2. **Sacrificial Layer Deposition** — Deposit a thin film of SiO₂ (typically 0.5–2 µm) using LPCVD or thermal oxidation (1 mark).
3. **Photolithography (Patterning Sacrificial Layer)** — Coat with photoresist, expose through mask, develop to create anchor openings (1 mark).
4. **Etch Sacrificial Layer** — Use BHF (Buffered HF) to open anchor holes down to the substrate (1 mark).
5. **Structural Layer Deposition** — Deposit polysilicon (the structural material) over the patterned sacrificial layer using LPCVD (1 mark).
6. **Photolithography and Patterning of Structural Layer** — Define the shape of the mechanical element (1 mark).
7. **Release Etch** — Immerse in HF to dissolve the sacrificial SiO₂, freeing the polysilicon structure to move. **Critical step — stiction is a risk** (1 mark).

**Block Diagram:**

```
Substrate → Sacrificial Layer (SiO₂) → Anchor Patterning →
Structural Layer (Polysilicon) → Structural Patterning →
Release Etch (HF) → Free-Standing MEMS Structure
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Explain with a block diagram, the LIGA process. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 200 mm diameter silicon wafer is used to fabricate MEMS accelerometers, each die occupying an area of $2.5 \text{ mm} \times 3 \text{ mm}$. The edge exclusion is 2 mm. If the defect density is 0.3 defects/cm², calculate the number of good dies per wafer. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) LIGA Process (7 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **LIGA** = **Li**thographie + **G**alvanoformung (Electroforming) + **A**bformung (Molding). It is a German acronym.

**Block Diagram / Process Steps:**

```
Step 1: Thick X-Ray Resist Coating (PMMA, ~100–1000 µm thick)
        ↓
Step 2: X-Ray Exposure through Synchrotron (Mask aligner)
        ↓
Step 3: Development of Exposed Resist
        ↓
Step 4: Electroforming (Electro-plating of Ni, Cu, or Au)
        ↓
Step 5: Resist Stripping
        ↓
Step 6: Injection Molding or Hot Embossing (using the metal mold)
        ↓
Step 7: Final Micro Part
```

**Advantages (3 marks):**
- **Very high aspect ratio** (up to 100:1 or higher) — unrivaled by other techniques.
- **Excellent sidewall verticality** (≈ 90° ± 0.5°).
- **Wide material compatibility** — metals, plastics, ceramics.
- **Sub-micron resolution** with smooth sidewalls.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- **Requires synchrotron radiation** (extremely expensive).
- **Long exposure times** (hours per wafer).
- **Limited to 2.5D structures** (no true 3D complexity in standard LIGA).
- **High capital and operating cost**.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Wafer diameter $D = 200$ mm
- Die area $= 2.5 \text{ mm} \times 3 \text{ mm} = 7.5 \text{ mm}^2$
- Edge exclusion $e = 2$ mm
- Defect density $D_0 = 0.3 \text{ defects/cm}^2$

**Step 1: Usable diameter**
$$D_{\text{usable}} = 200 - 2(2) = 196 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Usable wafer area**
$$A_{\text{usable}} = \frac{\pi D_{\text{usable}}^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (196)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 38416}{4} = \pi \times 9604$$

$$A_{\text{usable}} = 30171.86 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Theoretical die count**
$$N_{\text{dies}} = \frac{A_{\text{usable}}}{A_{\text{die}}} = \frac{30171.86}{7.5} = 4022.9 \approx 4022 \text{ dies/wafer}$$

**Step 4: Convert die area to cm²**
$$A_{\text{die}} = 7.5 \text{ mm}^2 = 7.5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}^2 = 0.075 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 5: Die yield using Poisson model**
$$Y = e^{-D_0 \cdot A_{\text{die}}} = e^{-0.3 \times 0.075} = e^{-0.0225}$$

Computing the exponential:

$$Y = 0.9778$$

**Step 6: Number of good dies**
$$N_{\text{good}} = N_{\text{dies}} \times Y = 4022 \times 0.9778 = 3932.7 \approx 3933 \text{ good dies}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer: ≈ 3933 good MEMS accelerometers per 200 mm wafer.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Bulk vs. Surface Micromachining:** Students often state "surface micromachining etches the silicon substrate" — *wrong*. Surface micromachining deposits and releases thin films; the substrate is *not* etched in bulk.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract edge exclusion in wafer calculations:** Always apply $D_{\text{usable}} = D - 2e$ before computing die counts.
> 3. **Mixing up scaling laws:** The natural frequency scales as $N^{3/2}$ (not $N^2$ or $N^3$). The electrostatic force scales as $1/N^2$, but the force-to-mass ratio scales as $N$.
> 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers:** KTU examiners deduct up to 0.5 marks for missing units in 7-mark numerical problems.
> 5. **Forgetting the release-etch step:** In surface micromachining, the HF release etch and *stiction* prevention (critical-point drying, freeze-drying) are routinely tested.
> 6. **Stating LIGA is a "lithography-only" process:** LIGA = lithography + electroplating + molding. All three stages must be mentioned for full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Overview of Micro Manufacturing**

- **Micro manufacturing** = fabrication of features in the **1 µm – 1000 µm** range.
- **Three process families:** Silicon-based, Non-silicon (LIGA, EDM, laser), and Hybrid/Additive (3D printing, soft lithography).
- **Conventional vs Micro:** Macro processes use mechanical energy + macroscale tools; micro processes use chemical/ion energy + masks/photoresist.
- **Wafer-level batch processing** is the key cost advantage — hundreds to thousands of devices per wafer.
- **Scaling laws to memorize:**
  - Area $\propto 1/N^2$, Volume $\propto 1/N^3$, Mass $\propto 1/N^3$.
  - $S/V$ ratio $\propto N$ — surface effects dominate.
  - Natural frequency $\omega_n \propto N^{3/2}$ (uniform scaling).
  - Electrostatic force $F_e \propto 1/N^2$, but $F_e/m \propto N$ — favors MEMS.
- **Bulk micromachining** = substrate is etched (KOH, EDP, DRIE); used for diaphragms, microchannels.
- **Surface micromachining** = thin-film deposition + sacrificial release; used for polysilicon cantilevers, comb drives.
- **LIGA** = Lithography (X-ray) + Electroplating + Molding; offers extreme aspect ratio (100:1+) and vertical sidewalls.
- **Selectivity** $S = R_t / R_m$ must be sufficiently high so the etch mask survives — KOH has Si:SiO₂ selectivity ~400:1.
- **Wafer yield calculation:** $N_{\text{good}} = (A_{\text{usable}} / A_{\text{die}}) \times e^{-D_0 \cdot A_{\text{die}}}$.
- **Standard wafer sizes:** **100 mm (4″), 150 mm (6″), 200 mm (8″), 300 mm (12″)**.
- **Driving forces:** Miniaturization, performance enhancement, batch-fabrication cost reduction, multifunctional integration.
- **MEMS fabrication workflow:** Cleaning → Oxidation → Lithography → Etching → Stripping → Deposition → Doping → Dicing → Packaging.
- **Laminar flow regime at micro-scale:** Reynolds number $R_e \propto 1/N$ — important for microfluidics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Exam Punchline:**
> *Micro manufacturing trades mechanical energy for chemical/ion energy, macroscale tools for lithographic masks, and serial production for wafer-level batch fabrication — all driven by the unique physics that emerges at the micrometer scale.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk Micro Manufacturing, Surface Micro Machining, LIGA Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT742 - INTRODUCTION TO MEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bulk Micro Manufacturing, Surface Micro Machining, LIGA Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bulk Micromachining (Bulk Micro Manufacturing)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Bulk Micromachining):**
> Bulk Micromachining is a micro-fabrication technique in which three-dimensional microstructures are created by the **selective removal of significant portions of the silicon substrate (bulk material)** using chemical wet etching, dry etching, or a combination of both, to form mechanical structures such as membranes, beams, cantilevers, and cavities.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Carving a Statue from Marble" Approach
Imagine you have a solid block of marble (the silicon wafer). To create a sculpture (the MEMS device), a sculptor (the etchant) selectively removes unwanted portions of the marble using specialized tools (mask patterns), leaving behind the desired 3D shape. The material is *carved away* from the substrate itself — you are essentially **subtracting** material. This is exactly how bulk micromachining works at the micro-scale.

### Key Characteristics
- **Substrate:** Single-crystal silicon wafer (typically (100) or (110) oriented)
- **Material Removed:** Bulk Si from the wafer itself
- **Feature Depths:** Typically **1 µm to 500 µm**
- **Aspect Ratios:** Generally low-to-moderate (limited by crystallographic etch behavior)
- **Mask Materials:** SiO₂, Si₃N₄, heavily doped boron regions, metals
- **Compatible With:** CMOS post-processing

### Common Bulk Etchants (Standard KTU Table)
| Etchant | Type | Selectivity | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **KOH (Potassium Hydroxide)** | Anisotropic wet | Si : SiO₂ ≈ 400:1 | Standard MEMS |
| **TMAH (Tetra-Methyl Ammonium Hydroxide)** | Anisotropic wet | CMOS compatible | IC-MEMS integration |
| **EDP (Ethylene Diamine Pyrocatechol)** | Anisotropic wet | High selectivity | Research-grade |
| **HNA (HF + HNO₃ + Acetic acid)** | Isotropic wet | No crystal dependence | Rounded structures |
| **DRIE (Deep Reactive Ion Etching)** | Dry (Bosch/ICP) | Vertical sidewalls | High aspect ratio |

---

## 1.2 Surface Micromachining

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Surface Micromachining):**
> Surface Micromachining is a micro-fabrication process in which micro-mechanical structures are **built up additively on top of the substrate** by alternating thin-film depositions of structural and sacrificial (sacrificial spacer) layers, followed by selective removal of the sacrificial layers to release the suspended mechanical elements.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Layer-Cake Construction" Approach
Think of building a multi-story house (the MEMS structure) on a flat plot of land (the silicon wafer). You pour the foundation (sacrificial layer), build the walls and floors (structural layer), pour another floor (sacrificial again), build the roof (another structural layer), and finally — at the very end — you dissolve away all the temporary support layers (sacrificial removal), leaving the house standing freely with hollow spaces (air gaps) where the supports used to be. Surface micromachining is this exact "add-then-release" philosophy.

### Key Characteristics
- **Structural Material:** Polysilicon (most common), SiN, metals
- **Sacrificial Material:** SiO₂ (most common), PSG (Phospho-Silicate Glass)
- **Layer Count:** Typically 2–5 polysilicon layers in commercial processes
- **Feature Depths:** Limited to thin-film thickness (**1 µm to 25 µm**)
- **Critical Issue:** **Stiction** (sticking of released structures to substrate)

### The Three-Step Process Cycle
1. **Deposit Sacrificial Layer** → patterned with anchor openings
2. **Deposit & Pattern Structural Layer** → sits on anchors and sacrificial
3. **Release Etch (typically HF)** → removes sacrificial → frees structure

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Surface Micromachining Matters in KTU Examinations:**
> The sacrificial-layer concept is the single most-tested topic in this module. Examiners frequently ask students to draw cross-sections showing the chronological deposition order and the final released state.

---

## 1.3 LIGA Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (LIGA Process):**
> LIGA is a German acronym derived from **LI**thographie (lithography), **GA**lvanoformung (electroplating/electroforming), and **A**bformung (molding). It is a microfabrication technique used to produce **high-aspect-ratio microstructures** by combining deep X-ray lithography, electroplating of metals into the developed resist mold, and optional plastic molding for mass replication.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Lost-Wax Casting at Micro-Scale" Approach
Consider ancient goldsmithing: a craftsman carves a detailed wax model, encases it in clay, melts out the wax (leaving a cavity), and pours molten gold into the cavity. Once cooled, the clay is broken, revealing a perfect gold replica. LIGA works similarly: a polymer resist (PMMA) acts as the "wax mold," X-rays carve it precisely, and metal is electrodeposited into the void. The original resist is then dissolved, leaving a pure metal microstructure.

### Step-by-Step Concept
1. **Deep X-ray Lithography:** Synchrotron radiation exposes PMMA resist through a mask
2. **Development:** Exposed PMMA is dissolved, leaving deep cavities (mold)
3. **Electroplating:** Metal (Ni, Cu, Au) is electro-deposited into the mold cavities
4. **Strip Resist:** PMMA is removed, leaving free-standing metal microstructures
5. **Optional Molding:** The metal mold is used as a master to replicate plastic parts

### Key Characteristics
- **Resist:** PMMA (Polymethyl methacrylate) — thick layers (100s of µm)
- **Radiation Source:** Synchrotron (X-ray wavelengths **0.2–0.5 nm**)
- **Structural Heights:** Up to **1000 µm (1 mm)**
- **Lateral Resolution:** Sub-micron (down to **~0.5 µm**)
- **Aspect Ratios:** Extremely high (**>100:1** possible)
- **Sidewalls:** Nearly vertical, optically smooth

---

## 1.4 Comparative Overview at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> The following three-process comparison is a **high-yield KTU question** pattern. Memorize the distinctions.

| Parameter | Bulk Micromachining | Surface Micromachining | LIGA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Material removed/added** | Removed (subtractive) | Added (additive + release) | Added (electroformed) |
| **Feature depth** | 1–500 µm | 1–25 µm | Up to 1000 µm |
| **Aspect ratio** | Low–Moderate (< 50:1) | Low (< 20:1) | Very High (> 100:1) |
| **Sidewall profile** | Angled (54.74° for (100) KOH) | Vertical | Vertical, smooth |
| **IC compatibility** | Moderate | Excellent | Poor |
| **Cost** | Low | Low–Medium | Very High |
| **Equipment** | Wet benches, DRIE | Standard thin-film tools | Synchrotron facility |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bulk Micromachining — Deep Theory

### 2.1.1 Anisotropic Wet Etching Fundamentals
Anisotropic etching exploits the **orientation-dependent etch rate** of silicon in alkaline solutions. The etch rate varies with crystal plane due to differences in atomic packing density and the number of available back-bonds per surface atom.

**Crystal Plane Atomic Density Order:**
$$\text{(111)} > \text{(110)} > \text{(100)}$$

**Back-bonds per surface atom:**
- **(100) plane:** 2 back-bonds
- **(110) plane:** 1 back-bond
- **(111) plane:** 3 back-bonds (slowest etching)

For KOH etching of (100) silicon, the characteristic **sidewall angle** is:
$$\theta = 54.74°$$

This is the angle between the (100) surface and the (111) plane, which forms the etch-stop sidewall.

### 2.1.2 Underlying Chemical Reaction
The general reaction for Si in alkaline (KOH) etchant is:
$$\text{Si} + 2\text{OH}^- + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{SiO}_2(\text{OH})_2^{2-} + 2\text{H}_2\uparrow$$

Water acts as the oxidizer, and hydroxyl ions (OH⁻) drive the dissolution of the Si–O complex into the solution.

### 2.1.3 Selectivity and Etch Rate Parameters
> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU High-Yield Data for KOH Etching:**
> - Etch rate of (100) Si: **~1 µm/min** (at 30 wt% KOH, 80°C)
> - Etch rate of (111) Si: **~0.003 µm/min** (400× slower)
> - Selectivity over SiO₂: **~400:1**
> - Selectivity over Si₃N₄: **>1000:1**

### 2.1.4 Etch-Stop Techniques
- **Boron Etch-Stop:** Heavy p⁺ doping (≥ 5×10¹⁹ cm⁻³) virtually halts etching.
- **Electrochemical Etch-Stop:** A bias voltage applied across the wafer creates a passivating layer at p-n junctions.
- **Dielectric Etch-Stop:** Buried SiO₂ (in SOI wafers) acts as an automatic etch barrier.

---

## 2.2 Surface Micromachining — Deep Theory

### 2.2.1 The Sacrificial Layer Principle
The defining innovation of surface micromachining is the **sacrificial layer (spacer)**. The process exploits differential etch selectivity: the structural material must be **immune** to the release etchant, while the sacrificial material must be **highly susceptible** to it.

### 2.2.2 Process Flow Logic (4 Stages)
- **Stage 1 — Sacrificial Deposition & Patterning:** SiO₂ (often LPCVD or thermally grown) is deposited, and **anchor openings** are etched to expose the underlying substrate. The anchors are the *only points* the future structure will be attached to.
- **Stage 2 — Structural Layer Deposition:** Polysilicon is conformally deposited (typically LPCVD) over the patterned sacrificial layer. The polysilicon fills the anchor openings, forming **mechanical anchors**.
- **Stage 3 — Structural Layer Patterning:** Reactive Ion Etching (RIE) defines the lateral geometry of beams, plates, or comb fingers.
- **Stage 4 — Release Etch:** A timed **HF dip** (often with critical-point drying to prevent stiction) dissolves the SiO₂, freeing the structure.

### 2.2.3 Stiction — The Dominant Failure Mode
> [!WARNING]
> Stiction (static friction) occurs when capillary forces during liquid drying pull released structures into permanent contact with the substrate. Prevention techniques:
> - **Critical Point Drying (CPD)** — eliminates liquid-vapor interface
> - **Freeze Drying** — sublimates the rinse liquid
> - **HF Vapor Release** — never lets liquid touch the structure
> - **Anti-stiction coatings** (e.g., self-assembled monolayers of FDTS)

### 2.2.4 Material Property Considerations
Residual **stress gradients** in polysilicon films cause released structures to bend or curl. The stress gradient $\frac{d\sigma}{dz}$ is critical:
$$\text{Beam tip deflection } \delta \propto \frac{d\sigma}{dz} \cdot \frac{L^2}{E \cdot t^2}$$

where $L$ is beam length, $E$ is Young's modulus, and $t$ is thickness.

---

## 2.3 LIGA Process — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 Why X-rays Instead of UV?
Conventional UV lithography cannot penetrate thick resist layers due to high absorption. **Synchrotron X-rays** with wavelengths of **0.2–0.5 nm** offer:
- Extremely low absorption in PMMA
- Near-vertical penetration through hundreds of microns of resist
- High collimation (parallel beam) → vertical sidewalls
- Very short wavelength → sub-micron lateral resolution

### 2.3.2 The Three-Stage LIGA Flow

**Stage A — Deep X-ray Lithography:**
A thick PMMA layer (100–1000 µm) bonded to a conductive substrate (for later electroplating) is exposed through a gold/Si X-ray mask. The exposed regions undergo chain scission, becoming soluble in the developer (GG developer: 60% morpholine, 20% 2-aminoethanol, 5% DI water, 15% other).

**Stage B — Galvanoformung (Electroplating):**
The developed PMMA mold has a conductive bottom (the seed layer, typically Cu or Au). The wafer is immersed in an electroplating bath (e.g., Ni sulfamate). Metal ions are reduced and deposit into the mold cavities, growing from the bottom up. Current density and bath chemistry control the deposition rate and stress.

**Stage C — Abformung (Molding/Replication):**
After the PMMA is stripped, the metal microstructure serves as a **master insert** for hot embossing or injection molding of polymers (PMMA, POM, COC) for mass production at low cost.

### 2.3.3 Key Mathematical Relationships

**Synchrotron radiation penetration depth** in PMMA follows the Beer-Lambert law:
$$I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$

where $\mu$ is the linear absorption coefficient of PMMA, $x$ is the depth, and $I_0$ is the incident intensity. For $\lambda = 0.3$ nm, $\mu_{\text{PMMA}} \approx 1.5 \, \text{cm}^{-1}$.

**Electroplating growth rate** is governed by Faraday's law:
$$\text{Growth rate} = \frac{M \cdot J}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

where:
- $M$ = molar mass of deposited metal
- $J$ = current density (A/m²)
- $n$ = charge number (valence)
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $\rho$ = density of metal

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas for KTU Board Examination answers:**

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|
| Etch selectivity | $S = R_{\text{Si}} / R_{\text{mask}}$ | $R$ in µm/min | All wet etching |
| Anisotropic sidewall angle | $\theta = 54.74°$ | (100)/(111) Si in KOH | Bulk only |
| Beer-Lambert (X-ray) | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | $x$ in m, $\mu$ in m⁻¹ | LIGA |
| Electroplating rate (Faraday) | $r = M J / (n F \rho)$ | $r$ in m/s | LIGA / Surface |
| Polysilicon beam deflection | $\delta \propto (d\sigma/dz) L^2 / (E t^2)$ | $\sigma$ in Pa | Surface |
| DRIE aspect ratio limit | $AR_{\text{max}} \approx 30:1$ to $50:1$ | Bosch process | Bulk (dry) |
| LIGA aspect ratio | $AR_{\text{LIGA}} > 100:1$ | Heights 100–1000 µm | LIGA |
| Stiction prevention (CPD) | Eliminates $P_{\text{Laplace}} = 2\gamma \cos\theta / r$ | $\gamma$ surface tension | Surface release |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Bulk micromachined devices** dominate automotive pressure sensors, inkjet print heads, and accelerometers (e.g., ADXL series).
> - **Surface micromachined devices** are the workhorse of commercial MEMS (Analog Devices iMEMS, Texas Instruments DMD chip).
> - **LIGA-fabricated devices** are crucial in micro-optics, micro-fluidics, X-ray optics (e.g., collimators for space telescopes), and high-aspect-ratio connectors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Silicon Etch Rate Anisotropy

**Goal:** Derive the relationship between the etch rate along (100) and (111) planes and the resulting sidewall angle in KOH-etched silicon.

### Step 1 — Define the etch rate along each principal direction
Let:
- $R_{100}$ = etch rate along the (100) direction (vertical into substrate)
- $R_{111}$ = etch rate along the (111) direction (along the slanted sidewall)

For KOH at 30 wt%, 80°C:
$$R_{100} \approx 1.0 \, \mu\text{m/min}, \quad R_{111} \approx 0.003 \, \mu\text{m/min}$$

### Step 2 — Define the selectivity ratio
$$\text{Anisotropy factor} = \frac{R_{100}}{R_{111}} = \frac{1.0}{0.003} \approx 333$$

This high ratio is the **fundamental reason** anisotropic bulk micromachining is possible.

### Step 3 — Geometry of the etch profile
On a (100) wafer, the etch front moves vertically downward at rate $R_{100}$. The exposed (111) sidewall is inclined at angle $\theta$ from the wafer surface. The lateral component of the etch must equal the (111) etch rate:

$$R_{100} \cdot \tan\theta = R_{111}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $\theta$
$$\tan\theta = \frac{R_{111}}{R_{100}} \approx 0.003 \approx 0$$

This yields $\theta \approx 0°$ *only if* etch rates were truly identical. In reality, the limiting (111) plane is geometrically fixed by the crystal lattice.

### Step 5 — Apply crystallographic constraint
The (100) wafer surface and (111) sidewall planes intersect at a fixed angle determined by the **diamond cubic crystal structure** of silicon:
$$\cos\theta = \frac{h_1 h_2 + k_1 k_2 + l_1 l_2}{\sqrt{h_1^2 + k_1^2 + l_1^2} \cdot \sqrt{h_2^2 + k_2^2 + l_2^2}}$$

For (100) and (111):
$$\cos\theta = \frac{(1)(1) + (0)(1) + (0)(1)}{1 \cdot \sqrt{3}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

$$\theta = \arccos\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = 54.7356°$$

### Step 6 — Final result
$$\boxed{\theta_{(100)/(111)} = 54.74°}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Regardless of the etchant, any anisotropic etchant will produce a (111) sidewall at 54.74° on a (100) wafer — provided the (111) etch rate is *not zero*. This angle is the geometric signature of anisotropically etched silicon cavities.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Polysilicon Beam Deflection Under Stress Gradient

**Goal:** Determine the out-of-plane tip deflection of a released cantilever beam due to a residual stress gradient.

### Step 1 — Define the problem
A polysilicon cantilever of length $L$, thickness $t$, and width $w$ is released from the substrate. The film has a residual stress $\sigma(z)$ that varies linearly through the thickness:
$$\sigma(z) = \sigma_0 + \frac{d\sigma}{dz} \cdot z$$

### Step 2 — Bending moment integration
The moment per unit width caused by the stress distribution is:
$$M = \int_0^t \sigma(z) \cdot z \, dz$$

Assuming a linear stress gradient $\sigma(z) = \sigma_0 + G z$ where $G = d\sigma/dz$:
$$M = \int_0^t (\sigma_0 + Gz) z \, dz = \int_0^t (\sigma_0 z + G z^2) \, dz$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the integral
$$M = \sigma_0 \cdot \frac{t^2}{2} + G \cdot \frac{t^3}{3}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Euler-Bernoulli beam theory
The curvature $\kappa$ of the beam is:
$$\kappa = \frac{M}{E I}$$

For a beam of unit width, the moment of inertia is:
$$I = \frac{t^3}{12}$$

Therefore:
$$\kappa = \frac{12 M}{E t^3} = \frac{12}{E t^3} \left( \frac{\sigma_0 t^2}{2} + \frac{G t^3}{3} \right) = \frac{6 \sigma_0}{E t} + \frac{4 G}{E}$$

### Step 5 — Integrate curvature to find tip deflection
For small deflections of a cantilever:
$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{\kappa L^2}{2}$$

Substituting:
$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{L^2}{2} \left( \frac{6 \sigma_0}{E t} + \frac{4 G}{E} \right)$$

### Step 6 — For pure stress gradient (no mean stress $\sigma_0 = 0$)
$$\boxed{\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{2 G L^2}{E} = \frac{2 L^2}{E} \cdot \frac{d\sigma}{dz}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The deflection scales with the **square of length** — doubling the beam length quadruples the deflection. This is why stress-gradient control is critical in long-surface-micromachined comb fingers and cantilever mirrors.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — LIGA Electroplating Time

**Goal:** Calculate the time required to electroplate a nickel microstructure of height $h = 200 \, \mu\text{m}$ using a current density of $J = 5 \, \text{A/dm}^2$.

### Step 1 — Gather constants
- Molar mass of Ni: $M = 58.69 \, \text{g/mol}$
- Valence: $n = 2$
- Faraday constant: $F = 96{,}485 \, \text{C/mol}$
- Density of Ni: $\rho = 8.908 \, \text{g/cm}^3 = 8908 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- Current density: $J = 5 \, \text{A/dm}^2 = 500 \, \text{A/m}^2$
- Target height: $h = 200 \, \mu\text{m} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$

### Step 2 — Apply Faraday's law
The mass deposited per unit area is:
$$m = \frac{M J t}{n F}$$

The mass per unit area is also equal to the volume per unit area times density:
$$m = \rho \cdot h$$

### Step 3 — Equate the two expressions
$$\rho \cdot h = \frac{M J t}{n F}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for time $t$
$$t = \frac{n F \rho h}{M J}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute numerical values
$$t = \frac{(2)(96{,}485)(8908)(2 \times 10^{-4})}{(0.05869)(500)}$$

Numerator:
$$(2)(96{,}485) = 192{,}970$$
$$(192{,}970)(8908) = 1.7190 \times 10^9$$
$$(1.7190 \times 10^9)(2 \times 10^{-4}) = 343{,}804$$

Denominator:
$$(0.05869)(500) = 29.345$$

### Step 6 — Final calculation
$$t = \frac{343{,}804}{29.345} \approx 11{,}716 \, \text{seconds}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 11{,}716 \, \text{s} \approx 3.25 \, \text{hours}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** LIGA plating is **time-intensive**. The slow growth (compared to thin-film deposition) is why the mold is designed to plate multiple parts in parallel, and why molding/replication (Abformung) is essential for cost reduction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bulk Micromachining Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Prime (100) Si Wafer"] --> B["Thermal Oxidation Grow SiO2 Mask"]
    B --> C["Photolithography Pattern Mask"]
    C --> D["BOE Etch Open Windows in SiO2"]
    D --> E["Strip Photoresist"]
    E --> F["Anisotropic KOH Etch at 80C"]
    F --> G{"Etch Stop Reached?"}
    G -- "No" --> F
    G -- "Yes - Time" --> H["Remove Mask in HF"]
    G -- "Yes - p+ Stop" --> H
    G -- "Yes - SOI Buried Oxide" --> H
    H --> I["Final Bulk MEMS Structure Diaphragm or Cantilever"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Surface Micromachining Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Si Wafer with SiO2"] --> B["LPCVD Deposit Sacrificial SiO2 2um"]
    B --> C["Photolithography Pattern Anchors"]
    C --> D["RIE Etch Anchor Openings"]
    D --> E["Strip Resist"]
    E --> F["LPCVD Deposit Polysilicon 2um"]
    F --> G["Photolithography Pattern Structure"]
    G --> H["RIE Etch Polysilicon"]
    H --> I["Strip Resist"]
    I --> J["Release in HF 49 percent"]
    J --> K["Critical Point Drying to Prevent Stiction"]
    K --> L["Final Released MEMS Beam"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — LIGA Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Conductive Seed Layer on Substrate"] --> B["Bond PMMA Resist Sheet 100 to 1000 um"]
    B --> C["Synchrotron X-ray Exposure 0.2 to 0.5 nm"]
    C --> D["GG Developer Dissolves Exposed PMMA"]
    D --> E["Deep Cavity Mold Formed"]
    E --> F["Electroplating Ni or Cu into Mold"]
    F --> G["Strip PMMA in Solvent"]
    G --> H["Free Standing Metal Microstructure"]
    H --> I["Optional: Hot Embossing or Injection Molding for Mass Replication"]
    I --> J["Polymer Replica Micro Parts"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparative Selection Logic for Fabrication Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Design Requirement Analysis"] --> B{"Target Feature Depth?"}
    B -- "1 to 25 um" --> C["Surface Micromachining"]
    B -- "25 to 500 um" --> D{"High Aspect Ratio?"}
    B -- "Greater than 500 um" --> E["LIGA Process"]
    D -- "Yes greater than 20:1" --> E
    D -- "No less than 20:1" --> F["Bulk Micromachining"]
    C --> G["Low Cost IC Compatible"]
    F --> H["Mature Industry Standard"]
    E --> I["High Cost Synchrotron Required"]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anisotropic KOH Etch Profile Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Mask Opening on 100 Si Wafer"] --> B["Etch Front Propagates Vertically at R100"]
    B --> C["111 Sidewall Forms at 54.74 degrees"]
    C --> D["Final Cavity: Rectangular with Sloped Walls"]
    D --> E["Lateral Undercut equals h * tan 35.26 degrees"]
    D --> F["Vertical Depth equals h"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:**
> The 54.74° sidewall is the **signature visual feature** in any bulk micromachining schematic. In KTU answers, always draw the sidewall angle explicitly and label the (100) and (111) planes to score full marks on the diagram question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B, Q1a]**
**Define Bulk Micromachining. Mention any two etchants used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
